I_{\text{min}} = \log_2(21) \approx 4.39 \text{ bits}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the redundancy (degeneracy).**
The excess information capacity is:

$$
I_{\text{redundant}} = I_{\text{codon}} - I_{\text{min}} = 6 - 4.39 = 1.61 \text{ bits}
$$

This redundancy is what allows **degeneracy** — multiple codons map to the same amino acid, conferring **mutational robustness**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Computational Insight:** This redundancy is exploited in codon optimization algorithms for heterologous protein expression (e.g., designing synthetic genes for *E. coli*).

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Expected Length of an Open Reading Frame in a Random Sequence

When scanning a random nucleotide sequence in one reading frame, what is the expected ORF length before encountering a stop codon?

**Step 1 — Probability that any given codon is a stop.**
There are 3 stop codons (UAA, UAG, UGA) out of 64 total codons:

$$
P_{\text{stop}} = \frac{3}{64}
$$

**Step 2 — Expected number of codons before a stop.**
For a geometric distribution, the expected number of trials until the first success is:

$$
E[L] = \frac{1}{P_{\text{stop}}} = \frac{1}{3/64} = \frac{64}{3} \approx 21.33 \text{ codons}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to nucleotides (including the stop codon).**

$$
E[L_{\text{nt}}] = \left(\frac{64}{3} + 1\right) \times 3 = 67 \text{ nucleotides}
$$

**Step 4 — Significance in gene finding.**
A real ORF longer than ~150 nt (50 codons) in a random sequence is statistically very unlikely ($P < 10^{-10}$), which is the basis for minimum ORF length thresholds in gene prediction tools.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Gene Organization

| **Feature** | **Prokaryotic Gene** | **Eukaryotic Gene** |
|---|---|---|
| Introns | Absent | Present (split genes) |
| Chromatin structure | No nucleosomes | Nucleosome-based |
| RNA processing | Minimal | 5' cap, splicing, 3' poly-A tail |
| mRNA half-life | Short (~minutes) | Variable (minutes to hours) |
| Transcription / Translation coupling | Coupled (co-transcriptional) | Spatially separated (nucleus vs. cytoplasm) |
| Operons | Common (polycistronic) | Absent (monocistronic) |
| Regulation dominance | Transcriptional | Multiple layers (chromatin to post-translational) |
| Promoter structure | –10 / –35 boxes | TATA / Inr / DPE + distal enhancers |

---

### 3.4 Worked Analytical Example: Codon Usage Bias Computation

Compute the **Relative Synonymous Codon Usage (RSCU)** for Leucine in a hypothetical gene with the following counts:

| **Codon** | **Count (X_ij)** |
|---|---|
| UUA | 5 |
| UUG | 10 |
| CUU | 3 |
| CUC | 4 |
| CUA | 2 |
| CUG | 16 |

**Step 1 — Total Leucine codons observed.**

$$
\sum_j X_{ij} = 5 + 10 + 3 + 4 + 2 + 16 = 40
$$

**Step 2 — Number of synonymous codons (n_i) for Leucine.**

$$
n_i = 6
$$

**Step 3 — Average count per synonymous codon (expected under uniform usage).**

$$
\bar{X_i} = \frac{1}{n_i}\sum_j X_{ij} = \frac{40}{6} \approx 6.67
$$

**Step 4 — RSCU for each codon.**

$$
\text{RSCU}_{ij} = \frac{X_{ij}}{\bar{X_i}}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{RSCU(UUA)} &= 5 / 6.67 \approx 0.75 \\
\text{RSCU(UUG)} &= 10 / 6.67 \approx 1.50 \\
\text{RSCU(CUU)} &= 3 / 6.67 \approx 0.45 \\
\text{RSCU(CUC)} &= 4 / 6.67 \approx 0.60 \\
\text{RSCU(CUA)} &= 2 / 6.67 \approx 0.30 \\
\text{RSCU(CUG)} &= 16 / 6.67 \approx 2.40
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Interpretation:** CUG is the most over-represented (RSCU = 2.40) — this gene is *CUG-biased* for Leucine, suggesting high expression in a host where the matching tRNA is abundant.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 High-Level Architecture of a Eukaryotic Gene

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Upstream Regulatory Region] --> B[Distal Enhancers and Silencers]
    B --> C[Proximal Promoter with CAAT and GC box]
    C --> D[Core Promoter with TATA box and Inr]
    D --> E[5 Prime UTR with Kozak sequence]
    E --> F[Exon 1]
    F --> G[Intron 1]
    G --> H[Exon 2]
    H --> I[Intron 2]
    I --> J[Exon N]
    J --> K[3 Prime UTR]
    K --> L[Polyadenylation Signal AAUAAA]
    L --> M[Transcription Terminator]
    M --> N[Mature mRNA after Splicing and Capping]
    N --> O[Translation into Polypeptide]
```

> **Reading Guide:** Arrows represent the 5' → 3' directional flow of transcription. The mature mRNA (N) is the output of the **splicing step** that excises introns (G, I) and ligates exons (F, H, J).

---

### 4.2 The Lac Operon — Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph REGLAC["Regulatory Module"]
        R1[LacI Gene]
        R2[LacI Repressor Protein]
        R1 --> R2
    end

    subgraph OPERON["Lac Operon Structural Module"]
        P[Promoter P]
        O[Operator O]
        Z[lacZ encodes Beta galactosidase]
        Y[lacY encodes Permease]
        A[lacA encodes Transacetylase]
        T[Terminator]
        P --> O --> Z --> Y --> A --> T
    end

    subgraph EFFECTOR["Effector Molecules"]
        E1[Allolactose from Lactose]
        E2[cAMP CAP complex from Low Glucose]
    end

    R2 -- Binds and blocks transcription --> O
    E1 -- Inactivates repressor --> R2
    E2 -- Binds CAP and enhances --> P
    T --> OUT[Functional Lac proteins produced]
```

> **Operational Note:** Transcription proceeds only when (1) the repressor is inactivated by allolactose AND (2) the cAMP-CAP complex is bound to the promoter — a logical AND gate in molecular terms.

---

### 4.3 Central Dogma Information Flow with Control Layers

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    DNA[DNA Genome] --> T1[Chromatin Level Control]
    T1 --> T2[Transcriptional Control]
    T2 --> T3[RNA Processing Control]
    T3 --> T4[RNA Transport Control]
    T4 --> T5[Translational Control]
    T5 --> T6[Post Translational Control]
    T6 --> PROT[Functional Proteome]

    T2 -. Feedback via signaling .-> T2
    T6 -. Feedback to chromatin .-> T1
```

> **Annotation:** Dashed arrows represent **feedback loops** — the cell uses protein output to dynamically regulate upstream steps (e.g., transcription factor activation by phosphorylation cascades).

---

### 4.4 Split Gene → Mature mRNA — Splicing Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PRE[mRNA Precursor] --> SPL[spliceosome action]
    SPL --> EX[Joined Exons]
    SPL --> IN[Excised Introns as Lariat]
    EX --> CAP[5 Prime Cap added]
    EX --> POL[3 Prime Poly A tail added]
    CAP --> MAT[Mature mRNA]
    POL --> MAT
    MAT --> RIB[Export to Cytoplasm and Translation]
```

> **Engineering Analogy:** Splicing is analogous to a compiler's **preprocessor**, removing comment blocks (introns) and concatenating executable code (exons) before the program (mRNA) is shipped for execution (translation).

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define a gene. With a neat diagram, describe the structure of a eukaryotic gene.*

**Model Answer:**
A gene is a functional unit of heredity consisting of a DNA sequence that encodes a functional product (RNA and/or protein). A eukaryotic gene contains the following structural elements (in 5' → 3' order):

1. **Regulatory region:** Enhancers, silencers, and promoter elements (TATA box, CAAT box, GC box).
2. **5' UTR:** Contains the Kozak consensus sequence (in mammals) for ribosome recognition.
3. **Exons:** Coding sequences preserved in the mature mRNA.
4. **Introns:** Non-coding sequences spliced out during RNA processing.
5. **3' UTR:** Contains the polyadenylation signal (AAUAAA) and regulatory elements.
6. **Terminator:** Signals the end of transcription.

*Neat diagram (to be drawn in the exam):*

```
[Enhancer]──[Silencer]──[Promoter]──[5'UTR]──[Exon1]–[Intron1]–[Exon2]–[Intron2]–[Exon3]──[3'UTR]──[Terminator]
```

The **mature mRNA** contains only the exons plus UTRs, after capping, splicing, and polyadenylation.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Explain the operon model of gene regulation with reference to the lac operon.*

**Model Answer:**
The operon model, proposed by **Jacob and Monod (1961)**, describes a cluster of structural genes under the control of a single promoter and operator, enabling coordinated regulation. In the **lac operon**:

- **Regulatory gene (lacI):** Encodes the LacI repressor protein, constitutively expressed.
- **Operator (O):** Binding site for the repressor; when bound, transcription is blocked.
- **Three structural genes:** lacZ (β-galactosidase), lacY (permease), lacA (transacetylase).
- **Negative regulation:** In the absence of lactose, LacI binds the operator → transcription is OFF. When lactose is present, it is converted to **allolactose**, which binds and inactivates LacI → transcription is ON (induced).
- **Positive regulation (catabolite repression):** When glucose is low, intracellular cAMP rises and binds **CAP** (catabolite activator protein). The cAMP-CAP complex binds the promoter and *enhances* RNA polymerase recruitment. This is the **"AND gate"** logic: transcription is maximal when lactose is present AND glucose is absent.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]**

**(a)** *[7 Marks]* Describe the hierarchical levels of gene regulation in eukaryotes. Give one example for each level.

**(b)** *[7 Marks]* With a neat block diagram, explain the structure and control of the lac operon. Discuss both negative and positive regulation.

---

**Model Solution to (a):**

Gene expression in eukaryotes is regulated at **six major levels**:

| **Level** | **Mechanism** | **Example** |
|---|---|---|
| 1. Chromatin level | Histone acetylation (HATs) opens chromatin; deacetylation (HDACs) compacts it | X-chromosome inactivation by Xist lncRNA |
| 2. Transcriptional | Transcription factors (TFs) bind promoters/enhancers | p53 activates p21 gene transcription upon DNA damage |
| 3. RNA processing | Alternative splicing; RNA editing | Calcitonin / CGRP alternative splicing |
| 4. RNA transport | Nuclear export of mRNA via export factors | HIV Rev-mediated unspliced mRNA export |
| 5. Translational | miRNA binding to 3' UTR; eIF regulation | Lin-4 miRNA regulates lin-14 in *C. elegans* development |
| 6. Post-translational | Ubiquitination, phosphorylation, proteolysis | Cyclin B degradation by APC/C for cell cycle exit |

This multi-level architecture allows **fine-tuned, context-specific** gene expression — vastly more complex than the simple on/off switches of prokaryotes.

---

**Model Solution to (b):**

Refer to the **Mermaid block diagram in Section 4.2** for the structural layout.

**Negative Regulation:**
- The *lacI* gene produces the **LacI repressor**, a tetrameric protein.
- In the absence of lactose, LacI binds tightly to the **operator** sequence, physically blocking RNA polymerase from transcribing lacZYA.
- When lactose enters the cell, it is isomerized to **allolactose** by β-galactosidase (basal level).
- Allolactose binds LacI, causing a conformational change → repressor dissociates → transcription proceeds (**induction**).

**Positive Regulation:**
- When **glucose is scarce**, intracellular **cAMP** levels rise.
- cAMP binds to the **CAP (CRP) protein** → cAMP-CAP complex.
- This complex binds a site upstream of the lac promoter, **bending DNA** and facilitating RNA polymerase binding.
- Without cAMP-CAP, transcription proceeds at a low basal level even when the repressor is removed.

**Logic Summary:**

| **Glucose** | **Lactose** | **Lac Operon Expression** |
|---|---|---|
| High | Absent | OFF (repressor on, no cAMP-CAP) |
| High | Present | Low basal (repressor off, no cAMP-CAP) |
| Low | Absent | OFF (repressor on, but cAMP-CAP present) |
| Low | Present | **HIGH (repressor off AND cAMP-CAP on)** |

This is a **molecular Boolean AND-NOT gate**: active when [Lactose = TRUE] AND [Glucose = FALSE].

---

#### **Question B — [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**(a)** *[7 Marks]* With suitable examples, explain the concepts of exons, introns, and alternative splicing. Discuss the evolutionary and computational significance of introns.

**(b)** *[7 Marks]* Define the genetic code. List its salient properties. Compute the information content per codon and the expected ORF length in a random nucleotide sequence.

---

**Model Solution to (a):**

**Exons** are DNA sequences retained in the mature mRNA after splicing. **Introns** are intervening sequences excised during pre-mRNA processing.

**Example:** The human **troponin T gene** contains 18 exons. Through **alternative splicing**, different combinations produce cardiac, skeletal, and smooth muscle isoforms — increasing proteomic diversity without increasing gene count.

**Types of Alternative Splicing:**
1. **Exon skipping** (most common in metazoans)
2. **Mutually exclusive exons**
3. **Alternative 5' splice sites**
4. **Alternative 3' splice sites**
5. **Intron retention**

**Evolutionary Significance of Introns:**
- **Exon Shuffling Hypothesis (Gilbert, 1978):** Introns facilitated the evolution of new proteins by allowing exons (functional domains) to be recombined.
- **Group I/II Introns:** Some are self-splicing ribozymes, suggesting an ancient "RNA world" origin.
- **Alternative Splicing:** Dramatically expands proteome complexity — ~20,000 human genes encode >100,000 proteins.

**Computational Significance:**
- **Gene prediction tools** (GENSCAN, AUGUSTUS) must model intron length distributions and splice site motifs (GT-AG rule).
- **RNA-seq alignment** tools (TopHat, STAR) must handle junction-spanning reads.
- Introns present **alignment challenges** for short-read mappers.

---

**Model Solution to (b):**

**Definition:** The genetic code is the set of rules by which information encoded within nucleic acid sequences is translated into proteins by living cells.

**Salient Properties:**
1. **Triplet** — three nucleotides per codon.
2. **Unambiguous** — one codon specifies only one amino acid.
3. **Degenerate (redundant)** — 64 codons for 20 amino acids + stop.
4. **Nearly universal** — with minor mitochondrial variants.
5. **Non-overlapping and comma-free** — codons are read in fixed frame, no gaps.
6. **Start codon (AUG) and stop codons (UAA, UAG, UGA)** demarcate reading frames.

**Information Content per Codon:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{\text{codon}} &= \log_2(4^3) \\
&= \log_2(64) \\
&= 6 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Comparison with minimum information needed:**

$$
I_{\min} = \log_2(21) \approx 4.39 \text{ bits}
$$

**Redundancy:**

$$
I_{\text{redundancy}} = 6 - 4.39 \approx 1.61 \text{ bits}
$$

**Expected ORF Length in Random Sequence:**

$$
P_{\text{stop}} = \frac{3}{64}
$$

$$
E[L_{\text{codons}}] = \frac{1}{P_{\text{stop}}} = \frac{64}{3} \approx 21.33 \text{ codons}
$$

$$
E[L_{\text{nt}}] = \left(\frac{64}{3} + 1\right) \times 3 = 67 \text{ nucleotides}
$$

**Conclusion:** A true biological ORF of >150 nt is statistically significant and serves as a **first-pass filter in computational gene finding**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Confusing promoter with operator:** Promoter binds RNA polymerase; operator binds the repressor protein. Examiners specifically deduct for this mix-up.
> 2. **Missing the "AND-NOT" logic** of the lac operon: Many students describe only negative control and ignore catabolite repression (positive control), losing 2–3 marks.
> 3. **Forgetting units in numerical problems:** Always write "bits," "nucleotides," or "codons" — naked numbers lose marks.
> 4. **Drawing block diagrams without labels:** A diagram with unlabeled arrows is treated as decorative and earns zero.
> 5. **Splicing mechanism skipped:** Students often define introns but fail to mention the **GT-AG splice site rule** and the **lariat intermediate** — both are KTU-favorite sub-parts.
> 6. **Coding sequence vs. gene confusion:** CDS ≠ entire gene. A gene includes UTRs and introns; CDS is the translated portion only.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Gene definition:** Functional unit of heredity — a *union of genomic sequences* encoding potentially overlapping functional products (Sequence Ontology).
- **Eukaryotic gene structure:** Enhancers → Promoter (TATA, CAAT, GC) → 5' UTR → Exon – Intron – Exon ... → 3' UTR → Poly-A signal → Terminator.
- **Prokaryotic gene structure:** –35 box → –10 (Pribnow) box → 5' UTR (Shine-Dalgarno) → Continuous CDS → Terminator (hairpin).
- **Central Dogma:** DNA → RNA → Protein (Crick, 1958). Reverse transcription and self-splicing introns are notable exceptions.
- **Six layers of eukaryotic gene control:** Chromatin → Transcriptional → RNA processing → RNA transport → Translational → Post-translational.
- **Lac Operon Logic:** "ON" only when **Lactose = TRUE AND Glucose = FALSE** (Boolean AND-NOT).
- **Genetic Code Properties:** Triplet, unambiguous, degenerate, nearly universal, non-overlapping, comma-free.
- **Key Formulas (must memorize):**
  - $H_{\max} = 2$ bits/symbol (DNA)
  - $I_{\text{codon}} = 6$ bits
  - $E[L_{\text{ORF}}] = 64/3$ codons ≈ 21.3 codons
  - $\text{GC\%} = \frac{G+C}{A+T+G+C} \times 100$
  - $\text{RSCU}_{ij} = \frac{X_{ij}}{\frac{1}{n_i}\sum_j X_{ij}}$
- **Computational Biology Perspective:** Gene structure underlies every major bioinformatics problem — gene prediction, sequence alignment, expression analysis, and synthetic gene design.
- **Exam Favorites:** Alternative splicing examples (troponin T, Dscam, Drosophila), p53 as TF, lac operon diagram, codon table recitation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Transcription
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT526 - COMPUTATIONAL BIOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Transcription

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Transcription** is the biochemically and computationally defined process by which the genetic information encoded in a segment of **DNA (Deoxyribonucleic Acid)** is enzymatically copied into a complementary **RNA (Ribonucleic Acid)** molecule by the enzyme **RNA Polymerase (RNAP)**. In the context of **Computational Biology (PEADT526)**, transcription is modeled as a high-fidelity string-to-string mapping problem, where nucleotide sequence patterns in non-coding regulatory regions (promoters, enhancers, CpG islands) determine the precise coordinates, orientation, and likelihood of transcriptional initiation — the *Transcription Start Site (TSS)*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Snapshot:** Transcription = DNA → RNA (specifically, mRNA, tRNA, rRNA, or other non-coding RNAs). Computational Biology treats it as a **sequence-pattern recognition** and **signal detection** problem on genomic strings over the alphabet {A, C, G, T}.

Mathematically, given a DNA template strand $T$ of length $L$, the RNA transcript $R$ is a sequence of length $N \le L$ such that:

$$R[i] = \text{complement}(T[i]) \quad \text{for } i = 1, 2, \dots, N$$

with the exception that **Thymine (T)** in DNA is replaced by **Uracil (U)** in RNA.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The Office-Copy Machine Analogy:**
> Imagine a thick, irreplaceable master file (the **DNA**) locked in a vault. Nobody is allowed to take the original out. Instead, an office worker (the **RNA Polymerase**) walks to the vault, opens it carefully at a marked page (the **Promoter**), and uses a copy machine to produce a working duplicate (the **RNA transcript**). The master file stays safe, while the copy is taken to the workshop (the **Ribosome**) to build the product (**Protein**).

For computational biology students, here is a second analogy:

- Think of the genome as a 3-billion-character text file.
- Transcription is a **"find-and-print"** subroutine: the program scans the file looking for a known "start signal" (the promoter motif), then outputs a copy of the subsequent text until it finds a "stop signal" (terminator).
- A **bioinformatician's job** is to write the *signal detector* — to predict *where* the copier will start and stop, using only the raw text of the genome.

---

## 1.3 Core Biological Actors and Their Roles

| Molecule | Role in Transcription | KTU High-Yield Symbol |
|----------|----------------------|----------------------|
| **DNA Template Strand** | Anti-sense strand; physically read 3'→5' | $T$ |
| **DNA Coding (Sense) Strand** | Has same sequence as RNA (with T→U) | $C$ |
| **RNA Polymerase** | Catalyzes RNA synthesis | **RNAP** |
| **Promoter** | DNA signal that recruits RNAP | — |
| **Transcription Start Site** | First nucleotide transcribed (position +1) | **TSS** |
| **Terminator** | DNA signal that halts RNAP | — |
| **Sigma Factor (σ)** | Prokaryotic RNAP subunit that recognizes promoter | $\sigma^{70}$ |
| **Transcription Factors (TFs)** | Eukaryotic proteins aiding RNAP II binding | **TFIID, TFIIA, TFIIB…** |
| **5' UTR / 3' UTR** | Untranslated regions flanking the coding sequence | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metrics to Memorize:**
> - **TATA Box (Goldberg–Hogness box):** consensus **TATAAA**, located approximately **-25 to -30 bp** upstream of TSS.
> - **CAAT Box:** consensus **GGCCAATCT**, located around **-75 to -80 bp**.
> - **GC Box:** consensus **GGGCGG**, common in housekeeping gene promoters.
> - **Prokaryotic -10 (Pribnow box):** **TATAAT**.
> - **Prokaryotic -35 box:** **TTGACA**.
> - **Transcription rate (E. coli):** roughly **40–80 nucleotides/second** at 37 °C.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Phases of Transcription (Mechanistic Decomposition)

Transcription is a tightly regulated biochemical pipeline that can be decomposed into three discrete operational stages. From a computational standpoint, each stage corresponds to a different sequence-pattern class detectable in silico.

### Phase 1 — Initiation (Promoter Recognition + Open Complex Formation)

1. **RNAP Holoenzyme Assembly** (prokaryotes): The core enzyme $\alpha_2\beta\beta'\omega$ binds the **sigma factor** $\sigma$ to form the **holoenzyme**.
2. **Promoter Search:** The holoenzyme performs **1-D sliding and 3-D hopping** along DNA, recognizing the **-35 and -10 consensus elements**.
3. **Closed Complex → Open Complex:** The DNA locally melts (denatures) over ~12–14 bp around the -10 region, exposing the template strand.
4. **Initial RNA synthesis** (abortive cycling): Short 2–9 nt RNA fragments are synthesized and released before productive elongation.
5. **Promoter clearance:** σ factor is released, and the enzyme transitions into the elongation complex.

> [!NOTE]
> **Eukaryotic Initiation Complexity (KTU High-Yield):** Eukaryotes use **RNA Polymerase II** plus general transcription factors in a strict ordered assembly: **TFIID** (with TBP) → **TFIIA** → **TFIIB** → **TFIIF + RNAP II** → **TFIIE** → **TFIIH** (helicase activity melts DNA). The **Mediator complex** then bridges to enhancer-bound activators.

### Phase 2 — Elongation (Processive RNA Synthesis)

- The **transcription bubble** (~17 nt melted) moves with RNAP.
- New ribonucleotides (NTPs) are added to the 3'-OH of the growing RNA chain.
- **Proofreading:** RNAP has intrinsic 3'→5' exonuclease activity (backtracking) to excise misincorporated bases; error rate ≈ **10⁻⁵** per nucleotide.

### Phase 3 — Termination

| Organism | Termination Type | Signal |
|----------|------------------|--------|
| Prokaryote | **Rho-independent (ρ-independent)** | GC-rich **hairpin** + U-rich tract |
| Prokaryote | **Rho-dependent (ρ-factor)** | Rut site (ρ-utilization), no hairpin |
| Eukaryote | **Polyadenylation-coupled** | AAUAAA signal + downstream GU-rich element |

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Identity | Meaning | Units / Domain |
|---|-------------------|---------|----------------|
| 1 | $R[i] = \text{complement}(T[i])$ with $T \to U$ | Template → RNA mapping | Index $i$ over transcript |
| 2 | $\text{GC\%} = \dfrac{n_G + n_C}{L} \times 100$ | GC content (used to predict CpG islands & isochores) | Percent |
| 3 | $\text{Obs/Exp}_{CpG} = \dfrac{p_{CG}}{p_C \cdot p_G}$ | CpG island detection statistic | Ratio ≥ 0.6 ⇒ island |
| 4 | $\log \text{Likelihood}(s) = \sum_{j=1}^{W} \log \dfrac{P(s_j \mid \text{motif})}{P(s_j \mid \text{background})}$ | Position Weight Matrix (PWM) score | Real-valued log score |
| 5 | $P(\text{gene} \mid \text{sequence}) \propto P(\text{sequence} \mid \text{gene}) \cdot P(\text{gene})$ | Bayes' rule for gene prediction | Posterior probability |
| 6 | $\text{Transcription rate} \approx k_{cat} \cdot [\text{NTP}] / (K_M + [\text{NTP}])$ | Michaelis–Menten kinetics of RNAP | nt/s |
| 7 | $E[\text{polymer length}] \approx \dfrac{k_{\text{elong}}}{k_{\text{term}}}$ | Mean transcript length (Poisson assumption) | Nucleotides |
| 8 | $Z = \dfrac{x - \mu}{\sigma}$ | Z-score for motif enrichment | Standardized score |
| 9 | $\text{TPM}_i = \dfrac{X_i / \ell_i}{\sum_j X_j / \ell_j} \times 10^6$ | RNA-seq normalization (Transcripts Per Million) | Counts |
| 10 | $\text{RPKM} = \dfrac{X \cdot 10^9}{L \cdot N}$ | Reads Per Kilobase per Million mapped reads | Counts |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL: No vertical pipes inside table cells.** The `\vert` or `\mid` convention is used only when absolutely required, and pipe symbols have been avoided in the table above to keep markdown table syntax intact.

---

## 2.3 Why and How — The "Five W's" of Transcription from a Computational Lens

- **What** sequence signals mark a TSS? → **PWM / HMM models** (e.g., MEME, HMMER, TSSW).
- **Where** in the genome are these signals? → **Promoter-prediction tools** (e.g., Promoter 2.0, NNPP, Dragon Promoter Finder).
- **How** strongly will a TF bind? → **Energy matrices** $E = \sum_j w_j \cdot \mathbb{1}[s_j = b]$ or PWM log-likelihood scores.
- **When** does the gene turn on/off? → **Expression data** (RNA-seq, microarrays) → **differential expression** (DESeq2, edgeR).
- **Which** strand is template? → Strand-specific RNA-seq library prep + **bioinformatic read mapping** (TopHat, STAR, HISAT2).

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world engineering utility:** Synthetic biology & gene-circuit design (e.g., CRISPRa, dCas9-VPR fusions) rely entirely on accurate computational prediction of transcription initiation to engineer promoters with desired strength (strong, medium, weak, inducible).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From DNA Template to RNA — The Base-Pairing Mapping

We derive the formal transcription function $f : \Sigma_{DNA}^* \to \Sigma_{RNA}^*$ for a single nucleotide.

**Given:** DNA alphabet $\Sigma_{DNA} = \{A, C, G, T\}$; RNA alphabet $\Sigma_{RNA} = \{A, C, G, U\}$.

**Complementarity rules (Chargaff's rules):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &\longleftrightarrow T \quad (\text{DNA–DNA pairing}) \\
C &\longleftrightarrow G \quad (\text{DNA–DNA pairing}) \\
A &\longleftrightarrow U \quad (\text{DNA–RNA pairing in transcription}) \\
C &\longleftrightarrow G \quad (\text{DNA–RNA pairing in transcription})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1:** Read template strand 3' → 5' direction. The polymerase synthesizes RNA 5' → 3'.

**Step 2:** Apply base-pairing, but substitute every $T$ in DNA with $U$ in RNA:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{template}} &= A \;\Rightarrow\; R = U \\
T_{\text{template}} &= A \;\Rightarrow\; R = U \\
T_{\text{template}} &= T \;\Rightarrow\; R = A \\
T_{\text{template}} &= C \;\Rightarrow\; R = G \\
T_{\text{template}} &= G \;\Rightarrow\; R = C \\
T_{\text{template}} &= C \;\Rightarrow\; R = G
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Worked example. Template (3'→5'): `T A C T G A C A T T A`. The corresponding coding (sense) strand (5'→3') is the reverse-complement with $T$ kept: `A A T G T C A G T A T`. The RNA transcript (5'→3') is identical to the coding strand but with $U$ replacing $T$:

$$
\boxed{\,\text{mRNA (5'→3')} = \texttt{AAUGUCAGUAU}\,}
$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: CpG Island Score (Observed/Expected Ratio)

CpG islands are computationally important because ~70% of human gene promoters overlap a CpG island. We derive the Obs/Expected CpG statistic.

**Step 1:** Define the observed frequency of the dinucleotide CG in a window of length $L$:

$$
p_{CG} = \dfrac{n_{CG}}{L - 1}
$$

**Step 2:** Define the expected frequency of CG under a null Bernoulli model where each nucleotide is independent:

$$
p_C \cdot p_G = \left(\dfrac{n_C}{L}\right) \cdot \left(\dfrac{n_G}{L}\right)
$$

**Step 3:** Form the ratio:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\left(\dfrac{\text{Obs}}{\text{Exp}}\right)_{CpG} &= \dfrac{p_{CG}}{p_C \cdot p_G} \\
&= \dfrac{n_{CG} / (L-1)}{(n_C / L) \cdot (n_G / L)} \\
&= \dfrac{n_{CG} \cdot L}{(L-1) \cdot n_C \cdot n_G}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Apply the standard Gardiner–Garden & Frommer (1987) thresholds:
- Window: $L \ge 200$ bp
- $\text{GC\%} \ge 50\%$
- $\left(\dfrac{\text{Obs}}{\text{Exp}}\right)_{CpG} \ge 0.6$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These three criteria are universally used in tools such as **CpGplot (EMBOSS)**, **CpGproD**, and UCSC Genome Browser tracks.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Position Weight Matrix (PWM) Log-Odds Score for Motif Matching

A PWM is the **central quantitative object** of computational transcription analysis. We derive it.

**Step 1:** Build a count matrix $C$ of size $4 \times W$ (4 nucleotides, motif width $W$):

$$
C = \begin{pmatrix}
c_{1,A} & c_{2,A} & \cdots & c_{W,A} \\
c_{1,C} & c_{2,C} & \cdots & c_{W,C} \\
c_{1,G} & c_{2,G} & \cdots & c_{W,G} \\
c_{1,T} & c_{2,T} & \cdots & c_{W,T}
\end{pmatrix}
$$

where $c_{j,b}$ is the count of base $b \in \{A,C,G,T\}$ at position $j$.

**Step 2:** Convert to frequency matrix with **pseudocount** $\alpha$ (Laplace smoothing, typically $\alpha = 0.25$ or $0.8$):

$$
f_{j,b} = \dfrac{c_{j,b} + \alpha}{N + 4\alpha}
$$

where $N$ is the total number of aligned training sequences.

**Step 3:** Compute background frequencies $p_b$ from the genome or a local background model.

**Step 4:** Construct PWM $w_{j,b}$ (log-odds):

$$
w_{j,b} = \log_2 \dfrac{f_{j,b}}{p_b}
$$

**Step 5:** Score any candidate sequence $s = s_1 s_2 \dots s_W$:

$$
\text{Score}(s) = \sum_{j=1}^{W} w_{j,\, s_j}
$$

**Step 6 (worked example for TATA box):** Suppose we have a 6-bp motif and after alignment we obtain PWM:

$$
\begin{array}{c|cccccc}
 & 1 & 2 & 3 & 4 & 5 & 6 \\
\hline
A & 0.1 & 0.05 & 0.85 & 0.95 & 0.05 & 0.85 \\
C & 0.2 & 0.05 & 0.05 & 0.02 & 0.05 & 0.05 \\
G & 0.2 & 0.05 & 0.05 & 0.01 & 0.05 & 0.05 \\
T & 0.5 & 0.85 & 0.05 & 0.02 & 0.85 & 0.05 \\
\end{array}
$$

with background $p_A = p_T = 0.3$, $p_C = p_G = 0.2$.

A candidate sequence `T A T A A A` would be scored:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Score} &= \log_2\dfrac{0.5}{0.3} + \log_2\dfrac{0.05}{0.2} + \log_2\dfrac{0.05}{0.2} \\
&\quad + \log_2\dfrac{0.95}{0.3} + \log_2\dfrac{0.05}{0.2} + \log_2\dfrac{0.85}{0.3} \\
&\approx 0.737 + (-2.000) + (-2.000) + 1.663 + (-2.000) + 1.503 \\
&\approx -2.097 \; \text{bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Negative PWM scores are common; the **threshold** is set so that the false-positive rate stays below a chosen level (e.g., $10^{-4}$ per bp). Tools like **MAST**, **FIMO**, and **HOMER** use this exact algorithm.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Prokaryotic vs Eukaryotic Transcription (KTU High-Yield Comparison)

| Feature | Prokaryote | Eukaryote |
|---|---|---|
| Genome organization | Circular, no introns | Linear, chromatin, introns |
| RNA polymerase | One (RNAP, 5 subunits) | Three: **Pol I** (rRNA), **Pol II** (mRNA), **Pol III** (tRNA, 5S rRNA) |
| Promoter elements | **-35 (TTGACA)**, **-10 (TATAAT)** | **TATA box (-30)**, **Inr**, **BRE**, **CAAT box (-80)**, **GC box** |
| Initiation factors | **Sigma factor** (σ) | **GTFs**: TFIID, TFIIA, TFIIB, TFIIE, TFIIF, TFIIH |
| Termination | ρ-dependent / ρ-independent hairpin | Poly-A signal (AAUAAA) + cleavage |
| Coupled to translation | Yes (no nucleus) | No (nuclear compartmentalization) |
| mRNA processing | None / minimal | **5' cap, splicing, 3' poly-A tail** |
| Typical TSS detection method | Computational σ⁷⁰ promoter scan | CpG-island + TATA + Inr + experimental CAGE |
| Promoter-prediction tools | **Prokaryotic Promoter Prediction (PPP)**, **BPROM** | **Promoter 2.0**, **NNPP**, **DragonPF**, **TSSW** |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart: The Transcription Pipeline (Biological + Computational View)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Genomic DNA sequence] --> nodeB[Sliding window scan of length 200 to 500 bp]
    nodeB --> nodeC{Detects promoter motif? -10 -35 or TATA box}
    nodeC -- No --> nodeD[Continue sliding window]
    nodeC -- Yes --> nodeE[Compute PWM log odds score]
    nodeE --> nodeF{Score exceeds threshold}
    nodeF -- No --> nodeD
    nodeF -- Yes --> nodeG[Annotate candidate promoter region]
    nodeG --> nodeH[Identify TSS at position +1]
    nodeH --> nodeI[Initiate in silico transcription]
    nodeI --> nodeJ[RNA polymerase model: elongation]
    nodeJ --> nodeK[Look for terminator signal: hairpin or AAUAAA]
    nodeK --> nodeL[Generate predicted RNA transcript]
    nodeL --> nodeM[Cross-validate with RNA-seq reads]
    nodeM --> nodeN[Final annotated gene model]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Functional Architecture of a Promoter-Prediction Tool

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeInput[Input FASTA sequence] --> nodePre[Preprocessing: masking repeats, soft lowercase]
    nodePre --> nodeSig[Signal detection module: TATA, CAAT, GC, Inr]
    nodePre --> nodeContent[Content sensors: GC percent, CpG obs exp]
    nodePre --> nodeCpG[CpG island module]
    nodeSig --> nodeML[Machine learning classifier: ANN or SVM]
    nodeContent --> nodeML
    nodeCpG --> nodeML
    nodeML --> nodeScore[Probability of promoter at each position]
    nodeScore --> nodeThresh[Threshold and merge overlapping predictions]
    nodeThresh --> nodeOut[Output GFF or BED file with TSS and promoter]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid State Diagram: Phases of Transcription (Mechanistic View)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> ClosedComplex
    ClosedComplex --> OpenComplex : DNA melting at minus 10
    OpenComplex --> AbortiveInitiation : synthesis of 2 to 9 nt RNA
    AbortiveInitiation --> ProductiveElongation : sigma factor release
    ProductiveElongation --> ProductiveElongation : NTP addition 5 prime to 3 prime
    ProductiveElongation --> Termination : terminator hairpin or rho
    Termination --> [*]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram: Transcription Unit and Promoter Anatomy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    node1[Enhancer: distal regulatory element] --> node2[Core Promoter region]
    node2 --> node3[TATA box: TATAAA at minus 30]
    node2 --> node4[BRE: TFIIB recognition element]
    node2 --> node5[Inr: initiator YR at plus 1]
    node2 --> node6[DPE: downstream promoter element at plus 28]
    node3 --> node7[Transcription Start Site TSS at plus 1]
    node4 --> node7
    node5 --> node7
    node6 --> node7
    node7 --> node8[5 prime UTR]
    node8 --> node9[Open Reading Frame ORF]
    node9 --> node10[3 prime UTR]
    node10 --> node11[Poly A signal AAUAAA]
    node11 --> node12[Transcription Terminator]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define transcription. Differentiate between template and coding strands. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

Transcription is the enzymatic process by which a DNA sequence is copied into a complementary RNA molecule by RNA polymerase, using a DNA template strand read in the 3' → 5' direction while the nascent RNA is synthesized in the 5' → 3' direction.

| Feature | Template Strand | Coding (Sense) Strand |
|---|---|---|
| Direction read by RNAP | 3' → 5' | Not read |
| Sequence relationship to RNA | Complementary (with T→U) | Identical (with T→U) |
| Also called | Anti-sense strand | Sense strand / non-template strand |
| Function | Physical instruction for RNAP | Reference for gene annotation |

### Question 2: List any three sequence elements used in computational promoter prediction. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

1. **TATA Box** — consensus **TATAAA** at approximately -25 to -30 bp upstream of TSS; recognized by TBP subunit of TFIID.
2. **CAAT Box** — consensus **GGCCAATCT** at approximately -75 to -80 bp; binds C/EBP and NF-Y transcription factors.
3. **CpG Island** — a region of length ≥ 200 bp, GC% ≥ 50%, and Obs/Exp CpG ratio ≥ 0.6; correlates with ~70% of human gene promoters.
4. **(Bonus)** Initiator (Inr) — consensus **YYANWYY** straddling the TSS, and the **GC box (GGGCGG)** bound by Sp1.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students commonly lose **2–3 marks** by forgetting to mention the **Uracil (U) substitution for Thymine (T)** in RNA — a non-negotiable KTU high-yield point.
> - Drawing the **direction of synthesis** (5'→3' for RNA, 3'→5' for template reading) incorrectly costs the full 7 marks of a sub-part.
> - In computational questions, students often write the **formula** for PWM but skip the **pseudocount correction** — examiners specifically test this.
> - **Always** state the window size, threshold, and the organism context (prokaryote/eukaryote) for promoter-prediction problems.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**(a)** Describe the three phases of transcription in prokaryotes with suitable diagrams. Highlight the role of the sigma factor and terminator hairpin. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the Position Weight Matrix (PWM) method for computational detection of transcription factor binding sites. Construct a PWM for the given aligned sequences of a 4-bp motif: `ACGT, ACGT, ACGT, ACGT, AGGT, ATGT`. Use a pseudocount of 0.5 and a uniform background of 0.25 for each nucleotide. Score the sequence `ACAT`. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a):

1. **Initiation:** The RNA polymerase holoenzyme (core: $\alpha_2\beta\beta'\omega$ + σ factor) binds the -35 (TTGACA) and -10 (TATAAT) promoter elements. The closed complex forms, then DNA strands separate to form the open complex. Abortive transcripts (2–9 nt) are made.
2. **Elongation:** σ factor dissociates; the core RNAP moves along DNA, adding ribonucleotides 5'→3'. The transcription bubble (~17 nt) travels with the enzyme. Error rate ≈ $10^{-5}$ per nt.
3. **Termination:** Two types: (i) **Rho-independent**: GC-rich inverted repeat folds into a hairpin followed by a U-rich tract, causing RNAP to dissociate. (ii) **Rho-dependent**: The ρ helicase binds the rut site and unwinds the RNA-DNA hybrid.
4. **Sigma factor role:** confers promoter specificity, decreases affinity for non-promoter DNA, and is essential for open-complex formation.
5. **Terminator hairpin:** provides the mechanical force to displace the RNA from the DNA-RNA hybrid and the active site, causing dissociation.

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Step 1 — Build count matrix $C$ of size 4×4** from the 6 aligned sequences (`ACGT × 4, AGGT, ATGT`):

|   | pos1 | pos2 | pos3 | pos4 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| A | 6 | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| C | 0 | 6 | 0 | 0 |
| G | 0 | 0 | 6 | 0 |
| T | 0 | 0 | 0 | 6 |

Wait — verify the input. Sequences: ACGT, ACGT, ACGT, ACGT, AGGT, ATGT. Position 1: A A A A A A = 6 A. Position 2: C C C C G T = 4 C, 1 G, 1 T. Position 3: G G G G G G = 6 G. Position 4: T T T T T T = 6 T. So the corrected matrix is:

|   | pos1 | pos2 | pos3 | pos4 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| A | 6 | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| C | 0 | 4 | 0 | 0 |
| G | 0 | 1 | 6 | 0 |
| T | 0 | 1 | 0 | 6 |

**Step 2 — Add pseudocount $\alpha = 0.5$ and normalize by $N + 4\alpha = 6 + 2 = 8$:**

$$
f_{j,b} = \dfrac{c_{j,b} + 0.5}{8}
$$

|   | pos1 | pos2 | pos3 | pos4 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| A | 0.8125 | 0.0625 | 0.0625 | 0.0625 |
| C | 0.0625 | 0.5625 | 0.0625 | 0.0625 |
| G | 0.0625 | 0.1875 | 0.8125 | 0.0625 |
| T | 0.0625 | 0.1875 | 0.0625 | 0.8125 |

**Step 3 — Compute log-odds with background $p_b = 0.25$:**

$$
w_{j,b} = \log_2 \dfrac{f_{j,b}}{0.25} = \log_2(4 \cdot f_{j,b})
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Score}(\texttt{ACAT}) &= \log_2(4 \cdot 0.8125) + \log_2(4 \cdot 0.5625) + \log_2(4 \cdot 0.0625) + \log_2(4 \cdot 0.0625) \\
&= \log_2(3.25) + \log_2(2.25) + \log_2(0.25) + \log_2(0.25) \\
&\approx 1.700 + 1.170 + (-2.000) + (-2.000) \\
&\approx -1.130 \; \text{bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

> The **low (negative) score** is expected: `ACAT` deviates from the conserved `ACGT` motif at positions 3 and 4.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` *(Alternative Choice)*

**(a)** Compare and contrast prokaryotic and eukaryotic transcription in detail, with a focus on promoter elements, RNA polymerases, and transcription factors. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Given the 400-bp upstream region of a human gene with nucleotide composition A = 95, C = 110, G = 115, T = 80, and a total of 22 CpG dinucleotides, determine whether this region qualifies as a CpG island. Show all calculations. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a):

| Aspect | Prokaryotic | Eukaryotic |
|---|---|---|
| Number of RNA polymerases | One | Three (Pol I, II, III) |
| Promoter elements | -10, -35 | TATA, BRE, Inr, DPE, CAAT, GC box, CpG island |
| Initiation proteins | Sigma factor (σ) | TFIID, TFIIA, TFIIB, TFIIE, TFIIF, TFIIH, Mediator |
| Operons | Yes (polycistronic mRNA) | No (monocistronic) |
| Chromatin | None (naked DNA) | Nucleosomes; requires chromatin remodelers |
| mRNA processing | None | 5' cap, splicing, 3' poly-A tail |
| Termination | Rho / hairpin | Poly-A signal + cleavage |
| Compartmentalization | Cytoplasm (coupled) | Nucleus (uncoupled) |

**Key explanation points:**
- Eukaryotic Pol II alone cannot bind DNA; the pre-initiation complex (PIC) of ~50+ proteins must assemble.
- The **TBP (TATA-binding protein)** subunit of TFIID bends the DNA by ~80° at the TATA box.
- TFIIH has both helicase activity (XPB, XPD) to melt DNA and kinase activity to phosphorylate the C-terminal domain (CTD) of Pol II, triggering promoter clearance.
- Prokaryotic promoters are recognized directly by σ without chromatin barriers.

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Given:** $L = 400$ bp, $n_A = 95$, $n_C = 110$, $n_G = 115$, $n_T = 80$, $n_{CG} = 22$.

**Criterion 1 — Window length:**

$$
L = 400 \ge 200 \quad \checkmark
$$

**Criterion 2 — GC content:**

$$
\text{GC\%} = \dfrac{110 + 115}{400} \times 100 = \dfrac{225}{400} \times 100 = 56.25\% \ge 50\% \quad \checkmark
$$

**Criterion 3 — Observed/Expected CpG ratio:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
p_{CG} &= \dfrac{22}{400 - 1} = \dfrac{22}{399} \approx 0.05514 \\
p_C \cdot p_G &= \left(\dfrac{110}{400}\right) \cdot \left(\dfrac{115}{400}\right) = 0.275 \times 0.2875 = 0.07906 \\
\left(\dfrac{\text{Obs}}{\text{Exp}}\right)_{CpG} &= \dfrac{0.05514}{0.07906} \approx 0.697
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** Since $0.697 \ge 0.6$ and all three criteria are satisfied, **the region qualifies as a CpG island**, consistent with its potential role as a human gene promoter.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Transcription = DNA → RNA**, catalyzed by **RNA Polymerase**, direction **5'→3'** for RNA and **3'→5'** for template reading.
- **Replace T with U** in the resulting RNA; this is the single most commonly missed point.
- **Three phases:** Initiation (promoter recognition, open complex), Elongation (processive NTPs added), Termination (hairpin or rho, or poly-A).
- **Prokaryotic promoter:** -35 (TTGACA) + -10 (TATAAT) + TSS; recognized by **σ factor**.
- **Eukaryotic Pol II promoter:** TATA box (-30), Inr (+1), DPE (+28), BRE, CAAT box, GC box, often overlapping a **CpG island**.
- **CpG island criteria (must all be true):** length ≥ 200 bp, GC% ≥ 50%, Obs/Exp CpG ratio ≥ 0.6.
- **PWM score** $S(s) = \sum_{j=1}^{W} w_{j,s_j}$ with $w_{j,b} = \log_2(f_{j,b}/p_b)$ and **pseudocount** added to avoid $\log(0)$.
- **Gene-prediction Bayes' rule:** $P(\text{gene}\mid\text{seq}) \propto P(\text{seq}\mid\text{gene})\,P(\text{gene})$.
- **RNA-seq normalization:** TPM is length-normalized then library-size normalized; RPKM is older.
- **Termination signals:** prokaryote — GC hairpin + U-tract (ρ-independent) or rut + ρ; eukaryote — AAUAAA poly-A signal.
- **Tools to remember:** MEME, FIMO (motifs); Promoter 2.0, NNPP (promoters); CpGplot (CpG islands); STAR, TopHat, DESeq2 (RNA-seq).
- **Mnemonic — TATAAA = "TATA's in the BOX, 30 bp upstream of the start".**
- **Mnemonic — CpG Island = "200, 50, 0.6"** (length, GC%, Obs/Exp).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Translation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT526 - COMPUTATIONAL BIOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Translation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Translation** is the biosynthetic process in which the sequence of codons in a messenger RNA (mRNA) molecule is decoded by the ribosome to synthesize a polypeptide chain (protein). In **Computational Biology**, *in silico* translation refers to the algorithmic conversion of a nucleotide sequence (DNA or mRNA) into its corresponding amino acid sequence using the **Standard Genetic Code** (codon table), without the need for any living cellular machinery.

The biological translation machinery consists of three principal actors:

| Actor | Function |
| :--- | :--- |
| **mRNA (messenger RNA)** | Carries the genetic blueprint transcribed from DNA, read in 5' → 3' direction |
| **tRNA (transfer RNA)** | Adaptor molecule carrying an anticodon and the corresponding amino acid |
| **Ribosome** | Ribozyme (rRNA + proteins) that catalyzes peptide bond formation |

## 1.2 The Universal Codon Table – KTU Syllabus Anchor

$$ \text{Number of possible codons} = 4^3 = 64 $$

Out of these 64 codons:
- **61 codons** encode the **20 standard amino acids** (degeneracy/redundancy)
- **3 codons** are *stop signals* (termination): **UAA, UAG, UGA** (named *ochre*, *amber*, *opal*)
- **1 codon (AUG)** acts as the universal *start signal* and codes for **Methionine (Met, M)**

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Factory Assembly Line"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Imagine a 3D printing factory:**
> - The **mRNA** is the **blueprint document** rolled out on a conveyor belt.
> - The **ribosome** is the **printer head** that slides along the document, reading one 3-letter word (codon) at a time.
> - The **tRNA** molecules are the **ink cartridges**, each labeled (anticodon) to match exactly one 3-letter code, and each carrying a specific colored powder (amino acid).
> - As the printer head moves, cartridges dock, deliver their color, and the printer welds (peptide bond) the colors together into a finished chain (protein).

This analogy clarifies why computational translation only needs the **codebook** (codon table) — the physical "printer" is replaced by an algorithm.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Salient Features of the Genetic Code (High-Yield for KTU)

1. **Triplet nature** — Three nucleotides (a codon) specify one amino acid.
2. **Non-overlapping** — Each nucleotide belongs to exactly one codon.
3. **Commaless / No gaps** — Reading frame is continuous; punctuation is provided by start/stop codons.
4. **Degeneracy (Redundancy)** — Multiple codons can specify the same amino acid (e.g., 6 codons for Leucine).
5. **Universality** — Almost identical across all organisms (with minor mitochondrial exceptions).
6. **Unambiguity** — A given codon always codes for the *same* amino acid (no two meanings).
7. **Polarity** — Codons for similar amino acids share similar nucleotides (e.g., all hydrophobic residues have U as the middle base).

## 2.2 The Wobble Hypothesis (Crick, 1966)

> [!NOTE]
> The **3rd base** of a codon (the *wobble position*) tolerates non-standard (non-Watson–Crick) base pairing with the 5' anticodon of tRNA. This explains how **61 sense codons** can be read by only **~30–40 tRNA species**.

Mathematical consequence — the *effective coding capacity* per tRNA:

$$ N_{\text{pairings}} = \sum_{i} \binom{4}{i} \text{  (for allowed wobble rules)} $$

Standard wobble rules:

| 5' Anticodon Base | Recognized 3' Codon Bases |
| :--- | :--- |
| **C** | G only |
| **A** | U only |
| **G** | C or U |
| **U** | A or G |
| **Inosine (I)** | A, U, or C |

## 2.3 The Three Stages of Biological Translation

1. **Initiation** — Small ribosomal subunit binds 5' cap of mRNA, scans to **AUG** start codon, recruits initiator tRNA$^Met$, large subunit joins. Initiation factors: eIFs (eukaryotes) / IFs (prokaryotes).
2. **Elongation** — Three-step cycle: (i) aminoacyl-tRNA binds **A-site**, (ii) peptidyl transfer (peptide bond), (iii) translocation to **P-site**, then **E-site** (exit).
3. **Termination** — Stop codon enters A-site; release factors (eRF1 in eukaryotes / RF1, RF2 in prokaryotes) hydrolyze the polypeptide.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Expression | Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Number of codons | $4^n$ | $n$ = nucleotide length of codon (typically 3) |
| Coding capacity | $4^3 = 64$ | 61 sense + 3 stop |
| Information per codon | $\log_2 64 = 6$ **bits** | Shannon info measure |
| Avg. info per nucleotide | $2$ **bits** | $6/3$ |
| Codon degeneracy (Leu/Ser/Arg) | $6$ codons | Most degenerate |
| Stop codons | **UAA, UAG, UGA** | 3 of 64 |
| Start codon | **AUG** | Codes for Met |
| Six reading frames | $3(\text{forward}) + 3(\text{reverse}) = 6$ | Antiparallel dsDNA consideration |
| ORF length (aa) | $L_{aa} = (L_{nt} - L_{\text{stop}})/3$ | $L_{nt}$ in nucleotides |
| Codon usage frequency | $f_{ijk} = \dfrac{N_{ijk}}{\sum_{abc} N_{abc}}$ | Relative Synonymous Codon Usage (RSCU) basis |
| RSCU | $RSCU_{ij} = \dfrac{\text{Observed}_{ij}}{\text{Expected}_{ij}} = \dfrac{N_{ij}}{(1/n_i)\sum_j N_{ij}}$ | $n_i$ = synonymous codons for aa $i$ |
| Shannon entropy of codon usage | $H = -\sum_i p_i \log_2 p_i$ | Measured in **bits/codon** |

> Use $\vert x \vert$ rendered as $\vert x \vert$ (vertical bar is escaped via LaTeX only, never inside markdown table pipes).

## 2.5 Why Translation Matters in Computational Biology (Engineering Utility)

- **Gene prediction** — identifying coding regions (ORFs) in raw genomic DNA.
- **Annotation of newly sequenced genomes** — translating hypothetical ORFs to predict function via homology (BLAST).
- **Synthetic biology / gene design** — codon optimization for heterologous expression in *E. coli*, yeast, CHO cells.
- **Drug target identification** — translating viral ORFs to find conserved protein motifs.
- **Phylogenetics** — protein-level alignments are often more informative than nucleotide alignments.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Codon-Table Mapping and Number of Reading Frames

### 3.1.1 Reading-Frame Enumeration

**Step 1** — A single-stranded mRNA of length $L$ nucleotides can be read in **3 distinct reading frames** (offsets 0, 1, 2):

$$ \text{Frames}_{ss} = \{ \text{Frame}_0, \text{Frame}_1, \text{Frame}_2 \} $$

**Step 2** — A double-stranded DNA (dsDNA) is read computationally on **both strands** because genes can lie on either strand. Each strand contributes 3 frames:

$$ \text{Frames}_{ds} = 3 \text{ (forward)} + 3 \text{ (reverse)} = 6 \text{ frames} $$

**Step 3** — The reverse strand sequence $S^{\text{rev}}$ is obtained by:
1. **Reverse** the original 5'→3' sequence.
2. **Complement** each base ($A \leftrightarrow T$, $C \leftrightarrow G$).
This is the *reverse-complement* operation:

$$ S^{\text{rev}} = \text{complement}\big(\text{reverse}(S)\big) $$

**Step 4** — Total possible protein sequences from a random DNA of length $L$ nt (ignoring stop codons):

$$ N_{\text{translations}} = 6 \text{ frames} \times \left\lfloor \frac{L}{3} \right\rfloor \text{ codons/frame} $$

### 3.1.2 Worked Example — Six-Frame Translation

**Input sequence (5'→3', sense strand):**
`ATGGCCATTGTAATGGGCCGCTGAAAGGGTGCCCGATAG`

**Step A — Forward strand reading frames:**

Frame +1 (offset 0): `ATG GCC ATT GTA ATG GGC CGC TGA AAG GGT GCC CGA TAG`
Frame +2 (offset 1): `TGG CCA TTG TAA TGG GCC GCT GAA AGG GTG CCC GAT AG`
Frame +3 (offset 2): `GGC CAT TGT AAT GGG CCG CTG AAA GGG TGC CCG ATA G`

**Step B — Reverse complement:**

`CTATCGGGCACCCTTTCAGCGGCCCATTACAATGGCCAT`

Frame −1: `CTA TCG GGC ACC CTT TCA GCG GCC CAT TAC AAT GGC CAT`
Frame −2: `C GAT CGG GCA CCC TTT CAG CGG CCC ATT ACA ATG GCC AT`
Frame −3: `CG ATC GGG CAC CCT TTC AGC GGC CCA TTA CAA TGG CCA T`

**Step C — Translation using standard genetic code:**

- Frame +1: **M-A-I-V-M-G-R-\* (stop)** → 7 amino acids then termination
- Frame +3: **G-H-C-N-G-P-L-K-G-C-P-I** → no stop in window
- Other frames: contain internal stops → likely non-coding.

## 3.2 Derivation: Open Reading Frame (ORF) Detection Algorithm

### 3.2.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> An **Open Reading Frame (ORF)** is a continuous stretch of codons in a given reading frame that begins with a start codon (AUG in eukaryotes; AUG, GUG, UUG in prokaryotes) and ends with a *nearest downstream* in-frame stop codon (UAA, UAG, UGA), with no intervening stop codons.

### 3.2.2 Step-by-Step Algorithm (Logic Flow)

**Step 1** — Generate the three forward and three reverse-complement reading frames of the input DNA (Section 3.1.1, Step 1–3).

**Step 2** — For each frame, scan codons from position 1 (5' end):

$$ \text{For codon } c_i \in \{c_1, c_2, \dots, c_{\lfloor L/3 \rfloor}\} \text{ in frame } f: $$

**Step 3** — If $c_i = \text{AUG}$ (start), mark position $i$ as ORF start candidate.

**Step 4** — Continue reading codons $c_{i+1}, c_{i+2}, \dots$ and accumulate amino acids. Stop scanning only when a stop codon ($c_j \in \{\text{UAA, UAG, UGA}\}$) is encountered.

**Step 5** — Record the ORF as the segment $[i, j]$:

$$ \text{ORF}_{ij} = (c_i, c_{i+1}, \dots, c_{j-1}) \text{ such that } c_i = \text{AUG},\ c_j \in \text{Stops} $$

**Step 6** — ORF length in nucleotides:

$$ L_{\text{ORF}} = 3 \times (j - i + 1) $$

**Step 7** — ORF length in amino acids (excluding stop):

$$ L_{\text{aa}} = j - i $$

**Step 8** — Apply minimum-length filter (a common bioinformatics threshold):

$$ L_{\text{aa}} \geq L_{\min}, \quad \text{typically } L_{\min} = 100 \text{ aa} $$

**Step 9** — Report all ORFs satisfying the threshold across all 6 frames, sorted by length (longest first).

### 3.2.3 Worked Numerical Example

Sequence: `ATGAAACGCATTAGCATGCCCCGATAATAA`
Length $L = 30$ nt, so $\lfloor L/3 \rfloor = 10$ codons in each forward frame.

**Frame +1 codons:**

| Pos $i$ | Codon $c_i$ | AA |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 1 | ATG | M (start) |
| 2 | AAA | K |
| 3 | CGC | R |
| 4 | ATT | I |
| 5 | AGC | S |
| 6 | ATG | M |
| 7 | CCC | P |
| 8 | GAT | D |
| 9 | AAT | N |
| 10 | TAA | * (stop) |

**ORF detected:** $i=1,\ j=10$ → length $L_{\text{aa}} = 9$ amino acids (M-K-R-I-S-M-P-D-N).

If threshold is 100 aa, this ORF is rejected. With a smaller threshold (e.g., 5 aa), it is accepted.

## 3.3 Derivation: Relative Synonymous Codon Usage (RSCU)

RSCU quantifies **codon bias** — organisms do not use synonymous codons equally.

**Step 1** — Let $N_{ij}$ = observed count of codon $j$ for amino acid $i$.

**Step 2** — Let $n_i$ = number of synonymous codons for amino acid $i$ (e.g., $n_{\text{Leu}} = 6$).

**Step 3** — Expected count under *no bias*:

$$ E_{ij} = \frac{\sum_j N_{ij}}{n_i} $$

**Step 4** — RSCU:

$$ RSCU_{ij} = \frac{N_{ij}}{E_{ij}} = \frac{n_i \cdot N_{ij}}{\sum_j N_{ij}} $$

**Step 5** — Interpretation:
- $RSCU_{ij} = 1.0$ → codon used as expected (no bias).
- $RSCU_{ij} > 1.0$ → codon used *more* than expected (preferred).
- $RSCU_{ij} < 1.0$ → codon used *less* than expected (avoided).

**Step 6** — Numerical worked example for Leucine in a small gene: codons observed = {CUU: 12, CUC: 3, CUA: 1, CUG: 24, UUA: 2, UUG: 8}. Total = 50.

$$ RSCU_{\text{CUG}} = \frac{6 \times 24}{50} = \frac{144}{50} = 2.88 $$

So **CUG is heavily preferred** for Leucine in this gene (e.g., typical in *E. coli*).

## 3.4 Shannon Entropy of Codon Usage

The randomness / diversity of codon usage is measured by Shannon entropy (in **bits per codon position**):

$$ H = -\sum_{i=1}^{64} p_i \log_2 p_i \quad \text{where } p_i = \frac{N_i}{\sum_j N_j} $$

- $H_{\max} = \log_2 64 = 6$ **bits** (uniform usage — no bias).
- Lower $H$ → stronger codon bias.

This is the core metric used by the **CAI (Codon Adaptation Index)** and **codon bias analysis tools** (e.g., EMBOSS `cusp`, `chips`).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Computational Translation Pipeline

The diagram below depicts the end-to-end *in silico* translation workflow — from raw sequence input to functional protein annotation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw DNA FASTA Input] --> B[Sequence Validation and Cleaning]
    B --> C[Reverse Complement Generation]
    C --> D[Six Frame Reading Frame Extraction]
    D --> E[Frame 1 Fwd]
    D --> F[Frame 2 Fwd]
    D --> G[Frame 3 Fwd]
    D --> H[Frame 1 Rev]
    D --> I[Frame 2 Rev]
    D --> J[Frame 3 Rev]
    E --> K[Codon Table Lookup]
    F --> K
    G --> K
    H --> K
    I --> K
    J --> K
    K --> L[Translated Protein Sequences 6]
    L --> M[ORF Detection and Filtering]
    M --> N[Longest ORF Selection]
    N --> O[Homology Search BLASTp]
    O --> P[Functional Annotation Output]
```

**Interpretation of the diagram:**
- **Node A→B** ensures IUPAC nucleotide alphabet compliance.
- **Node C** produces the antisense strand.
- **Node D→J** decomposes both strands into triplets.
- **Node K** is the *codon table* — a 64-entry dictionary mapping.
- **Node M** is the bioinformatics decision step (length threshold, start/stop).
- **Node O** integrates the translated protein into a knowledge base.

## 4.2 Process Diagram: The Elongation Cycle of Ribosomal Translation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[A site Empty] -->|Aminoacyl tRNA Binds| B[A site Occupied]
    B -->|Peptidyl Transferase Activity| C[Peptide Bond Formed]
    C -->|Translocation by EF G| D[A site becomes P site]
    D -->|E site tRNA Released| E[P site Holds Peptidyl tRNA]
    E -->|Next Codon Read| A
```

## 4.3 Hierarchical Block View: Codon-to-Amino Acid Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Start Codon Recognition] --> T{Codon in A site of Ribosome}
    T -->|AUG| M[Methionine Initiator tRNA]
    T -->|UAA UAG or UGA| ST[Release Factor RF1 or RF2]
    T -->|Any of 61 sense codons| AA[Corresponding Aminoacyl tRNA Recruited]
    AA --> PT[Peptide Bond Catalyzed by 23S rRNA]
    M --> PT
    PT --> NX[Next Codon]
    ST --> TER[Polypeptide Released Termination]
```

**Engineering takeaway:** The decision tree above is exactly mirrored by an `if-elif-else` lookup structure in a translation algorithm — the genetic code is essentially a hash map of size 64.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
**List and explain any four salient features of the genetic code.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Triplet code** — Three consecutive nucleotides specify one amino acid, yielding $4^3 = 64$ codons. (0.75)
2. **Degeneracy (redundancy)** — Multiple codons (up to 6 for Leu, Ser, Arg) code for the same amino acid, increasing robustness. (0.75)
3. **Universality** — The code is nearly identical across all organisms (bacteria, plants, animals), with rare exceptions in mitochondrial genomes. (0.75)
4. **Start and Stop punctuation** — **AUG** is the universal start codon (Met); **UAA, UAG, UGA** are stop codons. (0.75)

*(Any four equivalent features with one-line explanation fetch full 3 marks.)*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Expected Pattern]**
**What is meant by "six-frame translation"? Why is it performed computationally?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Six-frame translation is the process of translating a double-stranded DNA sequence in **all six possible reading frames** — three on the sense (5'→3') strand at offsets 0, 1, 2, and three on the reverse-complement (antisense) strand at the same offsets. (1.5 marks)

It is performed computationally because:
- The **coding strand is not known a priori** in newly sequenced DNA. (0.75)
- A gene may lie on **either strand**, and frameshift errors can completely change the protein. (0.75)

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students forget to mention the *reverse-complement* operation and only describe the three forward frames — this loses **at least 1 mark**. Always specify that six = three forward + three reverse-complement.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Expected Pattern]`

**(a)** Explain the biological process of translation in *E. coli* with neat block diagram. Discuss the role of initiation, elongation, and termination factors. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe with example how the Standard Genetic Code is used for *in silico* translation. Translate the following sequence in all three forward frames:
`5'-ATGAAGCTTCTGTAA-3'` **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to A(a):

**Step 1 — Initiation (2 marks):**
The small ribosomal subunit (30S) binds the Shine–Dalgarno sequence (AGGAGG) on the mRNA. Initiator fMet-tRNA$^fMet$ (formyl-methionine, prokaryotic start) is recruited. The large subunit (50S) joins to form the 70S initiation complex. Initiation factors **IF1, IF2, IF3** are involved; GTP hydrolysis drives subunit joining.

**Step 2 — Elongation (3 marks):**
A three-step cycle repeats for each codon:
- **A-site binding** — Elongation factor **EF-Tu·GTP** delivers the correct aminoacyl-tRNA.
- **Peptide bond formation** — Catalyzed by the **23S rRNA** (a ribozyme) of the 50S subunit; the growing chain is transferred to the new amino acid.
- **Translocation** — **EF-G·GTP** pushes the ribosome by one codon, shifting tRNAs from A → P → E sites.
One GTP is hydrolyzed per step; the cycle is fast (~15–20 codons/sec in *E. coli*).

**Step 3 — Termination (2 marks):**
When a stop codon (UAA/UAG/UGA) enters the A-site, no tRNA recognizes it. **Release factors RF1 (UAA, UAG) and RF2 (UAA, UGA)** bind. **RF3·GTP** stimulates hydrolysis of the peptidyl-tRNA, releasing the polypeptide. The ribosome dissociates into subunits, recycled by **IF3**.

**Block Diagram (compulsory for full marks):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[30S Subunit binds mRNA Shine Dalgarno] --> B[fMet tRNA recruited IF2 GTP]
    B --> C[50S Joins Forms 70S Initiation Complex]
    C --> D{A site Encounter}
    D -->|Sense Codon| E[EF Tu Delivers aa tRNA]
    E --> F[Peptide Bond by 23S rRNA]
    F --> G[EF G Translocation]
    G --> D
    D -->|Stop Codon UAA UAG UGA| H[RF1 or RF2 Binds]
    H --> I[Polypeptide Released RF3 GTP]
    I --> J[Ribosome Recycled by IF3]
```

---

#### Model Solution to A(b):

**Step 1 — Genetic code concept (1 mark):**
The Standard Genetic Code is a lookup table mapping each of the 64 triplet codons to an amino acid (or stop signal). It is implemented computationally as a dictionary/hash map of size 64.

**Step 2 — Sequence given:** `5'-ATGAAGCTTCTGTAA-3'` (length 15 nt, so 5 codons in frame +1).

**Step 3 — Three forward frame translations (4 marks):**

| Frame | Codons (3-letter) | Translation (1-letter) |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| +1 (offset 0) | `ATG AAG CTT CTG TAA` | **M-K-L-L-\*** (5 aa then stop) |
| +2 (offset 1) | `TGA AGC TTC TGT AA` | \*-S-F-C (starts with stop → truncated) |
| +3 (offset 2) | `GAA GCT TCT GTA A` | E-A-S-V (incomplete final codon) |

**Step 4 — Interpretation (2 marks):**
- **Frame +1** yields the longest meaningful ORF with a clean start (AUG = Met) and stop (TAA). This is the *coding frame*.
- Frames +2 and +3 are non-coding or spurious translations in this example.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (Part A/b):** Students often:
> 1. Forget to convert DNA codons (using **T**) into RNA codons (using **U**) — but for *in silico* translation, the DNA codon table (with T instead of U) is acceptable. Both answers are valid. State your convention clearly.
> 2. Forget to mark the **stop codon** explicitly with an asterisk `*` — this is a mandatory 0.5-mark penalty zone.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]`

**(a)** What is an Open Reading Frame (ORF)? Describe the algorithmic steps for ORF detection in a DNA sequence. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Define **Codon Usage Bias (CUB)**. Compute the RSCU values for the amino acid *Arginine* from the following observed codon counts: {CGU: 8, CGC: 12, CGA: 1, CGG: 1, AGA: 4, AGG: 2}. Comment on the result. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to B(a):

**Step 1 — Definition of ORF (1 mark):**
An ORF is a continuous stretch of codons in a specific reading frame that begins with a **start codon (AUG)** and ends at the next in-frame **stop codon (UAA/UAG/UGA)**, with no intervening stop codons.

**Step 2 — Why ORFs matter (1 mark):**
ORFs are the primary *ab initio* evidence for protein-coding regions; in prokaryotes, long ORFs (>100 aa) are strong gene predictors.

**Step 3 — Algorithmic procedure (5 marks):**

1. **Input** — DNA sequence $S$ of length $L$ nt.
2. **Generate reverse complement** $S^{\text{rev}}$ (Section 3.1.1).
3. **Extract 6 reading frames** — 3 from $S$ and 3 from $S^{\text{rev}}$.
4. **Tokenize** each frame into codons of length 3.
5. **Scan codons** sequentially; when a start codon is found, mark the position.
6. **Continue reading** codons until a stop codon or end-of-sequence is reached.
7. **Record** the ORF with coordinates (frame, start, stop, length, translation).
8. **Filter** by minimum length (e.g., $L_{\min} = 100$ aa or user-defined).
9. **Output** sorted list of ORFs (longest first) with protein sequences.

**Pseudocode (logic level, language-agnostic):**

```
function findORFs(sequence S):
    orfs = []
    for strand in [S, reverseComplement(S)]:
        for offset in [0, 1, 2]:
            frame = codons of strand starting at offset
            in_orf = false
            for i, codon in enumerate(frame):
                if not in_orf and codon == "ATG":
                    start = i; in_orf = true
                elif in_orf and codon in ["TAA","TAG","TGA"]:
                    orfs.append(ORF(frame, start, i))
                    in_orf = false
    return sorted(orfs, key=length, reverse=True)
```

*(This is **algorithm description** for an exam — full code listing is not required. The KTU 2024 scheme expects logic flow, not syntax.)*

---

#### Model Solution to B(b):

**Step 1 — Definition of CUB (2 marks):**
Codon Usage Bias is the non-random, preferential use of synonymous codons by different organisms (and even different genes within the same organism). It reflects a balance between:
- **Translation efficiency** (favored codons match abundant tRNAs).
- **Accuracy** (avoiding mis-incorporation).
- **GC content** of the genome.

**Step 2 — RSCU Formula Recap (1 mark):**

$$ RSCU_{ij} = \frac{n_i \cdot N_{ij}}{\sum_j N_{ij}} $$

For Arginine, $n_i = 6$ (six synonymous codons). Total observed:

$$ \sum_j N_{ij} = 8 + 12 + 1 + 1 + 4 + 2 = 28 $$

**Step 3 — Step-by-step RSCU computation (3 marks):**

| Codon | $N_{ij}$ | $RSCU = (6 \times N_{ij}) / 28$ | Result |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| CGU | 8 | $48 / 28$ | **1.714** |
| CGC | 12 | $72 / 28$ | **2.571** |
| CGA | 1 | $6 / 28$ | **0.214** |
| CGG | 1 | $6 / 28$ | **0.214** |
| AGA | 4 | $24 / 28$ | **0.857** |
| AGG | 2 | $12 / 28$ | **0.429** |
| **Sum** | 28 | — | **6.000** ✓ |

**Step 4 — Comment (1 mark):**
- **CGC** ($RSCU = 2.571$) is the most preferred Arg codon.
- **CGA** and **CGG** are strongly avoided ($RSCU = 0.214$).
- This bias is consistent with high-GC organisms (e.g., *Streptomyces*), where C-ending codons are favored.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (Part B/b):**
> 1. **Arithmetic slip** — students frequently compute RSCU as $N_{ij}/\text{mean}$ but forget to multiply by $n_i$. Verify the formula before computing.
> 2. **No sum check** — RSCU values for a given amino acid should sum to exactly $n_i$ (here, 6). A quick sanity check catches errors and earns partial credit.
> 3. **No biological interpretation** — listing numbers without *commenting* on the preferred/avoided codons forfeits the final 1 mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Revision Bullets — Translation in Computational Biology**

- **Translation = mRNA → Protein** (computational translation = codon-table lookup).
- The genetic code has **64 codons** → $4^3$, encoding **20 amino acids + 3 stop signals**.
- **Start codon:** **AUG** (Methionine). **Stop codons:** **UAA, UAG, UGA**.
- The code is **degenerate, universal, non-overlapping, commaless, and unambiguous**.
- **Wobble hypothesis** (Crick, 1966) — flexibility at the **3rd codon position** explains $\sim$30 tRNAs reading 61 codons.
- **Six-frame translation** = 3 forward + 3 reverse-complement frames; the reverse-complement is mandatory.
- **ORF** = start codon (AUG) → first in-frame stop, no internal stops.
- ORF length filter commonly set at **100 amino acids** for gene prediction in prokaryotes.
- **Codon Usage Bias (CUB)** is organism-specific; quantified by **RSCU**.
  - $RSCU = 1$ → no bias; $RSCU > 1$ → preferred; $RSCU < 1$ → avoided.
  - RSCU values for a single amino acid **sum to $n_i$** (number of synonymous codons).
- **Shannon entropy** of codon usage: $H = -\sum p_i \log_2 p_i$; maximum 6 bits = no bias.
- **Initiation factors (prokaryotes):** IF1, IF2, IF3. **Elongation factors:** EF-Tu, EF-G. **Release factors:** RF1, RF2, RF3.
- **Shine–Dalgarno sequence** (AGGAGG) aligns 30S to prokaryotic mRNA; **Kozak sequence** (gccRccAUGG) does the same in eukaryotes.
- The 23S rRNA is a **ribozyme** — the catalytic engine of peptide bond formation.
- Common exam tools: **EMBOSS `transeq`** (six-frame), **`getorf`** (ORFs), **`cusp`** (codon usage).
- For synthetic gene design, always optimize codons for the **host organism** (e.g., humanized codons for mammalian expression).
- In exams, **always draw a block diagram** for translation processes — missing diagrams cost 1–2 marks.
- Mention the **direction of reading** (5'→3' mRNA; N→C protein) at least once for full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to Structure of Prokaryotic and Eukaryote Gene
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT526 - COMPUTATIONAL BIOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Structure of Prokaryotic and Eukaryote Gene

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **gene** is the fundamental physical and functional unit of heredity, consisting of a specific nucleotide sequence in DNA (or RNA, in some viruses) that encodes the information required to produce a functional product, typically a protein or a non-coding RNA molecule (such as tRNA, rRNA, or snRNA). The structure of a gene defines the precise arrangement of its regulatory, coding, and non-coding regions along the DNA molecule, which determines how genetic information is transcribed and ultimately translated into a phenotype.

In **molecular biology**, gene structure is studied under two broad categories corresponding to the cellular organization of the organism:

- **Prokaryotic Genes** — Found in organisms lacking a true membrane-bound nucleus (Bacteria and Archaea). The gene is typically **continuous (collinear)** with its mRNA, meaning the coding sequence is uninterrupted.
- **Eukaryotic Genes** — Found in organisms whose cells contain a membrane-bound nucleus (Animals, Plants, Fungi, Protists). The gene is typically **discontinuous (split)**, containing coding regions (exons) interrupted by non-coding regions (introns).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The distinction between *collinear* (prokaryotic) and *split / mosaic* (eukaryotic) gene organization is a **high-frequency 3-mark question** in KTU board examinations. Memorize the terms **"colinearity"** and **"split gene"** as exam-ready keywords.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Library & Recipe Book

Imagine the **cell** as a large industrial **kitchen**, and the **genome** as the master **recipe book** stored safely in the library (the nucleus in eukaryotes; the cytoplasm in prokaryotes).

- Each **gene** is a single **recipe** (a set of instructions to bake a specific protein-cake).
- The **promoter region** is the *title of the recipe* — it tells the chef (RNA polymerase) "Start reading here!"
- The **coding sequence** is the *actual recipe steps* — the edible instructions.
- The **terminator sequence** is the word *"STOP"* written at the end of the recipe.
- **Introns** in eukaryotes are like *unnecessary editor's notes or advertisements printed in the middle of the recipe* — they are spliced out (cut) before the recipe is delivered to the kitchen.
- **Exons** are the *true, useful recipe lines* that are kept and stitched together.

> [!NOTE]
> Just as a chef would never leave an advertisement inside a recipe instruction, a eukaryotic cell's **spliceosome complex** removes introns from pre-mRNA to form mature mRNA. Prokaryotes do not have introns in most genes — their "recipes" are published clean and ready-to-cook.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Key Constants

| Biological Unit | Standard Size / Metric | Functional Role |
|-----------------|------------------------|------------------|
| Average gene length (prokaryotic) | **~1,000 base pairs (1 kb)** | Compact, efficient genome |
| Average gene length (eukaryotic) | **~10,000–50,000 base pairs (10–50 kb)** | Long due to introns |
| Average exon length | **~150 bp** | Average exon size in humans |
| Average intron length | **~3,000 bp** | Highly variable, can be > 100 kb |
| Human genome size | **~3.2 × 10⁹ bp** | ~20,000–25,000 protein-coding genes |
| *E. coli* genome size | **~4.6 × 10⁶ bp** | ~4,400 genes |
| Coding density (prokaryote) | **~90%** | Almost every base is coding |
| Coding density (eukaryote) | **~1.5%** | Vast majority is non-coding / intronic |
