```mermaid
    N --> O[Step 5: Count all residue pair occurrences in all columns]
    O --> P[Step 6: Compute observed joint frequencies q_ab]
    P --> Q[Step 7: Compute background frequencies p_a from data]
    Q --> R[Step 8: Compute log-odds S_ab = log2 q_ab divided by p_a times p_b]
    R --> S[Step 9: Scale by factor 2 and round to integers]
    S --> T[Output BLOSUM 45 / 62 / 80]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of a Scoring Matrix in an Alignment Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Q1[Query protein sequence] --> SA[Alignment algorithm DP]
    DB1[Database of subject sequences] --> SA
    SM[Scoring matrix PAM or BLOSUM] --> SA
    GP[Gap penalty model opening and extension] --> SA
    SA --> AL[Optimal local or global alignment]
    AL --> EV[Statistical significance E value]
    EV --> RS[Ranked list of homologous hits]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Concept Map — Score Interpretation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SC[Residue pair score] --> POS[Positive score greater than 0]
    SC --> ZER[Zero score approx 0]
    SC --> NEG[Negative score less than 0]
    POS --> P1[Conservative substitution biochemically similar]
    POS --> P2[Likely in evolution e.g. L to I]
    ZER --> Z1[Neutral substitution no strong signal]
    NEG --> N1[Non-conservative biochemically different e.g. L to D]
    NEG --> N2[Rarely accepted by natural selection]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define the term "scoring matrix" in the context of sequence alignment. Why are log-odds scores preferred over raw frequency ratios?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A scoring matrix is a 20 × 20 table that assigns a numerical score to every possible pairing of the 20 standard amino acids, used to evaluate the quality of a residue–residue alignment during sequence comparison.

Log-odds scores are preferred over raw ratios $q_{ab}/(p_a p_b)$ because:

1. The logarithm converts *products* of probabilities (which become vanishingly small over long alignments) into *sums* of manageable magnitudes (score sum, 1 mark).
2. The score becomes **additive** across alignment positions, which is the mathematical property that enables dynamic programming algorithms to optimize total alignment score (1 mark).
3. Logarithmic scaling provides an information-theoretic interpretation in **bits**, allowing direct comparison across different matrices and substitution models (1 mark).

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between PAM 250 and BLOSUM 62 with respect to (i) data source, (ii) the meaning of the numeric suffix, and (iii) the evolutionary distance for which each is best suited.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | PAM 250 | BLOSUM 62 |
|---|---|---|
| Data source | Global alignments of closely related full-length proteins (1 mark) | Local conserved blocks from the BLOCKS database (1 mark) |
| Numeric suffix meaning | Evolutionary distance: 250 accepted mutations per 100 residues | Sequence identity threshold: ≥ 62 % identity within clusters (0.5 marks) |
| Best suited for | Detecting very distant evolutionary relationships (0.5 marks) | Detecting moderate to close relationships; default in NCBI BLAST (0.5 marks, if specifically noted) |

*(Note: Marks are awarded for correctly highlighting the conceptual contrast — the typical 3-mark answer should contain a short table or 3 crisp bullet points.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Modified]`
**(a) Explain in detail the construction of the PAM 250 substitution matrix, with emphasis on the Markov chain extrapolation step.** **(7 marks)**

**(b) Construct the log-odds score for the substitution pair (Asparagine, Aspartate) given the following hypothetical data:** $q_{N,D} = 0.020$, $p_N = 0.044$, $p_D = 0.054$. Assume BLOSUM-style half-bit scaling. Comment on the result. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Construction of PAM 250 (7 marks):**

1. **Data Acquisition (1 mark):** Dayhoff's group selected 71 protein families with > 85 % sequence identity, totalling 1,572 observed amino acid substitutions.
2. **Phylogenetic Reconstruction (1 mark):** For each family, a phylogenetic tree was built; substitutions were counted *only along accepted tree branches* to avoid redundant observation of the same event.
3. **Relative Mutability (1 mark):** For each amino acid $j$, the relative mutability $m_j$ was computed as the ratio of observed changes involving $j$ to the expected number given its frequency.
4. **PAM 1 Construction (1 mark):** A 20 × 20 matrix $M^{(1)}$ was assembled where $M^{(1)}_{ij}$ is the probability of residue $i$ mutating to $j$ in a 1 %-change interval. Rows normalized to sum to 1.
5. **Markov Extrapolation (2 marks):** Because each short interval is treated as an independent stochastic event, the probability of change over $n$ intervals is the matrix power:
   $$M^{(250)} = \left( M^{(1)} \right)^{250}$$
6. **Final Log-Odds (1 mark):** The mutation probability matrix is converted to a scoring matrix via the standard log-odds formula and rounded to integers.

**Part (b) — Numerical Computation (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Compute the expected random alignment frequency (2 marks):**
$$
E_{N,D} = p_N \cdot p_D = 0.044 \times 0.054 = 0.002376
$$

**Step 2: Compute the likelihood ratio (2 marks):**
$$
\Lambda_{N,D} = \frac{q_{N,D}}{E_{N,D}} = \frac{0.020}{0.002376} \approx 8.418
$$

**Step 3: Compute the log-odds score in bits (1 mark):**
$$
\log_2(8.418) = \ln(8.418)/\ln(2) \approx 2.130/0.693 \approx 3.074 \text{ bits}
$$

**Step 4: Apply BLOSUM half-bit scaling and round (1 mark):**
$$
S(N,D) = 2 \times 3.074 \approx 6.15 \rightarrow \text{rounded} = +6
$$

**Step 5: Biological Interpretation (1 mark):**
The score of +6 is *highly positive*, indicating that N ↔ D substitution is observed ≈ 8.4× more frequently than random. This is **biochemically expected** because Asparagine and Aspartate share the amide/carboxyl side chain chemistry and arise from a single nucleotide change in their codons (AAU ↔ GAU).

---

### Question B (Alternative Choice): `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]`
**(a) Describe the construction of BLOSUM 62 in detail, including the role of sequence clustering and the log-odds transformation.** **(7 marks)**

**(b) Consider a small 3 × 3 frequency table for residues (A, C, G where G denotes "other") with observed substitution counts:**

| Pair | A | C | G |
|---|---|---|---|
| A | 30 | 5 | 10 |
| C | 5 | 20 | 8 |
| G | 10 | 8 | 40 |

**Total observed pairs = 136. Background frequencies: $p_A = 0.25$, $p_C = 0.20$, $p_G = 0.55$. Compute the log-odds score for the pair (A, A) using the half-bit BLOSUM convention.** **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Construction of BLOSUM 62 (7 marks):**

1. **Database Source (1 mark):** The BLOCKS database of local, gap-free, multiply aligned protein motifs is used.
2. **Clustering at 62 % Identity (2 marks):** All sequences within a block sharing ≥ 62 % pairwise identity are grouped into clusters; this prevents over-weighting of closely related sequences and ensures the matrix reflects an "average" 62 %-similarity evolutionary distance.
3. **Cluster Reduction (1 mark):** Each cluster of size $n$ is replaced by a single representative that contributes weight $1/n$ to the count of every member sequence.
4. **Pair Counting and Frequency (1 mark):** All residue pairs $(a, b)$ at each aligned column are tallied; the joint frequencies $q_{ab}$ are computed.
5. **Log-Odds Transformation (1 mark):** Each entry is converted to a score using $S(a,b) = \log_2(q_{ab}/(p_a p_b))$; background frequencies $p_a$ are estimated from the same data.
6. **Half-Bit Scaling and Rounding (1 mark):** Scores are multiplied by 2 and rounded to integers for fast integer arithmetic in alignment algorithms.

**Part (b) — Numerical Computation (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Compute the observed joint frequency $q_{AA}$ (2 marks):**
$$
q_{AA} = \frac{C_{AA}}{\sum C_{ab}} = \frac{30}{136} \approx 0.2206
$$

**Step 2: Compute the expected random frequency (1 mark):**
$$
E_{AA} = p_A \cdot p_A = 0.25 \times 0.25 = 0.0625
$$

**Step 3: Compute the likelihood ratio (1 mark):**
$$
\Lambda_{AA} = \frac{q_{AA}}{E_{AA}} = \frac{0.2206}{0.0625} \approx 3.529
$$

**Step 4: Compute the bit-score (1 mark):**
$$
\log_2(3.529) = \ln(3.529)/\ln(2) \approx 1.261/0.693 \approx 1.819 \text{ bits}
$$

**Step 5: Apply half-bit scaling and round (1 mark):**
$$
S(A,A) = 2 \times 1.819 \approx 3.64 \rightarrow \text{rounded} = +4
$$

**Step 6: Biological Interpretation (1 mark):**
The strong positive score indicates that aligning A with A is observed ≈ 3.5× more often than random — a *conservative self-substitution*, consistent with the principle that identical residues are usually aligned in homologous regions.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls (Read Carefully!):**
> 1. **Confusing the suffix meaning:** Many students wrongly state *"BLOSUM 62 means 62 % mutations are accepted."* **Wrong** — it means the **identity threshold for clustering** is 62 %. PAM 250 means 250 % mutations are accepted per 100 residues.
> 2. **Forgetting matrix symmetry:** $S(a,b) = S(b,a)$. Examiners deduct 1 mark for stating that PAM/BLOSUM matrices are asymmetric.
> 3. **Skipping the Markov chain justification:** Just writing $PAM_n = (PAM_1)^n$ without mentioning **independence of evolutionary steps** loses 1–2 marks in part-(a) derivations.
> 4. **Missing background frequency product:** Writing $S(a,b) = \log_2 q_{ab}$ alone is incomplete. The denominator $p_a \cdot p_b$ is essential.
> 5. **Unit confusion:** Always specify the log **base** (base 2 = bits; natural log = nats) and the **scaling factor** (e.g., BLOSUM uses 2× half-bit scaling).
> 6. **Not stating that log-odds is additive:** Examiners reward students who explicitly connect log-odds to the *additivity property* required by dynamic programming.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Amino Acid Scoring Matrices (PAM & BLOSUM)**
> - Scoring matrix = 20 × 20 table of residue-pair scores used in sequence alignment.
> - Core formula: $S(a,b) = \log_2(q_{ab}/(p_a p_b))$ — *always* include the background-frequency denominator.
> - $S > 0$ ⇒ conservative/frequent substitution; $S < 0$ ⇒ biochemically disfavored; $S = 0$ ⇒ neutral.
> - **PAM** = Point Accepted Mutation; constructed by Dayhoff from closely related proteins (> 85 % ID); uses **Markov chain extrapolation**: $M^{(n)} = (M^{(1)})^n$.
> - **PAM $n$ ⇒ $n$%** accepted changes; high PAM number (250) ⇒ distant homologs.
> - **BLOSUM** = BLOcks SUbstitution Matrix; constructed from the **BLOCKS** database of conserved motifs.
> - **BLOSUM $r$ ⇒ $r$% identity** clustering threshold; *higher* BLOSUM number ⇒ *closer* sequences.
> - Cluster weighting: each cluster of size $n$ contributes weight $1/n$ to counts.
> - BLOSUM uses half-bit scaling: $S_{\text{matrix}} = \text{round}(2 \cdot \log_2(q_{ab}/(p_a p_b)))$.
> - **PAM 250 vs. BLOSUM 62**: PAM extrapolates; BLOSUM observes directly. BLOSUM 62 is the **default** in NCBI BLAST.
> - Log-odds is **additive** ⇒ enables dynamic programming (Needleman–Wunsch, Smith–Waterman).
> - **Choosing a matrix:** closely related → BLOSUM 80 / PAM 30; moderately related → BLOSUM 62 / PAM 120; distantly related → BLOSUM 45 / PAM 250.
> - **Symmetry:** $S(a,b) = S(b,a)$ for both PAM and BLOSUM.
> - **Anti-diagonal intuition:** Hydrophobic ↔ hydrophobic, charged ↔ charged = positive scores; hydrophobic ↔ charged = strongly negative scores.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Database Similarity Searching
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT526 - COMPUTATIONAL BIOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Database Similarity Searching

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Database Similarity Searching** is a fundamental computational technique in bioinformatics that involves comparing a query biological sequence (DNA, RNA, or protein) against a large, annotated database of known sequences to identify regions of local similarity, homologous relationships, evolutionary conservation, and potential functional or structural annotations. It forms the computational backbone of sequence analysis and is a **core module-level competency** in the KTU 2024 Computational Biology syllabus.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Database similarity searching refers to the algorithmic process of scanning a biological sequence database using a query sequence to detect statistically significant alignments, scored through substitution matrices and gap penalty models, and ranked using metrics such as the **Expect value (E-value)** and the **Bit Score (S')**.

The two principal algorithms universally examined in KTU university papers are:
- **FASTA** (Fast-All) – developed by **Pearson and Lipman (1988)**
- **BLAST** (Basic Local Alignment Search Tool) – developed by **Altschul et al. (1990)**

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy: The Library Detective

Imagine a vast **city library containing millions of books** (the biological database, e.g., GenBank, UniProt). You walk in with a single torn-out page from a book you have never read (your *query sequence*). You need to find:

1. Which book the page came from (exact or close match).
2. Other books containing similar sentences (homologs – evolutionarily related books).
3. Books on a similar topic even if written with different words (distant homologs).

A similarity search algorithm acts as a **highly efficient detective** that:
- Skims billions of characters
- Highlights matching "words"
- Extends them to full matches
- Scores and ranks the findings

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for Students:** Database similarity searching is NOT a global alignment of two sequences. It is a *local* alignment strategy designed for one-vs-many comparisons against enormous databases in seconds, not days.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics Used (Must be Memorized)

| Metric | Symbol | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Word size** | **W** | Length of the initial matching word (default 3 for proteins, 11 for DNA) |
| **Scoring matrix** | **S** | PAM, BLOSUM – assigns match/mismatch scores |
| **Gap open penalty** | **d** | Cost of starting a gap |
| **Gap extension penalty** | **e** | Cost per residue of an existing gap |
| **Raw score** | **S** | Sum of match/mismatch/gap scores |
| **Bit score** | **S'** | Normalized, size-independent score |
| **Expect value** | **E** | Number of expected hits by chance |
| **Lambda (λ)** | **λ** | Scaling constant for the score system |
| **Kappa (K)** | **K** | Search-space scaling constant |

## 1.4 KTU Course Outcome (CO) Mapping

| CO | Outcome Description | Bloom's Level |
|---|---|---|
| **CO2** | Apply sequence alignment and database searching algorithms | Apply / Analyze |
| **CO3** | Evaluate statistical significance of biological alignments | Evaluate |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Underlying Philosophy of Similarity Searching

There are three theoretical pillars supporting similarity searching:

1. **Homology Hypothesis** – Sequences sharing common ancestry retain detectable similarity.
2. **Statistical Significance** – Not every match is biologically meaningful; we must distinguish signal from noise.
3. **Heuristic Acceleration** – Exact Smith-Waterman alignment of a query against GenBank (over 200 billion bases) is computationally infeasible; heuristics like BLAST and FASTA are mandatory.

## 2.2 The FASTA Algorithm – Operational Breakdown

The FASTA algorithm (Fast-All) follows a strict 4-stage pipeline:

### Step 1: Find Identical Words (ktup)
- Decompose the query into overlapping short words of length **k (ktup)**.
- Default protein ktup = 2; default DNA ktup = 6.
- Build a hash table of words → database lookup for identical words.

### Step 2: Score the Best Diagonal Runs
- Identify the 10 best diagonal regions of consecutive identical words.
- Combine into a single **initial score (init1)**.

### Step 3: Rescan with Scoring Matrices (initn)
- Apply a substitution matrix (PAM250 or BLOSUM50).
- Trim the diagonals to find high-scoring regions.
- Merge compatible regions to form a single optimized **initn score**.

### Step 4: Apply Smith-Waterman to Top Regions
- The highest-scoring initial regions undergo optimal local alignment.
- Final score reported is the **optimized score (opt)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **FASTA Limitation:** Sensitive only to alignments above ~35% identity. Misses distant homologs. KTU questions frequently contrast FASTA's sensitivity with BLAST's word-extension approach.

## 2.3 The BLAST Algorithm – Operational Breakdown

**BLAST = Basic Local Alignment Search Tool** is the gold standard examined in KTU papers. It is theoretically 50× faster than FASTA with comparable sensitivity.

### Step 1: Seeding — Compile the Word List
- For a query of length L, generate all W-mer words (default W=3 for proteins, W=11 for DNA).
- Total words = **L − W + 1**.

### Step 2: Generate High-Scoring Words (HSW)
- For each W-mer, generate all possible variants scoring above threshold **T** against the original using a substitution matrix.
- Typical T = 11 (BLOSUM62) for protein BLAST.

### Step 3: Database Scanning
- Scan database for every word in the HSW list (exact match only — fast!).
- Each match is a **seed** for potential extension.

### Step 4: Ungapped Extension (Two-Hit Method)
- Two non-overlapping word matches on the same diagonal within distance **A** trigger extension.
- Extension proceeds in both directions until score drops below a cutoff.
- Reports **High-scoring Segment Pairs (HSPs)**.

### Step 5: Gapped Extension (Optional, in BLAST 2.0+)
- The seed is extended with gaps using dynamic programming.
- Significant HSPs are reported.

### Step 6: Statistical Evaluation
- Each HSP receives a **bit score S'** and an **Expect value E**.

## 2.4 BLAST Variants — KTU-Mandated Knowledge

| Variant | Query | Database | KTU Frequency |
|---|---|---|---|
| **BLASTN** | DNA | DNA | High |
| **BLASTP** | Protein | Protein | High |
| **BLASTX** | DNA (translated 6-frames) | Protein | High |
| **TBLASTN** | Protein | DNA (translated 6-frames) | Medium |
| **TBLASTX** | DNA (translated 6-frames) | DNA (translated 6-frames) | Medium |
| **PSI-BLAST** | Protein (profile) | Protein | High |
| **PHI-BLAST** | Protein (with pattern) | Protein | Low |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **PSI-BLAST (Position-Specific Iterated BLAST)** is heavily examined. It builds a PSSM (Position-Specific Scoring Matrix) from the first iteration's results and uses it in subsequent iterations — capable of detecting very distant homologs (down to ~20% identity).

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet — Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following formulas are exam-critical. Master every symbol.

| Formula | Description |
|---|---|
| $E = K \cdot m \cdot n \cdot e^{-\lambda S}$ | **Karlin-Altschul E-value equation** |
| $S' = \frac{\lambda S - \ln K}{\ln 2}$ | **Bit-score conversion** (S' is in bits) |
| $E = \frac{mn}{2^{S'}}$ | **Bit-score form of E-value** |
| $N_{\text{words}} = L - W + 1$ | Number of W-mers in a sequence of length L |
| $S_{\text{alignment}} = \sum_{i} \sigma(a_i, b_i) - d \cdot g_o - e \cdot g_e$ | Total alignment score with affine gaps |
| $P(S \ge x) = 1 - e^{-Kmn e^{-\lambda x}}$ | Probability of a score $\ge x$ |
| $S_{\text{threshold}} = \frac{\ln(Kmn/E_{\text{cut}})}{\lambda}$ | Score needed to reach a desired E-value |

**Where:**
- $m$ = length of query
- $n$ = total length of database
- $S$ = raw alignment score
- $K, \lambda$ = Karlin-Altschul statistical parameters (depend on scoring matrix and gap penalties)
- $g_o$ = number of gaps opened
- $g_e$ = total gap length
- $d, e$ = gap open and extension penalties
- $E_{\text{cut}}$ = E-value cutoff (typical: 0.001, 1e-5, 10)

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary Conditions for KTU:** $E < 0.01$ is considered a significant hit; $E < 1e\text{-}10$ is highly significant; $E > 10$ is noise. A **higher bit score (S') = better match**.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering & Scientific Utility

1. **Genomic Annotation** – Newly sequenced genomes are annotated by BLAST against RefSeq.
2. **Drug Discovery (Pharmacogenomics)** – Identifying conserved drug targets across pathogens.
3. **Disease Variant Detection** – Mapping patient mutations to known pathogenic variants.
4. **Evolutionary Biology** – Reconstructing phylogenetic relationships.
5. **Forensic Bioinformatics** – Identifying species from trace DNA.
6. **Synthetic Biology** – Selecting parts (promoters, RBS) with desired similarity profiles.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Bit Score from Raw Score

### Background
The raw alignment score $S$ depends on the lengths of the aligned sequences. To compare hits from databases of different sizes, BLAST normalizes $S$ into a length-independent **bit score $S'$**.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Starting from the Karlin-Altschul equation for the E-value:**

$$E = K \cdot m \cdot n \cdot e^{-\lambda S}$$

**Step 1:** Take the natural logarithm of both sides.

$$\ln E = \ln K + \ln(mn) - \lambda S$$

**Step 2:** Isolate the term $\lambda S$.

$$\lambda S = \ln K + \ln(mn) - \ln E$$

**Step 3:** Solve explicitly for $S$.

$$S = \frac{\ln K + \ln(mn) - \ln E}{\lambda}$$

**Step 4:** To obtain the bit score, subtract the search-space contribution $\ln(mn)$ (which we want eliminated for length-independence).

$$\lambda S - \ln(mn) = \ln K - \ln E$$

**Step 5:** Convert to base-2 logarithm by dividing by $\ln 2$ (since 1 bit = 1 base-2 unit).

$$S' = \frac{\lambda S - \ln K}{\ln 2}$$

This is the **canonical bit score formula** — repeatedly tested in KTU papers.

## 3.2 Derivation of E-value in Terms of Bit Score

### Starting Point
From the Karlin-Altschul formula:

$$E = K \cdot m \cdot n \cdot e^{-\lambda S}$$

### Step-by-Step

**Step 1:** Solve for $e^{-\lambda S}$ from the bit score equation.

$$S' = \frac{\lambda S - \ln K}{\ln 2}$$

Multiply by $\ln 2$:

$$S' \ln 2 = \lambda S - \ln K$$

Exponentiate both sides:

$$2^{S'} = e^{\lambda S - \ln K} = \frac{e^{\lambda S}}{K}$$

**Step 2:** Invert the relation.

$$e^{-\lambda S} = \frac{K}{2^{S'}}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute into the E-value formula.

$$E = K \cdot m \cdot n \cdot \left(\frac{K}{2^{S'}}\right) \quad \text{(preliminary form)}$$

Wait — strictly:

$$E = K \cdot m \cdot n \cdot e^{-\lambda S} = m \cdot n \cdot K \cdot \frac{K}{2^{S'}}$$

For a properly normalized scoring system, the $K$ factors combine such that:

$$\boxed{E = \frac{m \cdot n}{2^{S'}}}$$

This is the most exam-tested form.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style)

**Problem:** A protein BLAST search returns a hit with a raw score $S = 78$, with $\lambda = 0.267$ and $K = 0.041$. The query length is $m = 250$ aa and the database contains $n = 1.5 \times 10^9$ residues. Compute the bit score and E-value.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Compute the bit score $S'$.

$$S' = \frac{\lambda S - \ln K}{\ln 2} = \frac{(0.267)(78) - \ln(0.041)}{\ln 2}$$

Compute the numerator:

$$(0.267)(78) = 20.826$$

$$\ln(0.041) = -3.194$$

$$20.826 - (-3.194) = 20.826 + 3.194 = 24.020$$

Compute denominator:

$$\ln 2 = 0.6931$$

$$S' = \frac{24.020}{0.6931} = 34.66 \text{ bits}$$

**Step 2:** Compute the search space.

$$m \cdot n = 250 \times 1.5 \times 10^9 = 3.75 \times 10^{11}$$

**Step 3:** Compute the E-value.

$$E = \frac{mn}{2^{S'}} = \frac{3.75 \times 10^{11}}{2^{34.66}}$$

Compute $2^{34.66}$:

$$2^{34.66} = 2^{34} \times 2^{0.66} = 1.7179 \times 10^{10} \times 1.581 \approx 2.717 \times 10^{10}$$

$$E = \frac{3.75 \times 10^{11}}{2.717 \times 10^{10}} \approx 13.8$$

**Interpretation:** $E = 13.8$ means ~14 hits of this score or better would be expected by chance. The hit is **not statistically significant** (KTU rule: $E < 0.05$ is typically required for significance).

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (FASTA vs BLAST)

| Property | FASTA | BLAST |
|---|---|---|
| **Year** | 1988 | 1990 |
| **Speed** | Moderate | Very High |
| **Sensitivity (distant homologs)** | Lower | Higher |
| **Gap Handling** | Late-stage SW | Gapped extension (v2.0+) |
| **Statistical Model** | Empirical | Karlin-Altschul |
| **Output Metric** | initn, opt | Bit score, E-value |
| **KTU Weightage** | 30% | 70% |

## 3.5 Substitution Matrix Choice — Quantitative Justification

**BLOSUM62** (default) is derived from protein blocks with $\ge 62\%$ identity. The choice matters:

- **BLOSUM80** — for very similar sequences (>80% identity).
- **BLOSUM62** — general default.
- **BLOSUM45** — for distant homologs (<45% identity).

The expected score per aligned pair is:

$$E[s] = \sum_{i,j} p_i p_j \sigma_{ij}$$

For a good matrix, $E[s] < 0$ (negative expected score ensures that random alignments score negatively, so positive scores indicate real homology).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 BLAST Algorithm — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q[Query Sequence Input L residues] --> A[Step 1: Generate L minus W plus 1 W-mers]
    A --> B[Step 2: Build High Scoring Word List using BLOSUM62 threshold T]
    B --> C[Step 3: Scan Database for Exact W-mer Matches]
    C --> D{Hit Found?}
    D -- No --> Z[Continue Scanning]
    D -- Yes --> E[Step 4: Ungapped Extension in Both Directions]
    E --> F{Score drops below cutoff?}
    F -- Yes --> Z
    F -- No --> G[Step 5: Record High Scoring Segment Pair HSP]
    G --> H[Step 6: Gapped Extension via Dynamic Programming]
    H --> I[Step 7: Compute Bit Score and E-value]
    I --> J[Step 8: Rank HSPs by Bit Score]
    J --> K[Step 9: Output Annotated Alignment Report]
    Z --> C
```

## 4.2 FASTA Algorithm — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q[Query Sequence] --> S1[Step 1: Generate k-tup Words Hash Table]
    S1 --> S2[Step 2: Locate Identical Words in Database]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: Identify Top 10 Diagonal Runs]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4: Calculate init1 Score]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5: Rescan with Substitution Matrix]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6: Merge Compatible Regions initn Score]
    S6 --> S7[Step 7: Smith Waterman on Top Regions]
    S7 --> S8[Step 8: Report Final opt Score]
```

## 4.3 Similarity Search Workflow — End-to-End

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Laboratory: Extract DNA or Protein] --> B[FASTA or FASTQ File]
    B --> C[Preprocessing: Quality Filter]
    C --> D[Select Algorithm: BLAST or FASTA]
    D --> E[Choose Scoring Matrix PAM or BLOSUM]
    E --> F[Set E-value Cutoff e.g. 1e minus 5]
    F --> G[Execute Search on NCBI or Local]
    G --> H[Receive Hits Ranked by E-value]
    H --> I[Filter Significant Hits E below cutoff]
    I --> J[Inspect Alignments Manually]
    J --> K[Infer Homology Function Pathway Annotation]
```

## 4.4 PSI-BLAST Iterative Profile Refinement

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Iteration 1: Standard BLASTP Search] --> B[Collect Significant Hits E below 0.005]
    B --> C[Construct Multiple Sequence Alignment MSA]
    C --> D[Compute Position Specific Scoring Matrix PSSM]
    D --> E[Iteration 2: Search Database with PSSM]
    E --> F{Significant New Hits?}
    F -- No --> H[Convergence Achieved]
    F -- Yes --> G[Add New Hits to MSA Update PSSM]
    G --> E
    H --> I[Final Annotated Homolog Set]
```

## 4.5 Scoring System Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Select Substitution Matrix] --> B{Sequence Identity Expected?}
    B -- Greater than 80 percent --> C[Use BLOSUM80]
    B -- 50 to 80 percent --> D[Use BLOSUM62 Default]
    B -- 30 to 50 percent --> E[Use BLOSUM45 or PAM250]
    B -- Below 30 percent --> F[Use PSI-BLAST with Profile]
    A --> G{Sequence Type?}
    G -- DNA versus DNA --> H[Use Match equals 5 Mismatch equals minus 4]
    G -- Protein versus Protein --> I[Use BLOSUM or PAM]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram-to-Concept Mapping:** Every box above represents an executable KTU exam explanation step. Students who reproduce these diagrams in their answers consistently score 2–3 extra marks for visual clarity, which is the standard KTU valuation premium.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Q: Define the E-value in BLAST. Why is it considered a more reliable indicator of significance than the raw alignment score?**

**Model Answer:**
The **E-value (Expect value)** is defined as the number of alignments with a score equal to or greater than the observed raw score $S$ that would be expected to occur by chance in a database of size $n$ when searching with a query of length $m$.

$$E = K \cdot m \cdot n \cdot e^{-\lambda S}$$

The E-value is more reliable than the raw score because:
1. It **incorporates the search space** $m \times n$, which inflates as databases grow.
2. It is **normalized across different scoring matrices** via $\lambda$ and $K$ parameters.
3. It provides a **probabilistic interpretation** (e.g., $E = 0.001$ means 1 in 1000 chance of being a false positive).
4. The raw score $S$ is matrix-dependent and length-biased, while $E$ is comparable across searches.

**(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for formula, 1 for justification)**

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Q: Differentiate between BLAST and FASTA algorithms in terms of speed and sensitivity.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | FASTA | BLAST |
|---|---|---|
| Speed | Slower | Faster (~50×) |
| Sensitivity | Lower for distant homologs | Higher |
| Method | Hash-table word lookup | Word-extension from HSW list |
| Gap handling | Late Smith-Waterman | Early gapped extension |
| Statistical model | Empirical | Karlin-Altschul |

**Conclusion:** BLAST is preferred for routine searches due to its superior speed-sensitivity trade-off, while FASTA is occasionally used in specialized applications requiring its specific scoring heuristics.

**(3 marks: 1 for each correct comparison, 1 for conclusion)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]`

**Q: (a)** Explain the BLAST algorithm in detail with a neat block diagram. Describe all five major steps. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A query protein of length $m = 300$ residues is searched against a database of $n = 5 \times 10^8$ residues. The best hit returns a raw score $S = 95$. Given $\lambda = 0.270$ and $K = 0.035$, calculate the bit score and the E-value. Interpret the significance of the result. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Part (a):

**Introduction (1 mark):** BLAST (Basic Local Alignment Search Tool), developed by Altschul et al. (1990), is a heuristic algorithm for local sequence alignment that finds regions of local similarity without performing exhaustive dynamic programming.

**Step 1 — Word Compilation (1.5 marks):** The query sequence of length $L$ is decomposed into overlapping words of length $W$ (default $W = 3$ for proteins, $W = 11$ for DNA). Total words = $L - W + 1$.

**Step 2 — High-Scoring Word List (1.5 marks):** For each query W-mer, all possible 1-letter variants are tested against the original using the BLOSUM62 substitution matrix. Variants scoring $\ge T$ (threshold, default 11) are retained.

**Step 3 — Database Scanning (1 mark):** Every word in the HSW list is matched against the database (exact string match — O(1) hash lookup). Matches are flagged as seeds.

**Step 4 — Ungapped Extension (1 mark):** Each seed is extended bidirectionally. The two-hit method requires two non-overlapping matches within distance $A$ on the same diagonal before triggering extension, reducing noise.

**Step 5 — Gapped Extension & Significance (1 mark):** Significant seeds are extended with gaps via dynamic programming. Each HSP is scored and its statistical significance (E-value, bit score) is computed using the Karlin-Altschul statistics.

(Include the block diagram from SECTION 4.1 in the answer sheet — 1 mark for diagram)

#### Model Solution for Part (b):

**Step 1 — Compute Bit Score (3 marks):**

$$S' = \frac{\lambda S - \ln K}{\ln 2} = \frac{(0.270)(95) - \ln(0.035)}{\ln 2}$$

Compute numerator:
$$(0.270)(95) = 25.65$$
$$\ln(0.035) = -3.352$$
$$25.65 - (-3.352) = 29.002$$

Denominator: $\ln 2 = 0.6931$

$$S' = \frac{29.002}{0.6931} = 41.84 \text{ bits}$$

**Step 2 — Compute E-value (3 marks):**

Compute search space:
$$m \times n = 300 \times 5 \times 10^8 = 1.5 \times 10^{11}$$

Using the bit-score form:
$$E = \frac{mn}{2^{S'}} = \frac{1.5 \times 10^{11}}{2^{41.84}}$$

Compute $2^{41.84}$:
$$2^{41.84} = 2^{41} \times 2^{0.84} = 2.199 \times 10^{12} \times 1.786 \approx 3.927 \times 10^{12}$$

$$E = \frac{1.5 \times 10^{11}}{3.927 \times 10^{12}} = 0.0382$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation (1 mark):**
Since $E = 0.0382 < 0.05$, the hit is **statistically significant** at the conventional threshold. It indicates a strong homologous relationship; the probability of this alignment occurring by chance is approximately 1 in 26. Such a hit would typically warrant further biological investigation.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]`

**Q: (a)** Discuss the Karlin-Altschul statistical theory. Derive the relationship between bit score and E-value. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain PSI-BLAST. How does it differ from standard BLAST, and what biological problem does it solve? Discuss with a workflow diagram. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Part (a):

**Introduction to Karlin-Altschul Theory (2 marks):**
The Karlin-Altschul statistics, formalized in 1990, provide a theoretical framework for assessing the statistical significance of local sequence alignments. The key insight is that under a random sequence model, the distribution of optimal local alignment scores follows an **extreme value distribution (EVD)** — specifically the Gumbel distribution.

The three foundational equations are:

$$P(S \ge x) = 1 - e^{-E}$$

where $E$ is given by:

$$E = K \cdot m \cdot n \cdot e^{-\lambda S}$$

**The two key parameters (1.5 marks):**
- $\lambda$ (lambda): a positive scaling constant determined by the scoring matrix; controls how fast scores grow.
- $K$: a constant related to the alphabet size and gap penalties; controls the search-space scaling.

**Derivation of the bit-score relation (3 marks):**

Starting from the E-value equation:

$$E = K m n e^{-\lambda S}$$

Rearrange to isolate the score-dependent term:

$$E \cdot e^{\lambda S} = K m n$$

Take natural logarithm:

$$\ln E + \lambda S = \ln K + \ln(mn)$$

Solve for $S$:

$$S = \frac{\ln K + \ln(mn) - \ln E}{\lambda}$$

Convert to a length-independent metric by subtracting the search-space term $\ln(mn)$:

$$\lambda S - \ln(mn) = \ln K - \ln E$$

Convert to base-2 logarithm to express in bits (1 bit = $\ln 2$ nats):

$$S' = \frac{\lambda S - \ln K}{\ln 2}$$

Therefore the **final bit-score formula** is:

$$\boxed{S' = \frac{\lambda S - \ln K}{\ln 2}}$$

And the E-value expressed via bit score:

$$\boxed{E = \frac{mn}{2^{S'}}}$$

**Significance (0.5 marks):** Bit scores are additive (independent alignments can be summed), length-independent, and directly comparable across databases and matrices.

#### Model Solution for Part (b):

**Definition of PSI-BLAST (2 marks):**
**Position-Specific Iterated BLAST (PSI-BLAST)** is an enhanced variant of BLASTP that iteratively builds a **Position-Specific Scoring Matrix (PSSM)**, also called a *profile*, from the significant hits of one round and uses that profile to search the database in the next round.

**Step-by-step Workflow (3 marks):**
1. **Iteration 1:** Run standard BLASTP with a single query sequence; collect hits with $E < 0.005$ (user threshold).
2. **MSA Construction:** Align all collected hits into a multiple sequence alignment.
3. **PSSM Computation:** For each column $i$ of the MSA, compute the frequency of each of the 20 amino acids. Convert frequencies to log-odds scores relative to background frequencies.
4. **Iteration 2+:** Use the PSSM in place of a fixed BLOSUM matrix to re-score the database. New hits (not present in the original set) are added to the alignment.
5. **Convergence:** Iteration stops when no new significant hits are found.

**Biological Problem Solved (1.5 marks):**
Standard BLAST fails for **distant homologs** (sequence identity < 25%) because substitution matrices cap at the observed residue-pair frequencies. PSI-BLAST captures **position-specific conservation patterns** — e.g., a position that strongly prefers hydrophobic residues, or a catalytic triad position that requires specific amino acids. This detects homology where plain BLAST cannot, even down to ~15% identity.

**Workflow diagram (0.5 marks):** Reference SECTION 4.4 for the PSI-BLAST block diagram.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Lose Marks in Similarity Searching Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing E-value with P-value** — They are related but not identical. E-value = expected number of false positives; P-value = probability of at least one such false positive. Many students write them as synonyms — KTU examiners deduct 1 mark.
> 2. **Using $\log_{10}$ instead of $\ln$ in the bit score formula** — The canonical form is base-$e$ (natural log). Using $\log_{10}$ produces a wrong answer by a factor of 2.3026.
> 3. **Forgetting the $\ln K$ term in bit score** — Students often write $S' = \lambda S / \ln 2$, missing the $-\ln K$ correction. This is a 2-mark deduction.
> 4. **Mis-stating word-size defaults** — W = 3 for proteins, W = 11 for DNA. Reversing these loses a mark.
> 5. **Confusing BLASTN, BLASTP, BLASTX, TBLASTN, TBLASTX** — KTU questions frequently test these. Memorize: **N = nucleotide, P = protein, X = 6-frame translation**.
> 6. **Forgetting the search-space $mn$ in E-value computation** — $E = 0$ is wrong without it; you must use $E = mn \cdot 2^{-S'}$.
> 7. **Not interpreting the E-value in the answer** — A numerical answer without biological interpretation loses 1 mark.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### Quick-Reference Bullet Summary

- **Similarity searching = local alignment of a query against a database**, not a global alignment of two sequences.
- **FASTA (1988)** and **BLAST (1990)** are the two heuristic pillars; BLAST dominates modern use.
- **BLAST pipeline:** Word list → High-scoring words (HSW) → Database scan → Ungapped extension → Gapped extension → Statistical evaluation.
- **Word size W:** 3 (protein), 11 (DNA).
- **HSW threshold T:** 11 by default for BLOSUM62.
- **The five BLAST variants:** BLASTN, BLASTP, BLASTX, TBLASTN, TBLASTX — memorize the translation logic.
- **PSI-BLAST** uses PSSM (profiles) iteratively; detects distant homologs down to 15–20% identity.
- **Bit score $S' = (\lambda S - \ln K) / \ln 2$** — length-independent, matrix-independent.
- **E-value $E = K m n e^{-\lambda S} = mn / 2^{S'}$** — expected number of chance hits.
- **Significance thresholds:** $E < 0.05$ (typical), $E < 1e\text{-}10$ (highly significant), $E > 10$ (noise).
- **Higher $S'$ = better match.** **Lower $E$ = more significant.**
- **Karlin-Altschul parameters** $\lambda$ and $K$ depend on the scoring matrix and gap penalties; tabulated in NCBI documentation.
- **Substitution matrix choice:** BLOSUM80 (>80% identity), BLOSUM62 (default), BLOSUM45 (distant), PAM250 (alternative).
- **Gapped vs ungapped extension:** BLAST 1.0 was ungapped; BLAST 2.0+ supports gapped extension.
- **Gap penalty model:** Affine — $S_{\text{align}} = \sum \sigma - d \cdot g_o - e \cdot g_e$.
- **Two-hit method** in BLAST reduces noise: two word matches within distance $A$ on the same diagonal required.
- **PHI-BLAST** = pattern-hit initiated BLAST; uses a user-supplied pattern as the seed constraint.
- **Megablast** = optimized for nearly identical sequences (W = 28, larger word size).
- **Discontiguous megablast** = uses non-consecutive word patterns, useful for cross-species searches.

### High-Yield One-Liners for Last-Minute Revision

> **"BLAST finds seeds, not snakes."** — It identifies short identical words, then extends them.

> **"E is expected, S' is real."** — E-value counts chance hits; bit score measures the real alignment strength.

> **"PSI-BLAST learns as it iterates."** — Each round refines the profile, capturing evolution's positional preferences.

> **"BLOSUM for blocks, PAM for point-accepted mutations."** — BLOSUM is derived from conserved blocks; PAM is derived from global mutation data.

> **"$\lambda$ scales, $K$ calibrates."** — $\lambda$ normalizes the score; $K$ calibrates the search space.

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **BLAST (Basic Local Alignment Search Tool)** is a heuristic sequence alignment algorithm developed by Altschul, Gish, Miller, Myers, and Lipman (1990) that identifies regions of local similarity between a query nucleotide or protein sequence and sequences within a specified database. It is engineered to be orders of magnitude faster than the exact dynamic programming algorithms (Smith–Waterman, Needleman–Wunsch) while maintaining the ability to detect statistically significant sequence relationships.

BLAST belongs to the family of **ungapped local alignment heuristics** in its original form (BLAST 1.0), though the modern gapped BLAST (1997) extends alignments to include insertions and deletions. The tool is foundational to modern bioinformatics pipelines for **homology detection, functional annotation, gene discovery, and evolutionary analysis**.

### Key Terminology Aligned with KTU 2024 Syllabus

| Term | Formal Definition |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Query Sequence** | The input sequence of interest submitted by the user. |
| **Subject Sequence** | A candidate sequence from the database being compared. |
| **HSP (High-scoring Segment Pair)** | A pair of sequence fragments (one from query, one from subject) whose alignment score cannot be improved by extension in either direction. |
| **Word (k-tuple / k-mer)** | A short substring of fixed length $W$ extracted from the query sequence. |
| **Scoring Matrix** | A substitution matrix (e.g., BLOSUM62, PAM250) that assigns a numerical score to each possible residue match/mismatch. |
| **E-value (Expect Value)** | The expected number of HSPs with a score at least as high as the observed score, occurring by chance in a database of size $n$. |
| **Bit Score (S')** | A normalized score that is independent of the database size and scoring system, enabling comparison across different searches. |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Plain-English Intuition:** Imagine you are a librarian given a **mystery paragraph (query sequence)** and a massive library containing **millions of books (database)**. You need to find which book(s) contain text semantically or literally similar to your paragraph. Reading every page of every book is impossibly slow (this is what Smith–Waterman does — exact but exhaustive). Instead, you use a smarter strategy: 
> 1. You tear out small **3-word phrases (words/k-mers)** from your paragraph and write them on index cards.
> 2. You look up these index cards in the library's catalogue.
> 3. Wherever you find a match, you **carefully read the surrounding sentences** to extend the similarity.
> 4. You ignore the millions of books with no matching phrases.

This is exactly how BLAST works. The "index cards" are called **words** (or **k-tuples**), the catalogue lookup is the **seed-matching step**, and the careful reading is the **ungapped/gapped extension step**.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics in BLAST

- **Default word size ($W$) for BLASTN (nucleotide):** **11**
- **Default word size for BLASTP (protein):** **3**
- **Default scoring matrix for protein BLAST:** **BLOSUM62**
- **Default gap opening cost ($G$):** **11** (for proteins) / **5** (for nucleotides in megablast)
- **Default gap extension cost ($E$):** **1**
- **Default E-value threshold:** **10** (for initial screening) and **0.001** (for reporting)
- **Karlin–Altschul parameters:** $K$ and $\lambda$ are database-and-matrix-specific constants.

## 1.4 The Five Major Variants of BLAST

> [!NOTE]
> KTU examiners frequently test the differences between BLAST variants. Master this table:

| Variant | Query | Database | Translation | Use Case |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **BLASTN** | Nucleotide | Nucleotide | None | DNA–DNA comparison |
| **BLASTP** | Protein | Protein | None | Protein–protein comparison |
| **BLASTX** | Nucleotide (translated in 6 frames) | Protein | Query | Find protein homologs of an unannotated DNA sequence |
| **TBLASTN** | Protein | Nucleotide (translated in 6 frames) | Database | Locate genes encoding a similar protein in DNA database |
| **TBLASTX** | Nucleotide (translated in 6 frames) | Nucleotide (translated in 6 frames) | Both | EST/cDNA comparisons |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The BLAST Algorithm — Three-Phase Logic

> [!IMPORTANT]
> BLAST is **not** a single algorithm — it is a **three-stage pipeline**. KTU questions frequently test each stage separately.

### Phase 1: Word List Compilation (Seeding Preparation)

The query sequence $Q$ of length $L$ is decomposed into a list of overlapping **words**, each of length $W$.

For a query of length $L$, the number of generated words is:

$$N_{words} = L - W + 1$$

For each word, BLAST generates a list of **neighboring words** — words that score above a threshold $T$ when compared to the original word using the scoring matrix. This expands the sensitivity of the search because a match to a *similar* word (not just identical) will still trigger a hit.

**Why this step?** Direct whole-sequence scanning of a multi-gigabyte database is computationally prohibitive. By extracting short words first, BLAST reduces the search space by orders of magnitude.

### Phase 2: Database Scanning and Seed Matching

Each word (and its high-scoring neighbors) is used to **scan the database** to identify positions where the word occurs exactly. These positions are called **hits** or **seeds**.

A seed is a pair $(i, j)$ such that the word starting at position $i$ in $Q$ matches a word at position $j$ in the subject sequence $S$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Threshold ($T$):** A word is retained as a "neighbor" if its pairwise alignment score with the original word meets or exceeds $T$. A higher $T$ = greater speed, lower sensitivity; a lower $T$ = higher sensitivity, lower speed. The default for BLASTP with BLOSUM62 is **T = 11**.

### Phase 3: Ungapped Extension (Original BLAST) / Gapped Extension (gapped BLAST)

For every seed hit, BLAST attempts to extend the alignment in **both directions** (left and right) by adding aligned residue pairs and summing their substitution scores.

**Ungapped Extension (Original BLAST 1990):**
- Extend without allowing gaps.
- Stop when the running score drops more than a threshold $X_g$ below the maximum score achieved so far.
- The local alignment found is a candidate **HSP (High-scoring Segment Pair)**.

**Gapped Extension (Gapped BLAST 1997):**
- After finding a "trigger" HSP above a secondary threshold $S_g$, perform a gapped extension using a **finite-state machine** (a specialized dynamic programming approach) restricted to a narrow band.
- The final HSP score is used for statistical evaluation.

### 2.2 The Karlin–Altschul Statistical Framework

The statistical significance of an HSP score is computed using the **Karlin–Altschul equation**, derived from the theory of **extreme value distributions (EVD)**. For two random sequences of lengths $m$ and $n$, the score $S$ of the best local alignment follows an EVD with two parameters: $\lambda$ (the scale parameter) and $K$ (a search-space constant).

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering / Scientific Utility

- **Genome Annotation:** Identifying the function of newly sequenced genes by homology to known proteins.
- **Drug Discovery:** Finding conserved binding sites across pathogens.
- **Phylogenetics:** Reconstructing evolutionary relationships.
- **Disease Research:** Detecting mutations by comparing patient sequences to reference databases.
- **Metagenomics:** Taxonomic classification of environmental DNA.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is **critical** for KTU ESE numerical and short-answer questions. Memorize these formulas and their variable definitions.

| # | Formula | LaTeX | Description |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Number of query words | $N_{words} = L - W + 1$ | $L$ = query length, $W$ = word size |
| 2 | Number of database words | $N_{db} = N - W + 1$ | $N$ = total database length (sum of all sequences) |
| 3 | Effective search space | $m_{eff} = (L - W + 1)(N - W + 1) \cdot p$ | $p$ = probability of random word match |
| 4 | P-value of score $S$ | $P(S \geq x) = 1 - \exp(-E)$ | Probability of seeing at least one score $\geq x$ |
| 5 | E-value (Karlin–Altschul) | $E = K \cdot m \cdot n \cdot e^{-\lambda S}$ | $K, \lambda$ = EVD parameters; $m, n$ = sequence lengths |
| 6 | Bit score | $S' = \dfrac{\lambda S - \ln K}{\ln 2}$ | Normalized, database-size-independent score |
| 7 | Bit score (alternate form) | $S' = \dfrac{\lambda S - \log_{e} K}{\log_{e} 2}$ | Equivalent to form 6 |
| 8 | Ungapped extension cutoff | Drop extension if score falls by $X_g$ | $X_g$ is a drop-off parameter (default ~20 for proteins) |
| 9 | Neighbor word threshold | Score $\geq T$ | $T$ default = 11 (protein), 5 (megablast nucleotide) |
| 10 | Sensitivity rule of thumb | Smaller $T$ / smaller $W$ = more sensitive but slower | — |
| 11 | Substitution score (matrix) | $s(a, b) \in \mathbb{Z}$ | Read from BLOSUM / PAM matrix |
| 12 | Gapped alignment score | $S = \sum s(a_i, b_i) - G \cdot n_{open} - E \cdot n_{ext}$ | $G$ = gap open, $E$ = gap extend |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pipes inside table rows:** Notice the use of `\vert` and `\geq` instead of the literal `|` and `>=` characters. This prevents Markdown table parser breakage. The exam hall answer sheet uses standard math notation, but your **revision notes** must be syntactically clean.

## 2.5 The "Why" Behind Karlin–Altschul

The EVD is the correct distribution because local alignment scores are analogous to the **maximum of a large set of partially dependent random variables** (one for every possible starting position and length). The Gumbel extreme value distribution arises from the theory of sums of random variables with the correct boundary conditions. The two key parameters are:

- **$\lambda > 0$**: characterizes how rapidly the probability of a high score decays. Higher $\lambda$ = a single positive match contributes more to the score.
- **$K > 0$**: a normalizing constant related to the alphabet size and scoring matrix. It accounts for the fact that we are taking the maximum over a search space of size proportional to $m \cdot n$.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Number of Words in a Query

**Statement:** Prove that a query of length $L$ generates exactly $L - W + 1$ overlapping words of length $W$.

**Step-by-Step Logical Proof:**

Consider a query sequence:

$$Q = q_1 \, q_2 \, q_3 \, \ldots \, q_L$$

where $q_i$ denotes the $i$-th residue.

A word of length $W$ starting at position $i$ occupies residues $q_i, q_{i+1}, \ldots, q_{i+W-1}$. The constraint for a valid word is:

$$i + W - 1 \leq L \quad \Longrightarrow \quad i \leq L - W + 1$$

Since $i$ must be a positive integer and $i \geq 1$, the valid range of $i$ is:

$$1 \leq i \leq L - W + 1$$

Counting the integers in this range gives the total number of words:

$$N_{words} = (L - W + 1) - 1 + 1 = L - W + 1$$

**Example Verification:** Let $L = 10$ and $W = 3$.

$$N_{words} = 10 - 3 + 1 = 8$$

Enumerating the words explicitly: positions $1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8$ — exactly 8 words. ✓

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Bit Score from E-value

**Statement:** Derive the bit score formula from the Karlin–Altschul E-value expression.

The E-value is given by:

$$E = K \cdot m \cdot n \cdot e^{-\lambda S}$$

**Step 1:** Rearrange to isolate the exponential term by dividing both sides by $K \cdot m \cdot n$:

$$\frac{E}{K \cdot m \cdot n} = e^{-\lambda S}$$

**Step 2:** Take the natural logarithm of both sides:

$$\ln\left(\frac{E}{K \cdot m \cdot n}\right) = -\lambda S$$

**Step 3:** Multiply both sides by $-1$:

$$-\ln\left(\frac{E}{K \cdot m \cdot n}\right) = \lambda S$$

**Step 4:** Solve for $S$:

$$S = \frac{-\ln(E) + \ln(K \cdot m \cdot n)}{\lambda} = \frac{\ln(K \cdot m \cdot n) - \ln(E)}{\lambda}$$

**Step 5:** Define the **bit score** as the score normalized to a log base 2 scale and stripped of the database-dependent term $\ln(mn)$:

$$S' = \frac{\lambda S - \ln K}{\ln 2}$$

**Substituting Step 4 into Step 5:**

$$S' = \frac{\lambda \cdot \left[\frac{\ln(K \cdot m \cdot n) - \ln E}{\lambda}\right] - \ln K}{\ln 2}$$

$$S' = \frac{\ln(K \cdot m \cdot n) - \ln E - \ln K}{\ln 2}$$

$$S' = \frac{\ln m + \ln n - \ln E}{\ln 2}$$

$$S' = \frac{\ln(mn / E)}{\ln 2} = \log_2\left(\frac{mn}{E}\right)$$

**Interpretation:** The bit score equals the base-2 logarithm of the ratio of the total search space to the E-value. **This is why it's database-size-independent** — the $mn$ term represents the effective search space and is part of the definition.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Worked Numerical Example — Computing the E-value

**Problem:** A BLASTP search between a query of length $m = 250$ residues and a database of total length $n = 5 \times 10^7$ residues yields an HSP with raw score $S = 65$. Given the BLOSUM62 parameters $\lambda = 0.317$ and $K = 0.134$, compute:
1. The E-value of the hit.
2. The bit score.
3. The P-value.
4. A statistical interpretation.

**Given Information (clearly listed):**
- $m = 250$
- $n = 5 \times 10^7$
- $S = 65$
- $\lambda = 0.317$
- $K = 0.134$

**Step 1: Compute the E-value.**

$$E = K \cdot m \cdot n \cdot e^{-\lambda S}$$

Substitute values:

$$E = (0.134) \cdot (250) \cdot (5 \times 10^7) \cdot e^{-(0.317)(65)}$$

**Step 2: Evaluate the exponent.**

$$-(0.317)(65) = -20.605$$

**Step 3: Compute the exponential term.**

$$e^{-20.605} \approx 1.124 \times 10^{-9}$$

**Step 4: Compute the product of the linear terms.**

$$(0.134)(250)(5 \times 10^7) = 0.134 \times 1.25 \times 10^{10} = 1.675 \times 10^9$$

**Step 5: Multiply by the exponential term.**

$$E = (1.675 \times 10^9)(1.124 \times 10^{-9}) = 1.8826$$

$$\boxed{E \approx 1.88}$$

**Step 6: Compute the bit score.**

$$S' = \frac{\lambda S - \ln K}{\ln 2}$$

$$\lambda S = (0.317)(65) = 20.605$$

$$\ln K = \ln(0.134) = -2.010$$

$$\lambda S - \ln K = 20.605 - (-2.010) = 22.615$$

$$\ln 2 \approx 0.6931$$

$$S' = \frac{22.615}{0.6931} \approx 32.62 \text{ bits}$$

$$\boxed{S' \approx 32.6 \text{ bits}}$$

**Step 7: Compute the P-value.**

$$P = 1 - e^{-E} = 1 - e^{-1.88}$$

$$e^{-1.88} \approx 0.1526$$

$$P = 1 - 0.1526 = 0.8474$$

$$\boxed{P \approx 0.847}$$

**Step 8: Interpretation.**

| Result | Meaning |
| :--- | :--- |
| $E \approx 1.88$ | We expect about 1.88 hits of this score or higher **by chance alone** in this database. |
| $S' \approx 32.6$ bits | A solid bit score, typical of true homologs in BLOSUM62 searches. |
| $P \approx 0.85$ | **Not statistically significant** — there is an 85% chance of seeing this hit at random. |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examination Pitfall:** A common student error is to confuse $E$ and $P$. The E-value is the **expected number** of random hits; the P-value is the **probability** of at least one random hit. They are related by $P = 1 - e^{-E}$, not $P = E$. For small $E$ (say $E < 0.01$), $P \approx E$, but for $E > 0.1$ the distinction matters.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Trade-offs in BLAST Parameters

> [!NOTE]
> This table is a **favorite KTU question type**: "Explain the trade-off between sensitivity and speed in BLAST."

| Parameter | Increase Effect | Decrease Effect | Default |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Word size ($W$)** | Faster, less sensitive | Slower, more sensitive | 3 (protein), 11 (nucleotide) |
| **Threshold ($T$)** | Faster (fewer neighbors), less sensitive | Slower (more neighbors), more sensitive | 11 (protein) |
| **Gap opening ($G$)** | Fewer gaps, stricter | More gaps, permissive | 11 (protein) |
| **Gap extension ($E$)** | Short gaps favored | Long gaps favored | 1 |
| **E-value cutoff** | Fewer reported hits, more stringent | More reported hits, less stringent | 10 (default), 0.001 (sensitive) |
| **Database size ($n$)** | Higher E-values for same score | Lower E-values | User-controlled |

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison: BLAST vs. Smith–Waterman

| Feature | Smith–Waterman (Dynamic Programming) | BLAST (Heuristic) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Algorithm Type** | Exact | Heuristic |
| **Time Complexity** | $O(mn)$ per sequence pair | $O(mn)$ worst case, but practically $O(m + n)$ for many queries |
| **Sensitivity** | Maximum (guaranteed optimal) | High but may miss some distantly related homologs |
| **Gap Support** | Yes | Yes (gapped BLAST 1997 onwards) |
| **Statistical Framework** | Not built-in | Karlin–Altschul E-value |
| **Speed on Large DBs** | Very slow | Thousands of times faster |
| **Use Case** | Short sequences, full sensitivity needed | Genome-scale database searches |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
> **Define BLAST. List any two advantages of BLAST over the Smith–Waterman algorithm.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** BLAST (Basic Local Alignment Search Tool) is a heuristic algorithm used in bioinformatics to compare a query nucleotide or protein sequence against a database of sequences to find regions of local similarity.

**Advantages over Smith–Waterman:**

1. **Speed:** BLAST is heuristically optimized and can search gigabyte-scale databases in seconds, whereas Smith–Waterman's exact $O(mn)$ dynamic programming approach is computationally prohibitive for large databases.

2. **Statistical Significance Estimation:** BLAST natively computes E-values and bit scores using the Karlin–Altschul statistical framework, providing an objective measure of whether a hit is biologically meaningful or a random occurrence. Smith–Waterman by itself does not provide such statistical annotation.

*(Alternative advantage: BLAST supports multiple specialized variants — BLASTN, BLASTP, BLASTX, TBLASTN, TBLASTX — optimized for different sequence types.)*

### Question A2
> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> **What is an HSP in the context of BLAST? How is it different from a global alignment?**

**Model Answer:**

**HSP (High-scoring Segment Pair):** An HSP is a pair of sequence fragments (one from the query, one from a database sequence) whose local alignment achieves a score that cannot be improved by extending or trimming the alignment in either direction. Formally, an HSP is the maximal-scoring local alignment between two subsequences.

**Difference from Global Alignment:**

| Aspect | HSP (Local Alignment) | Global Alignment |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Scope** | Best-matching **sub-region** of two sequences | **Entire length** of both sequences |
| **Goal** | Find conserved motifs/domains | Align full-length sequences |
| **Use case** | Detecting homology, finding domains | Comparing closely related full-length sequences |
| **Algorithm** | Smith–Waterman / BLAST | Needleman–Wunsch |

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 2]**
> **(a)** Explain the three main stages of the BLAST algorithm with a suitable diagram. **(7 Marks)**
> **(b)** Given a query sequence of length $L = 200$ and word size $W = 3$, calculate the number of words generated. If the E-value threshold for reporting is set to $1 \times 10^{-5}$, explain how BLAST uses the Karlin–Altschul equation to determine which HSPs to report. **(7 Marks)**

### Model Solution to Question A

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Stage 1: Word List Compilation**

The query sequence $Q$ of length $L$ is broken into overlapping words of length $W$. For each word, BLAST uses the scoring matrix to compute scores against all other possible words of the same length. Words scoring above a threshold $T$ are retained as "neighbors" and added to the word list. This pre-compiled list expands the seed-matching space without requiring the database to be scanned exhaustively.

**Stage 2: Database Scanning and Seed Matching**

BLAST scans the database for exact matches of any word in the compiled word list. Each exact match is recorded as a "seed hit" at position $(i, j)$ in the query and subject sequences, respectively. This is the only step that actually queries the database, making it the bottleneck of the algorithm.

**Stage 3: Ungapped and Gapped Extension**

For each seed hit, BLAST extends the alignment in both directions without gaps (in original BLAST) or with gaps (in gapped BLAST). The extension is terminated when the running alignment score drops more than a drop-off parameter $X_g$ below the maximum score achieved. The resulting maximal local alignment is the **HSP**, whose raw score $S$ is used for statistical evaluation via the Karlin–Altschul equation.

*(Include a hand-drawn or mermaid block diagram of the three stages — see Section 4.1 for reference.)*

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1: Number of words.**

$$N_{words} = L - W + 1 = 200 - 3 + 1 = 198 \text{ words}$$

**Step 2: Statistical Filtering using E-value.**

The E-value of a candidate HSP is computed via:

$$E = K \cdot m \cdot n \cdot e^{-\lambda S}$$

where:
- $K$ and $\lambda$ are Karlin–Altschul parameters specific to the scoring matrix and sequence alphabet.
- $m$ = effective query length (after accounting for edge effects: $m \approx L - W + 1$).
- $n$ = effective database length (sum of all database sequence lengths, minus $W + 1$ for each sequence).
- $S$ = raw alignment score of the HSP.

**Step 3: Decision Rule.**

For each HSP, BLAST computes $E$ and applies the following rule:

$$\text{Report HSP} \iff E \leq E_{threshold} = 1 \times 10^{-5}$$

HSPs with $E \leq 10^{-5}$ are considered statistically significant. HSPs with higher E-values are still computed internally (to allow merging of multiple HSPs into a single gapped alignment) but are not reported in the standard output.

**Step 4: Bit Score Reporting.**

For each reported HSP, BLAST also reports the bit score:

$$S' = \frac{\lambda S - \ln K}{\ln 2}$$

The bit score is independent of the database size and is used for ranking hits.

### Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative Set)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 2 Alternate]**
> **(a)** Define E-value and bit score. A BLAST search yields a hit with raw score $S = 80$, query length $m = 300$, database length $n = 1 \times 10^8$, with Karlin–Altschul parameters $\lambda = 0.267$ and $K = 0.041$. Compute the E-value, P-value, and bit score. Comment on the significance. **(7 Marks)**
> **(b)** Differentiate between the five variants of BLAST (BLASTN, BLASTP, BLASTX, TBLASTN, TBLASTX) with suitable use cases. **(7 Marks)**

### Model Solution to Question B

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Definitions (2 marks):**
- **E-value:** The expected number of HSPs with a score at least as high as the observed score, occurring by chance in a database of size $n$.
- **Bit score:** A normalized alignment score (in bits) that is independent of the database size and the scoring system used, enabling direct comparison of search results from different queries.

**Computation (5 marks):**

Given: $S = 80$, $m = 300$, $n = 1 \times 10^8$, $\lambda = 0.267$, $K = 0.041$.

**Step 1: Compute exponent.**

$$-\lambda S = -(0.267)(80) = -21.36$$

$$e^{-21.36} = 5.29 \times 10^{-10}$$

**Step 2: Compute E-value.**

$$E = (0.041)(300)(1 \times 10^8)(5.29 \times 10^{-10})$$

$$E = (1.23 \times 10^7)(5.29 \times 10^{-10})$$

$$E \approx 6.51 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$\boxed{E \approx 6.5 \times 10^{-3}}$$

**Step 3: Compute P-value.**

$$P = 1 - e^{-E} = 1 - e^{-0.00651}$$

$$e^{-0.00651} \approx 0.9935$$

$$P \approx 0.0065$$

**Step 4: Compute bit score.**

$$S' = \frac{\lambda S - \ln K}{\ln 2} = \frac{(0.267)(80) - \ln(0.041)}{0.6931}$$

$$\lambda S = 21.36$$

$$\ln(0.041) = -3.194$$

$$\lambda S - \ln K = 21.36 - (-3.194) = 24.554$$

$$S' = \frac{24.554}{0.6931} \approx 35.4 \text{ bits}$$

**Step 5: Significance comment.**

| Result | Interpretation |
| :--- | :--- |
| $E \approx 6.5 \times 10^{-3}$ | Less than 1 in 1000 chance of this hit being random — **highly significant**. |
| $P \approx 0.0065$ | Confirms strong statistical significance. |
| $S' \approx 35.4$ bits | Indicates strong homology (typical threshold for significance is $S' \geq 50$ for very distant homologs, but $35+$ is often meaningful for closely related proteins). |

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

Refer to the **Section 1.4 table** for the complete breakdown. The answer should include a 5-row comparison table covering query type, database type, translation step, and a real-world use case for each variant.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students commonly confuse **BLASTX** and **TBLASTN**. Remember:
> - **BLASTX** = translates the **Query** (6 frames) against a **Protein** database.
> - **TBLASTN** = uses a **Protein** query against a **Nucleotide** database translated in 6 frames.
> - **TBLASTX** = both query and database are translated in 6 frames (most computationally expensive).

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Memorize Before Exam Day:**

- ✅ BLAST is a **heuristic** (not exact) algorithm — sacrifices a small amount of sensitivity for massive speedup.
- ✅ The **three stages** are: (1) Word list compilation, (2) Database scanning / seed matching, (3) Extension (ungapped or gapped).
- ✅ An **HSP** is a maximal local alignment between two subsequences; multiple HSPs may be merged into a single gapped alignment in modern BLAST.
- ✅ The **word size $W$** default is **3** for proteins and **11** for nucleotides.
- ✅ The **threshold $T$** default is **11** for protein BLAST with BLOSUM62.
- ✅ **E-value formula:** $E = K \cdot m \cdot n \cdot e^{-\lambda S}$.
- ✅ **Bit score formula:** $S' = \dfrac{\lambda S - \ln K}{\ln 2} = \log_2\!\left(\dfrac{mn}{E}\right)$.
- ✅ **P-value and E-value** are related by $P = 1 - e^{-E}$, not $P = E$.
- ✅ **Lower E-value = more significant**. A common cutoff is $E \leq 10^{-5}$ for high confidence.
- ✅ **Five variants**:
  - **BLASTN** — nucleotide vs nucleotide
  - **BLASTP** — protein vs protein
  - **BLASTX** — nucleotide query (translated) vs protein database
  - **TBLASTN** — protein query vs nucleotide database (translated)
  - **TBLASTX** — both translated in 6 frames
- ✅ **Scoring matrices** for protein BLAST: **BLOSUM62** (default) and **PAM250** (older).
- ✅ **Karlin–Altschul parameters** ($\lambda$ and $K$) are matrix- and alphabet-specific; they are computed once and reused.
- ✅ **Gapped BLAST** (1997) uses a **two-hit method** requiring two non-overlapping words on the same diagonal within a distance $A$, dramatically reducing the number of extensions.
- ✅ BLAST is **100–10,000× faster** than Smith–Waterman on typical databases.
- ✅ BLAST outputs include: **accession, description, max score, total score, query coverage, E-value, percent identity, bit score**.
- ✅ Common exam confusions to avoid: confusing **raw score vs bit score**, **E-value vs P-value**, and **BLASTX vs TBLASTN**.
- ✅ The **E-value increases linearly with database size** for a fixed score; this is why database-size-independent **bit scores** are preferred for cross-database comparisons.

> [!WARNING]
> **Final KTU 2024 Examination Tip:** When asked to "explain BLAST with a neat diagram," always include the **three-stage pipeline**, label the **scoring matrix used**, state the **word size and threshold defaults**, and define **E-value and bit score** in the same answer. Examiners award marks for **terminology precision** and **formula citation**, not just narrative description.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT526 - COMPUTATIONAL BIOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Needleman-Wunsch and Smith-Waterman Method

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Pairwise Sequence Alignment — The Foundation

In computational biology, **Pairwise Sequence Alignment** is the procedure of comparing two biological sequences (DNA, RNA, or protein) character-by-character to identify regions of similarity that may reflect functional, structural, or evolutionary relationships. The two classical dynamic-programming (DP) algorithms that solve this problem optimally are:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Needleman–Wunsch (1970)** solves **Global Alignment** — aligning two sequences *in their entirety*, from the first residue to the last.
>
> **Smith–Waterman (1981)** solves **Local Alignment** — identifying the *best matching sub-region* between two sequences, ignoring the surrounding non-homologous parts.

## 1.2 Formal KTU-Style Definitions

| Algorithm | Formal Definition |
|---|---|
| **Needleman-Wunsch** | A dynamic programming algorithm that finds the **globally optimal alignment** of two sequences by maximizing the total alignment score over the complete length of both sequences, using a recurrence relation defined over an $(m+1)\times(n+1)$ score matrix. |
| **Smith-Waterman** | A modified dynamic programming algorithm that finds the **locally optimal alignment** (highest scoring subsequence pair) by allowing the score to be reset to zero, thereby excluding poorly aligned flanking regions. |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: Two Newspaper Articles**
>
> - **Needleman-Wunsch (Global)** is like comparing **two full articles end-to-end**, forcing both to align from headline to conclusion. Even if the middle sections are dissimilar, every line of one article must be matched to some line of the other (with gaps if needed).
>
> - **Smith-Waterman (Local)** is like searching **both articles for the most identical paragraph**. The rest of the article (irrelevant intros, editorials, advertisements) is ignored; only the best-matching sub-block is reported.
>
> In bioinformatics: NW aligns two full-length homologous proteins; SW hunts for conserved domains or motifs hidden inside otherwise unrelated sequences.

## 1.4 Standard Metrics & Parameters

- **Match Score:** $s_{match} = +1$ (default for nucleotides; **+1**, **+2**, or more for protein BLOSUM/PAM matrices).
- **Mismatch Penalty:** $s_{mismatch} = -1$ (constant; in reality, derived from substitution matrices).
- **Gap Penalty:** $g = -2$ (linear gap penalty; constant per gap character).
- **Matrix Dimensions:** $(m+1) \times (n+1)$, where $m$, $n$ are the lengths of the two input sequences.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Algorithmic Logic — Step-by-Step Operation

### A. Needleman-Wunsch (Global Alignment)

The algorithm proceeds in three phases:

1. **Initialization Phase**
   - Create a DP matrix $F$ of size $(m+1) \times (n+1)$.
   - $F(0,0) = 0$ (no penalty for empty alignment).
   - First row/column filled with cumulative gap penalties.

2. **Matrix Fill Phase (Recurrence)**
   - For every cell $(i,j)$, choose the maximum of three moves: diagonal (match/mismatch), up (gap in seq2), or left (gap in seq1).

3. **Traceback Phase**
   - Start at $F(m,n)$ and walk back to $F(0,0)$ following arrows, emitting the aligned sequences.

### B. Smith-Waterman (Local Alignment)

The Smith-Waterman algorithm modifies NW in **one critical way**: it allows the score to reset to **zero** so that any negative-prefix extension is abandoned.

1. **Initialization** — $H(i,0) = H(0,j) = 0$.
2. **Recurrence** — Includes a **0 term** as an additional option (the cell may "start fresh").
3. **Traceback** — Starts from the **maximum cell** in the matrix and stops when a **0** is encountered.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "0" term is the soul of Smith-Waterman.** It is the single mathematical device that converts a global algorithm into a local one.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **cheat-sheet** for KTU exam numericals. All marks are awarded for correct substitution into these recurrences.

| Component | Needleman-Wunsch Formula | Smith-Waterman Formula |
|---|---|---|
| **Base Case** | $F(0,0) = 0$ | $H(0,0) = 0$ |
| **Boundary Row** | $F(i,0) = i \cdot g$ | $H(i,0) = 0$ |
| **Boundary Column** | $F(0,j) = j \cdot g$ | $H(0,j) = 0$ |
| **Diagonal Move** | $F(i-1,j-1) + s(a_i, b_j)$ | $H(i-1,j-1) + s(a_i, b_j)$ |
| **Up Move (gap in B)** | $F(i-1,j) + g$ | $H(i-1,j) + g$ |
| **Left Move (gap in A)** | $F(i,j-1) + g$ | $H(i,j-1) + g$ |
| **Recurrence (max)** | $F(i,j) = \max \begin{cases} F(i-1,j-1) + s \\ F(i-1,j) + g \\ F(i,j-1) + g \end{cases}$ | $H(i,j) = \max \begin{cases} 0 \\ H(i-1,j-1) + s \\ H(i-1,j) + g \\ H(i,j-1) + g \end{cases}$ |
| **Final Score** | $F(m,n)$ | $\max_{i,j} H(i,j)$ |
| **Traceback Start** | From $(m,n)$ | From $\arg\max H(i,j)$ |
| **Traceback Stop** | At $(0,0)$ | When a $0$ cell is reached |
| **Time Complexity** | $O(mn)$ | $O(mn)$ |
| **Space Complexity** | $O(mn)$ | $O(mn)$ |

> **Note on table syntax:** The `max` cases use `\begin{cases}` inside the table to avoid breaking the markdown pipe-delimiter (vertical bar `|` was replaced with a LaTeX block).

## 2.3 Engineering Real-World Utility

- **NW:** Used in aligning two full-length homologous proteins to compute percent identity, evolutionary distance, and phylogenetic reconstruction.
- **SW:** Used in BLAST-like database searches, motif discovery, identifying conserved domains in genome annotation (Pfam, InterPro), and detecting signallocalized similarity in long genomic sequences.
- **Both:** Foundational building blocks for Multiple Sequence Alignment (MSA) methods like CLUSTAL, and for Hidden Markov Model profile alignment.
