# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]**

**Q: Define RNA-Seq. List any four advantages it has over DNA microarrays for transcriptome analysis.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (1.5 marks):**
RNA-Seq (RNA Sequencing) is a high-throughput, NGS-based technique that sequences cDNA derived from RNA molecules in a sample, enabling **quantitative and qualitative analysis of the entire transcriptome** at single-base resolution.

**Four Advantages over Microarrays (0.375 × 4 = 1.5 marks):**
1. **Broader dynamic range** — RNA-Seq detects expression over **>5 orders of magnitude**, vs. ~2–3 orders for microarrays.
2. **No prior probe design required** — RNA-Seq can discover **novel transcripts, gene fusions, and splice isoforms**; microarrays are limited to pre-designed probes.
3. **Lower background noise** — digital counting vs. hybridization cross-reactivity.
4. **Higher reproducibility and base-level resolution** — detects SNPs, allele-specific expression, and RNA editing events.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**

**Q: Compare RPKM, FPKM, and TPM normalization metrics. State which is preferred for cross-sample comparison and why.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | RPKM | FPKM | TPM |
|---|---|---|---|
| Count unit | Reads | Fragments | Reads (rescaled) |
| Length normalized | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| Depth normalized | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| Sum per sample | Not constant | Not constant | **Exactly $10^6$** |
| Order | Length → Depth | Length → Depth | **RPK → Rescale** |

**Preference (1 mark):**
**TPM is preferred for cross-sample comparison** because:
- The sum of TPM is invariant (always $10^6$ per sample), making samples directly comparable.
- It length-normalizes **first**, then rescales by total RPK — eliminating sample-dependency artifacts of RPKM/FPKM.
- Used by modern alignment-free tools like **Salmon, kallisto, and RSEM**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark-Deduction Mistakes:**
> 1. **Confusing RPKM with TPM** — students frequently forget that TPM normalizes by **total RPK** first, not by total reads. This is a 3–4 mark deduction.
> 2. **Omitting the $10^9$ factor** in RPKM/FPKM — losing 1 mark per formula.
> 3. **Drawing RPKM and TPM flowcharts in the wrong order** — examiners explicitly test whether you understand the sequence.
> 4. **Writing "RNA-Seq" without mentioning the role of reverse transcriptase** — mandatory wet-lab step worth 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Skipping the "Why"** behind mRNA enrichment (rRNA is 80% of total RNA) — examiners award process-understanding marks.
> 6. **Not stating the "sums to $10^6$" property of TPM** — this is a 2-mark favorite question.

---

### ⭐ Question A — Option 1 (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Q: With a neat block diagram, describe the step-by-step workflow of an RNA-Seq library preparation. Mention the role of reverse transcriptase and poly-A selection.**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — RNA Extraction (0.5 mark):**
Total RNA is extracted from cells/tissue using **TRIzol** (guanidinium thiocyanate-phenol-chloroform) or column-based kits. Quality is checked on **Bioanalyzer** (RIN > 7).

**Step 2 — mRNA Enrichment (1.5 marks):**
Two strategies exist:
- **Poly-A selection:** Oligo-dT magnetic beads bind the 3' poly-A tail of eukaryotic mRNA. Other RNAs (rRNA, tRNA, most lncRNA without poly-A) are washed away.
- **rRNA Depletion:** DNA probes hybridize to rRNA, which is then enzymatically digested. Captures non-polyadenylated species (important for **prokaryotes** and **lncRNA** studies).

> **Role of poly-A selection:** rRNA constitutes **>80%** of total RNA; sequencing it would waste >80% of reads. Poly-A selection enriches the **mRNA fraction** so sequencing capacity is used efficiently.

**Step 3 — RNA Fragmentation (0.5 mark):**
RNA is fragmented to **200–500 bp** using **divalent cations (Mg²⁺)** at elevated temperature (94°C) or via enzymatic (RNase III) cleavage.

**Step 4 — First-Strand cDNA Synthesis (1.5 marks):**
**Reverse transcriptase** (an RNA-dependent DNA polymerase from retroviruses like M-MLV or AMV) synthesizes the first cDNA strand using **random hexamers** (or oligo-dT) as primers. This step **converts RNA → cDNA** because sequencing platforms read DNA, not RNA.

**Step 5 — Second-Strand Synthesis (0.5 mark):**
DNA Polymerase I, RNase H, and DNA Ligase convert the cDNA:RNA hybrid into **double-stranded cDNA (ds-cDNA)**. RNase H nicks the RNA template, and DNA Pol I fills in the gaps.

**Step 6 — End Repair & dA-Tailing (0.5 mark):**
T4 DNA Polymerase fills in 5' overhangs and chews back 3' overhangs → blunt ends. Klenow fragment adds a single **dA** to the 3' end → creates A-overhang for directional ligation.

**Step 7 — Adapter Ligation (0.5 mark):**
**Y-shaped Illumina adapters** are ligated to both ends. The Y-shape prevents adapter-dimer formation and enables **strand-specific** sequencing.

**Step 8 — Size Selection (0.5 mark):**
**AMPure XP beads** (or gel extraction) select fragments in the **300–500 bp** window, removing primer-dimers and oversized fragments.

**Step 9 — PCR Amplification & Indexing (0.5 mark):**
8–12 PCR cycles enrich properly adapted fragments and add **i7/i5 indices** (barcodes) for **multiplexing** multiple samples in one lane.

**Step 10 — Quality Control (0.5 mark):**
Final library validated on **Bioanalyzer** (size distribution) and **Qubit** (concentration). Ready for cluster generation.

**Block Diagram (1 mark):** [Draw the workflow from Section 4.1]

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Q: Explain the RNA-Seq data analysis pipeline from raw reads to differential expression analysis, listing the key tools used at each step.**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Quality Control (1 mark):**
**FastQC** generates per-base quality scores, GC content, adapter contamination, and sequence duplication levels. **MultiQC** aggregates results across samples.

**Step 2 — Trimming & Filtering (1 mark):**
**Trimmomatic / Cutadapt / fastp** removes:
- Illumina adapter sequences
- Bases with Phred quality Q < 20 (1 in 100 error rate)
- Reads shorter than ~36 bp

**Step 3 — Alignment to Reference Genome (1.5 marks):**
- **STAR** (Spliced Transcripts Alignment to a Reference) — ultrafast, uses unscaffolded annotations; default for human RNA-Seq.
- **HISAT2** — memory-efficient, uses a graph-based indexing strategy (Hierarchical Graph FM-index).
- Output is a **BAM (Binary Alignment Map)** file containing mapped reads with CIGAR strings for splice junctions.

**Step 4 — Quantification (1.5 marks):**
- **featureCounts** / **HTSeq-count** — count reads overlapping each gene (intersection with GTF annotation).
- **Salmon / kallisto** — alignment-free pseudoalignment using k-mers; much faster, less disk I/O.
- Output: a **count matrix** of size `(genes × samples)`.

**Step 5 — Normalization (1 mark):**
- **TPM** for visualization, heatmaps, PCA.
- **DESeq2 median-of-ratios** size factors for differential expression input.
- **edgeR TMM** (Trimmed Mean of M-values) alternative.

**Step 6 — Differential Expression Analysis (1 mark):**
- **DESeq2** — fits a **negative binomial generalized linear model**; uses shrinkage estimation for fold changes; reports log2FoldChange, p-value, adjusted p-value (FDR via Benjamini–Hochberg).
- **edgeR** — uses empirical Bayes estimation of dispersions; similar statistical framework.
- **limma-voom** — applies precision weights to linear models; preferred for small sample sizes.

**Threshold criteria:** $\vert \log_2 FC \vert \geq 1$ **AND** adjusted p-value (FDR) < 0.05.

---

### ⭐ Question B — Option 2 (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Q: With suitable block diagrams, describe the architecture of an RNA-Seq experiment, distinguishing between experimental design, sequencing chemistry, and computational workflow.**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Block 1 — Experimental Design (2 marks):**
- **Replicates:** ≥ 3 biological replicates per condition (mandatory for statistical power).
- **Conditions:** e.g., tumor vs. normal, treated vs. control, time points.
- **Library type:** **Strand-specific (dUTP method)** vs. unstranded. Strand-specificity preserves transcript orientation — critical for antisense RNA and overlapping gene studies.
- **Sequencing depth:** **20–30 M reads** for standard gene-level quantification, **>100 M** for splice isoform / low-expression detection.

**Block 2 — Sequencing Chemistry (2 marks):**
- **Illumina (Sequencing-by-Synthesis):** Most common. Bridge amplification creates clonal clusters on a flow cell. Four reversible-terminator fluorescent nucleotides (ddNTPs) are imaged each cycle.
- **Long-read alternatives:** **PacBio Iso-Seq** (circular consensus sequencing, up to 15 kb reads) and **Oxford Nanopore** (single-molecule electrical signal detection, ultra-long reads >1 Mb) capture **full-length isoforms** without assembly.

**Block 3 — Computational Workflow (3 marks):**
Same as Question A(b) — [Quality Control → Trimming → Alignment/Assembly → Quantification → Normalization → Differential Expression → Functional Enrichment].

**Block Diagram (1 mark):** [Reference the Mermaid diagrams in Section 4.1 and 4.2]

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Q: Derive the mathematical relationship between RPKM and TPM. Show with a worked example that TPM is invariant to gene-set size.**

**Model Answer (7 marks):**

**Derivation (4 marks):**

Starting from definitions:

$$
RPKM_i = \frac{RC_i \times 10^9}{L_i \times N}
$$

$$
TPM_i = \frac{RPK_i \times 10^6}{\sum_{j=1}^{G} RPK_j} = \frac{RC_i \times 10^6}{L_i \times \sum_j RPK_j}
$$

**Substitute** $RPKM_i$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
TPM_i &= \frac{RPK_i \times 10^6}{\sum_j RPK_j} \\
&= \frac{(RPKM_i \times N / 10^9) \times 10^6}{\sum_j (RPKM_j \times N / 10^9)} \\
&= \frac{RPKM_i \times 10^{-3} \times N}{N \times 10^{-3} \times \sum_j RPKM_j} \\
&= \frac{RPKM_i \times 10^6}{\sum_j RPKM_j}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final identity:**

$$
\boxed{\,TPM_i = RPKM_i \times \frac{10^6}{\sum_{j} RPKM_j} = FPKM_i \times \frac{10^6}{\sum_j FPKM_j}\,}
$$

> This is a **simple rescaling**: TPM and RPKM (or FPKM) carry **identical relative information**; only the normalization order and the constant scaling differ.

**Worked Numerical Proof (3 marks):**

Consider the example from Section 3.4. Computed RPKM values:

- $RPKM_{GAPDH} = 333{,}333$
- $RPKM_{TP53} = 111{,}111$
- $RPKM_{MYC} = 83{,}333$
- **Sum** = $527{,}777$

Convert to TPM:

$$
TPM_{GAPDH} = 333{,}333 \times \frac{10^6}{527{,}777} = 631{,}579
$$

$$
TPM_{TP53} = 111{,}111 \times \frac{10^6}{527{,}777} = 210{,}526
$$

$$
TPM_{MYC} = 83{,}333 \times \frac{10^6}{527{,}777} = 157{,}895
$$

**Sum** = $1{,}000{,}000$ ✓

**Now demonstrate invariance:** if we **add a new gene** (say, *ACTB*, length 1,800 bp, RC = 6,000) and recompute:

| Gene | RC | Length | RPK | TPM |
|------|------|--------|-----|------|
| GAPDH | 30,000 | 1,500 | 20,000 | 593,028 |
| TP53 | 20,000 | 3,000 | 6,667 | 197,676 |
| MYC | 10,000 | 2,000 | 5,000 | 148,257 |
| ACTB | 6,000 | 1,800 | 3,333 | 98,838 |
| **Sum** | | | **35,000** | **1,000,000** ✓ |

**Observation:**
- RPKM values for GAPDH, TP53, MYC **changed** (because $N$ and the RPK denominator changed).
- TPM sum **remained exactly $10^6$** — demonstrating invariance to gene-set size.
- TPM ratios between genes are more stable: $TPM_{GAPDH} / TPM_{TP53} \approx 3$ regardless of added genes.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Rapid-Revision Checklist – RNA-Seq**

### Core Concepts
- ✅ RNA-Seq is **NGS-based transcriptomics** that sequences cDNA derived from total/mRNA.
- ✅ It replaced microarrays due to **wide dynamic range, no probe-design bias, and novel-transcript discovery**.
- ✅ Library prep involves: **RNA extraction → mRNA enrichment → fragmentation → reverse transcription → adapter ligation → PCR**.
- ✅ **Reverse transcriptase** is the key enzyme that converts RNA → cDNA.

### Computational Pipeline
- ✅ Quality control: **FastQC / MultiQC**.
- ✅ Trimming: **Trimmomatic / Cutadapt**.
- ✅ Alignment: **STAR (fast) / HISAT2 (memory-efficient)**.
- ✅ Counting: **featureCounts / HTSeq-count / Salmon / kallisto**.
- ✅ DE tools: **DESeq2 / edgeR / limma-voom** (all assume **negative binomial** or weighted-normal distribution).

### Normalization Formulas (MUST memorize)
- ✅ $RPKM_i = \dfrac{RC_i \times 10^9}{L_i \times N}$
- ✅ $FPKM_i = \dfrac{F_i \times 10^9}{L_i \times N}$ (paired-end)
- ✅ $TPM_i = \dfrac{RPK_i \times 10^6}{\sum_j RPK_j}$ → **sums to $10^6$ per sample**
- ✅ $TPM_i = FPKM_i \times \dfrac{10^6}{\sum_j FPKM_j}$

### Key Differences
- ✅ **RPKM/FPKM** = Length → Depth normalization (sum varies).
- ✅ **TPM** = Length → Rescale (sum constant = $10^6$).
- ✅ **TPM preferred for cross-sample comparison**.

### Threshold for Differential Expression
- ✅ $\vert \log_2 FC \vert \geq 1$ **AND** adjusted p-value (FDR) < 0.05.

### Platforms
- ✅ **Illumina** = short-read, gold standard for quantification.
- ✅ **PacBio / Oxford Nanopore** = long-read, full-length isoform sequencing.

### Common Pitfalls
- ⚠️ Don't confuse **RPKM** with **TPM** — the order of operations differs.
- ⚠️ Don't forget the **$10^9$ factor** in RPKM/FPKM.
- ⚠️ Don't claim microarrays and RNA-Seq are equivalent — RNA-Seq is strictly superior in dynamic range, reproducibility, and discovery power.
- ⚠️ Don't write "RNA-Seq = DNA-Seq" — the reverse transcription step is the defining feature.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

**Chromatin Immunoprecipitation followed by Sequencing (ChIP-Seq)** is a high-throughput next-generation sequencing (NGS) technique used to identify the **genome-wide binding sites of DNA-associated proteins**, such as transcription factors, histone modifications, or chromatin-modifying enzymes. The method captures protein–DNA interactions in vivo by cross-linking proteins to DNA, fragmenting chromatin, immunoprecipitating the protein of interest using a specific antibody, and then sequencing the co-precipitated DNA fragments.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** ChIP-Seq is treated as a *hypothesis-generating* assay, not a confirmatory one. Two biological replicates are the minimum standard for publication-quality results (per ENCODE consortium guidelines).

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a huge library containing millions of books (the genome). You want to find out which books a particular librarian (the **protein of interest**) touched yesterday. You cannot interview the librarian directly, but you have a unique fingerprint powder (the **antibody**) that sticks *only* to this librarian's fingerprints.

1. First, you spray a fixative that **freezes the librarian's fingers onto the books** (this is **cross-linking** with formaldehyde).
2. You then **shred every book** into tiny pages (this is **sonication** / chromatin fragmentation).
3. You sprinkle the fingerprint powder, and only the pages with the librarian's prints stick to it (this is **immunoprecipitation**).
4. Finally, you **sequence the text** on the captured pages to know exactly which books were touched (this is **high-throughput sequencing**).

### 1.3 Key Technical Terminology

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Cross-linking** | Covalent bonding of protein to DNA using formaldehyde (CH₂O), typically at a final concentration of **1%** for 10–15 min. |
| **Sonication** | Physical shearing of chromatin into **~200–500 bp** fragments using ultrasonic energy. |
| **Immunoprecipitation (IP)** | Antibody-mediated pull-down of the protein–DNA complex using Protein A/G beads. |
| **Input Control** | A non-IP, non-IP'd chromatin sample sequenced in parallel; used as the **background baseline**. |
| **Peak** | A genomic region showing statistically significant read enrichment vs. the input control. |
| **FRiP** | Fraction of Reads in Peaks; a quality metric where a value of **≥ 0.01** (1%) is considered acceptable. |
| **MACS** | Model-based Analysis for ChIP-Seq; the most widely cited peak-caller. |
| **Phred Score (Q)** | Base-calling quality where $Q = -10 \log_{10}(P_{\text{error}})$; a Phred score of **30** implies 99.9% base accuracy. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Cross-link reversal** is performed at **65 °C** for 4 hours (or with NaCl at **200 mM**). Without this reversal step, DNA remains covalently attached to protein and cannot be sequenced.

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The ChIP-Seq Workflow — Step-by-Step

1. **Cross-linking (In Vivo Fixation)**
   - *Why:* Captures the *transient* interaction between protein and DNA at the moment of cell lysis.
   - *How:* Formaldehyde reacts with lysine/arginine side chains of proteins and the adenine/cytosine bases of DNA, forming methylene bridges (–CH₂–).
   - *Critical step:* Cross-linking time must be optimized; over-fixation reduces sonication efficiency and epitope accessibility.

2. **Cell Lysis and Chromatin Preparation**
   - Cells are lysed in a buffer containing protease inhibitors.
   - Chromatin (DNA wrapped around histones + associated proteins) is released into the supernatant.

3. **Sonication (Shearing)**
   - Chromatin is sheared into **200–500 bp** fragments using focused ultrasound.
   - The fragment size distribution is verified by **agarose gel electrophoresis** before proceeding.
   - *Why this size range?* It balances two competing needs: (a) sufficient resolution to pinpoint binding sites, and (b) enough DNA per fragment for reliable sequencing.

4. **Immunoprecipitation**
   - A **specific antibody** against the target protein is added to the sheared chromatin.
   - The antibody–protein–DNA complex is captured on **Protein A/G-conjugated magnetic beads** (or agarose beads).
   - Stringent washing removes non-specific binders (the most common source of *false-positive peaks*).

5. **Reverse Cross-linking and DNA Purification**
   - Cross-links are reversed using **heat + salt**.
   - Proteins are digested using **Proteinase K**.
   - DNA is purified using **phenol-chloroform extraction** or silica-column kits.

6. **Library Preparation and Sequencing**
   - End-repair, dA-tailing, adapter ligation, and size selection.
   - **PCR amplification** with index primers.
   - Sequencing on an **Illumina platform** (commonly NovaSeq, NextSeq, or HiSeq).
   - Read length: **50–150 bp**, single-end or paired-end.

7. **Computational Analysis (Downstream)**
   - Quality control (FastQC).
   - Read alignment to a reference genome (BWA, Bowtie2).
   - Peak calling (MACS2).
   - Annotation and motif analysis (HOMER, MEME).

### 2.2 Statistical Foundations of Peak Calling

Peak callers transform raw read pileups into *confidence-scored* genomic intervals. The mathematical core is the **Poisson (or Negative Binomial) test** comparing the observed ChIP tag count to the expected background.

**Key Assumptions of MACS2:**
- ChIP-seq tags are positionally biased toward the **fragment midpoint** (since each fragment contributes reads to both ends).
- The local background is estimated from a **dynamic λ window** (default: **1000 bp**, **5000 bp**, and **10000 bp**).
- Effective genome size: human = **2.7 × 10⁹ bp**; *C. elegans* = **9 × 10⁷ bp**; *Drosophila* = **1.2 × 10⁸ bp**.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **exam-critical** and frequently tested in Part A and Part B.

| Concept | Formula | Variables / Units |
|---|---|---|
| Phred Quality Score | $Q = -10 \log_{10}(P_{\text{error}})$ | $P_{\text{error}}$ is base-call error probability |
| Fold Enrichment | $\text{FE} = \dfrac{\text{ChIP\_tag\_count}_{\text{peak}}}{\text{Input\_tag\_count}_{\text{peak}}}$ | Dimensionless ratio |
| Poisson Background | $P(X \geq k \mid \lambda) = 1 - \sum_{i=0}^{k-1} \dfrac{\lambda^{i} e^{-\lambda}}{i!}$ | $\lambda$ = local background rate, $k$ = observed count |
| MACS Fragment Shift | $d = \left(\dfrac{L_{f}}{2}\right) - L_{r}$ | $L_f$ = fragment length (bp), $L_r$ = read length (bp) |
| Effective Genome Fraction | $G_{\text{eff}} = G_{\text{total}} \times f_{\text{mappable}}$ | $f_{\text{mappable}}$ ≈ **0.85** for human |
| Normalization Factor | $\text{scale} = \dfrac{N_{\text{Input}}}{N_{\text{ChIP}}}$ | $N$ = total uniquely mapped reads |
| FRiP Score | $\text{FRiP} = \dfrac{\text{Reads in Peaks}}{\text{Total Mapped Reads}}$ | Acceptable threshold $\geq 0.01$ |
| Tag Count Threshold | $k_{\min}$ = smallest $k$ s.t. $P(X \geq k) < p$ | $p$ = user-defined p-value (default **1e-5**) |
| Negative Binomial Mean | $\mu = \lambda$, Variance $= \mu + \alpha \mu^{2}$ | $\alpha$ = over-dispersion parameter |
| GC-content Bias | $B_{\text{GC}} = \dfrac{f_{\text{GC,obs}} - f_{\text{GC,exp}}}{\sigma_{\text{GC}}}$ | $\sigma_{\text{GC}}$ is local std. deviation |

### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering and Science

ChIP-Seq is a **pillar technology** in modern molecular biology and translational research:
- **Drug Discovery:** Identifying transcription-factor binding sites reveals druggable chromatin regulators (e.g., BRD4 inhibitors in oncology).
- **Disease Mechanism:** Mapping altered histone marks (H3K27ac, H3K4me3) in tumor vs. normal cells pinpoints regulatory drivers.
- **Personalized Medicine:** Patient-derived ChIP-Seq profiles guide targeted therapy selection.
- **Synthetic Biology:** Designing synthetic promoters requires knowledge of TF binding motifs validated by ChIP-Seq.
- **Agricultural Biotechnology:** Engineering stress-tolerant crops by editing TF binding sites validated through ChIP-Seq.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the MACS Shift Model

The MACS peak-caller realigns reads by **$d$** bases toward the fragment center to account for the bimodal read distribution on the forward and reverse strands.

**Step 1: Define the observable quantity.** Each sequenced read of length $L_r$ originates from one end of a sonicated fragment of average length $L_f$. For a binding event of true position $x_0$, the forward-strand read is observed at $x_0$ and the reverse-strand read is observed at $x_0 + L_f$.

**Step 2: Express the shift.** The midpoint of the two reads is:

$$m = \dfrac{(x_0) + (x_0 + L_f)}{2} = x_0 + \dfrac{L_f}{2}$$

**Step 3: Compute the per-read offset to midpoint.** The forward read is located at a distance of $\dfrac{L_f}{2}$ from the midpoint. To align the forward read to the midpoint, we shift it by:

$$d_f = \dfrac{L_f}{2} - 0 = \dfrac{L_f}{2}$$

**Step 4: For the reverse read.** The reverse read is at position $x_0 + L_f$ (right end of fragment). The distance to the midpoint is:

$$d_r = \left(x_0 + \dfrac{L_f}{2}\right) - (x_0 + L_f) = -\dfrac{L_f}{2}$$

**Step 5: Unified shift formula.** Accounting for read length $L_r$, the **net shift** applied to each read to bring it to the fragment midpoint is:

$$d = \dfrac{L_f}{2} - L_r$$

**Step 6: Example evaluation.** Given $L_f = 250$ bp and $L_r = 50$ bp:

$$d = \dfrac{250}{2} - 50 = 125 - 50 = 75 \text{ bp}$$

So each read is shifted **75 bp** in the appropriate direction (forward reads shifted right, reverse reads shifted left) to recover the true binding position.

> [!NOTE]
> The choice of $L_f$ matters. If the user provides the sonicated fragment size from a Bioanalyzer trace (e.g., **~300 bp**), $L_f$ should be the **fragment length, not the sequencing read length**.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Poisson Background Test (MACS2 Logic)

**Step 1: Define the null hypothesis.** $H_0$: The observed ChIP tag count $k$ at a genomic position is explained by random background fluctuation.

**Step 2: Estimate the local background rate $\lambda$.** The ChIP signal density is computed across the genome, smoothed, and the local $\lambda$ in a window of width $W$ (default 1, 5, 10 kb) is given by:

$$\lambda_{\text{local}} = \dfrac{\text{ChIP tags in } W \times \text{scale}}{G_{\text{eff}} \times W / G_{\text{total}}}$$

where $\text{scale} = N_{\text{Input}} / N_{\text{ChIP}}$ corrects for sequencing depth differences.

**Step 3: Formulate the test statistic.** Under $H_0$, tags are independent and arrive with rate $\lambda_{\text{local}}$. The probability of observing **at least $k$ tags** at a given position is:

$$P(X \geq k) = 1 - \sum_{i=0}^{k-1} \dfrac{\lambda^{i} e^{-\lambda}}{i!}$$

**Step 4: Worked example.** Let $\lambda = 0.5$ and $k = 4$. We compute the cumulative Poisson probability:

$$P(X \leq 3) = e^{-0.5} \left( \dfrac{0.5^0}{0!} + \dfrac{0.5^1}{1!} + \dfrac{0.5^2}{2!} + \dfrac{0.5^3}{3!} \right)$$

$$P(X \leq 3) = 0.6065 \times \left( 1 + 0.5 + 0.125 + 0.02083 \right)$$

$$P(X \leq 3) = 0.6065 \times 1.6458 = 0.9982$$

$$P(X \geq 4) = 1 - 0.9982 = 0.0018$$

**Step 5: Apply multiple-testing correction.** The genome contains roughly $G_{\text{eff}} / W$ independent tests. Using a **Bonferroni correction** at $\alpha = 0.05$:

$$p_{\text{adj}} = 0.0018 \times \dfrac{2.7 \times 10^{9}}{W}$$

For $W = 200$ bp:

$$p_{\text{adj}} = 0.0018 \times 1.35 \times 10^{7} = 2.43 \times 10^{4}$$

This is greater than 1, so the result is **not significant** at the genome-wide level. Hence, MACS2 also requires a minimum fold-enrichment cutoff (default **2×** or **5×**).

### 3.3 Derivation of the FRiP Quality Metric

The Fraction of Reads in Peaks (FRiP) reflects how well the ChIP enrichment worked.

**Step 1: Count peaks.** Suppose peak calling yields $P$ peaks covering a total of $B$ bp.

**Step 2: Count reads in peaks.** The number of mapped reads that fall within peaks is:

$$R_{\text{in}} = \sum_{i=1}^{P} \sum_{j \in \text{peak}_i} r_j$$

**Step 3: Compute FRiP:**

$$\text{FRiP} = \dfrac{R_{\text{in}}}{R_{\text{total mapped}}}$$

**Step 4: Example.** If a ChIP-seq experiment yields **40 million** uniquely mapped reads, and **800,000** of these fall within called peaks:

$$\text{FRiP} = \dfrac{800{,}000}{40{,}000{,}000} = 0.02 = 2\%$$

A FRiP of **2%** is above the ENCODE minimum of **1%**, indicating a successful ChIP.

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Peak Callers

| Feature | MACS2 | HOMER | PeakSeq | SICER |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Statistical Model | Poisson / NegBin | Poisson | Poisson (sliding window) | Island-based |
| Handles Broad Peaks | Limited (with --broad) | No | No | **Yes (designed for)** |
| Handles Narrow Peaks | **Yes (designed for)** | Yes | Yes | No |
| Built-in Motif Discovery | No | **Yes** | No | No |
| Speed | Fast | Moderate | Slow | Fast |
| Best Use Case | TF ChIP-Seq | TF + Motif Analysis | Variable | Histone Modifications |

> [!NOTE]
> **Histone marks** (e.g., H3K27me3, H3K9me3) span **kilobases** of diffuse enrichment; tools like SICER are preferred. **Transcription factors** produce sharp **200–400 bp** peaks; MACS2 is ideal.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 End-to-End ChIP-Seq Experimental Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Cultured Cells or Tissue] --> A1[Formaldehyde Cross-Linking 1% v/v]
    A1 --> A2[Cell Lysis with Protease Inhibitors]
    A2 --> A3[Sonication 200 to 500 bp Fragments]
    A3 --> A4{Fragment Size QC on Agarose Gel}
    A4 -->|Pass| A5[Immunoprecipitation with Target Specific Antibody]
    A4 -->|Fail| A3
    A5 --> A6[Protein A or G Bead Capture]
    A6 --> A7[Wash to Remove Non Specific Binders]
    A7 --> A8[Reverse Cross Linking 65 C and Proteinase K Digestion]
    A8 --> A9[DNA Purification Phenol Chloroform or Column]
    A9 --> A10[Library Prep End Repair Adapter Ligation PCR]
    A10 --> A11[Illumina Sequencing 50 to 150 bp Reads]
    A11 --> A12[Raw FASTQ Output]
```

### 4.2 Computational Analysis Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    B1[Raw FASTQ Files] --> B2[FastQC Quality Control]
    B2 --> B3[Adapter Trimming Trimmomatic or Cutadapt]
    B3 --> B4[Read Alignment BWA or Bowtie2]
    B4 --> B5[SAM to BAM Conversion and Sort Samtools]
    B5 --> B6[Remove PCR Duplicates Picard MarkDuplicates]
    B6 --> B7[Peak Calling MACS2]
    B7 --> B8[Peak Annotation HOMER or ChIPseeker]
    B8 --> B9[Motif Discovery MEME or HOMER]
    B9 --> B10[Visualization IGV or UCSC Genome Browser]
    B7 --> B11[DiffBind for Differential Binding]
    B8 --> B12[Functional Enrichment GO and KEGG]
```

### 4.3 Peak Calling Decision Logic (MACS2 Block-Level Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    C0[Aligned BAM File] --> C1[Compute Genome Wide Tag Density]
    C1 --> C2[Estimate Local Lambda in Multiple Windows]
    C2 --> C3[Apply d Shift to Reads]
    C3 --> C4[Sliding Window Scan of Pileup]
    C4 --> C5[Poisson p value per Candidate Region]
    C5 --> C6{p value less than 1e-5}
    C6 -->|Yes| C7[Apply Fold Enrichment Filter 2x or 5x]
    C6 -->|No| C8[Reject Candidate]
    C7 --> C9[Merge Overlapping Peaks]
    C9 --> C10[Output BED File of Significant Peaks]
    C8 --> C11[Continue to Next Region]
```

### 4.4 Quality Control Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    D0[ChIP Seq Run Complete] --> D1{FRiP Score more than 0.01}
    D1 -->|No| D2[FAIL Poor Enrichment Re optimize Antibody]
    D1 -->|Yes| D3{PCR Duplication Rate less than 20 percent}
    D3 -->|No| D4[WARNING Over PCR Bias Reduce PCR Cycles]
    D3 -->|Yes| D5{ENCODE Phantom Peak Qc Metric}
    D5 -->|NSC more than 1.05 and RSC more than 0.8| D6[PASS High Quality Dataset]
    D5 -->|Below Threshold| D7[FAIL Low Signal to Noise]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **NSC** (Normalized Strand Coefficient) and **RSC** (Relative Strand Correlation) are ENCODE-recommended QC metrics. NSC > 1.05 indicates a strong signal, while RSC measures strand-shift artifact severity.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define ChIP-Seq. List any four applications of ChIP-Seq in functional genomics.*

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** ChIP-Seq (Chromatin Immunoprecipitation followed by Sequencing) is a high-throughput technique used to map the *in vivo* binding sites of DNA-associated proteins across the entire genome. It involves cross-linking proteins to DNA, fragmenting the chromatin, immunoprecipitating the protein–DNA complex using a specific antibody, and sequencing the pulled-down DNA to identify binding locations.

**Four Applications:**
1. **Mapping transcription factor (TF) binding sites** to understand gene regulatory networks.
2. **Profiling histone modifications** (e.g., H3K4me3 at promoters, H3K27me3 in repressed regions) to define chromatin states.
3. **Identifying drug targets** by mapping the genome-wide occupancy of oncoproteins and tumor suppressors.
4. **Validating enhancer–promoter interactions** when integrated with Hi-C or ChIA-PET data.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*What is peak calling in ChIP-Seq analysis? Name any two peak-calling algorithms and state the statistical model they use.*

**Model Answer:**

**Peak Calling Definition:** Peak calling is the computational process of identifying genomic regions that show statistically significant enrichment of sequencing reads (tags) in the ChIP sample relative to a control (input or IgG) sample. These enriched regions, called *peaks*, represent candidate protein–DNA binding sites.

**Two Peak-Calling Algorithms and Their Models:**

| Algorithm | Statistical Model |
|---|---|
| **MACS2** (Model-based Analysis for ChIP-Seq) | **Poisson distribution** (with optional Negative Binomial for over-dispersed data) |
| **HOMER** | **Poisson distribution** with local background estimation |
| **PeakSeq** | **Poisson** with sliding-window significance testing |
| **SICER** | **Island-based Poisson** for broad enrichment regions |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Students frequently lose marks in Part B by **(i) omitting the cross-linking reversal step** in their experimental workflow, **(ii) confusing fragment length with read length** in the MACS shift calculation, and **(iii) forgetting the multiple-testing correction** when applying the Poisson test. Always justify each step with a *biologically meaningful* reason.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model Paper 2]**

**(a)** Describe in detail the *wet-lab* experimental workflow of a ChIP-Seq experiment, highlighting the role of each step and the critical quality-control checkpoints. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the statistical model used by MACS2 for peak calling. A ChIP-Seq experiment produces 30 million uniquely mapped reads, while the input control has 25 million reads. At a candidate peak, the ChIP tag count is 18, and the local background rate $\lambda$ in a 1 kb window is 0.6. Using the Poisson distribution, calculate the p-value and determine whether the peak passes the default MACS2 significance threshold of $p < 10^{-5}$. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Question A:**

**(a) Wet-Lab Workflow of ChIP-Seq:**

1. **Cross-linking (1 mark):** Cells are treated with **1% formaldehyde** for 10–15 minutes at room temperature. This covalently links proteins to their bound DNA. *QC checkpoint:* Cross-linking duration must be optimized; over-fixation masks antibody epitopes.

2. **Cell Lysis and Chromatin Extraction (1 mark):** Cells are lysed in a buffer containing **protease inhibitors** (to prevent protein degradation) and **phosphatase inhibitors** (for phosphorylation-state ChIP). Chromatin is released into the supernatant.

3. **Sonication (1 mark):** Chromatin is sheared by ultrasonic waves into fragments of **200–500 bp**. *QC checkpoint:* Fragment size is verified on a **1.5% agarose gel**; ideal smear peaks at ~300 bp.

4. **Immunoprecipitation (1 mark):** A **target-specific antibody** is incubated with the sheared chromatin. The antibody–protein–DNA complex is captured on **Protein A/G beads**. *QC checkpoint:* Use only ChIP-grade antibodies validated by ENCODE (avoid polyclonal antibodies with cross-reactivity).

5. **Washing (1 mark):** Stringent washes (e.g., with **LiCl buffer** and **TE buffer**) remove non-specifically bound DNA. *QC checkpoint:* Washing stringency must balance signal vs. noise.

6. **Reverse Cross-linking (1 mark):** Cross-links are reversed by heating at **65 °C for 4 hours** (or overnight) in the presence of **NaCl (200 mM)** and **Proteinase K** to digest proteins.

7. **Library Preparation and Sequencing (1 mark):** Purified DNA is subjected to **end-repair**, **dA-tailing**, **adapter ligation**, **size selection** (gel or bead-based), and **PCR amplification**. Sequencing is performed on an Illumina platform (50–150 bp reads).

---

**(b) Statistical Model and Poisson Calculation:**

The MACS2 algorithm models the ChIP-seq tag count under the **null hypothesis** that the observed read count at a candidate position arises from random background fluctuation, following a **Poisson distribution** with rate parameter $\lambda$ estimated from a local genomic window.

**Step 1: Apply the sequencing-depth normalization.**

$$\text{scale} = \dfrac{N_{\text{Input}}}{N_{\text{ChIP}}} = \dfrac{25 \times 10^{6}}{30 \times 10^{6}} = 0.8333$$

**Step 2: Normalize $\lambda$.**

$$\lambda_{\text{normalized}} = \lambda \times \text{scale} = 0.6 \times 0.8333 = 0.500$$

**Step 3: Compute $P(X \geq 18 \mid \lambda = 0.5)$ using the Poisson survival function.**

$$P(X \geq 18) = 1 - \sum_{i=0}^{17} \dfrac{e^{-0.5} \cdot 0.5^{i}}{i!}$$

Computing the cumulative sum term-by-term for the first 18 terms:

$$P(X \leq 17) = e^{-0.5} \sum_{i=0}^{17} \dfrac{0.5^{i}}{i!}$$

A well-known result for $\lambda = 0.5$ gives:

$$P(X \leq 17) \approx 1 - 7.66 \times 10^{-15}$$

Therefore:

$$P(X \geq 18) \approx 7.66 \times 10^{-15}$$

**Step 4: Compare with the threshold.**

Since $7.66 \times 10^{-15} < 10^{-5}$, the peak **passes** the default MACS2 significance threshold.

**Conclusion:** The candidate peak is a **statistically significant ChIP-Seq peak** at $p \approx 7.66 \times 10^{-15}$, well below the $10^{-5}$ cutoff.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2023]**

**(a)** With a neat schematic, explain the **MACS shift model** in ChIP-Seq data analysis. A ChIP-Seq experiment uses **50 bp single-end reads** and the average sonicated fragment size is **250 bp**. Calculate the shift distance $d$ that MACS2 should apply to align reads to the fragment midpoints. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is the FRiP score? An experiment yields **25 million uniquely mapped reads** in total, and the called peaks together cover a total of **12 million bp**. The mean read coverage within the peaks is **8×**. Compute the FRiP score and comment on its quality. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Question B:**

**(a) MACS Shift Model (7 marks):**

**Concept (4 marks):** In paired-end or single-end ChIP-Seq, the sonicated DNA fragment is sequenced from its **5′ ends**, producing reads that flank the true protein–DNA binding site. For a binding event at position $x_0$, the forward-strand read is observed at $x_0$ and the reverse-strand read is observed at $x_0 + L_f$, where $L_f$ is the fragment length.

The **MACS shift model** estimates the shift $d$ required to relocate each read to the **fragment midpoint**, which approximates the true binding location:

$$d = \dfrac{L_f}{2} - L_r$$

where $L_r$ is the read length. The forward reads are shifted by $+d$ toward the 3′ direction, and the reverse reads are shifted by $-d$ toward the 5′ direction.

**Calculation (3 marks):** Given $L_f = 250$ bp and $L_r = 50$ bp:

$$d = \dfrac{250}{2} - 50 = 125 - 50 = 75 \text{ bp}$$

**Conclusion:** Each read must be shifted by **75 bp** to align with the fragment midpoint. The forward-strand reads shift right by 75 bp, and reverse-strand reads shift left by 75 bp, producing a refined peak centered on the true binding site.

---

**(b) FRiP Score Computation (7 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** The **Fraction of Reads in Peaks (FRiP)** is defined as the proportion of uniquely mapped sequencing reads that fall within the called peaks. It is a global indicator of ChIP enrichment success.

$$\text{FRiP} = \dfrac{\text{Reads in Peaks}}{\text{Total Uniquely Mapped Reads}}$$

**Step 1: Compute reads in peaks (3 marks).** Total bp covered by peaks = **12 × 10⁶ bp**. Mean coverage within peaks = **8×**.

$$R_{\text{in}} = \text{Mean coverage} \times \text{Total bp in peaks}$$

$$R_{\text{in}} = 8 \times 12 \times 10^{6} = 96 \times 10^{6} \text{ reads}$$

**Step 2: Compute FRiP (1 mark).** Given total mapped reads = **25 × 10⁶**:

$$\text{FRiP} = \dfrac{96 \times 10^{6}}{25 \times 10^{6}} = 3.84$$

**Note:** A FRiP > 1 is mathematically impossible in reality. This indicates that either the peak coordinates are inflated, the input depth is misreported, or the coverage was computed including duplicates. The expected FRiP for a well-executed TF ChIP-Seq is in the range of **0.01 to 0.10** (1%–10%).

**Step 3: Commentary on quality (1 mark).** Since FRiP > 1 indicates an inconsistency in the dataset, the experimenter should re-check peak coordinates and read counts. In a realistic scenario, a FRiP of **0.01–0.05** is acceptable, and **> 0.05** indicates excellent enrichment.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision Checklist (Top 12 High-Yield Points):**

- **ChIP-Seq** = Chromatin Immunoprecipitation + Sequencing; maps protein–DNA interactions *in vivo*.
- Cross-linking is done with **1% formaldehyde** for 10–15 minutes; reversed at **65 °C** with **200 mM NaCl**.
- Sonication produces **200–500 bp** chromatin fragments; **NOT** sequencing reads.
- **Input control** is mandatory for accurate background estimation; IgG is a weaker substitute.
- The **MACS shift formula** is $d = (L_f / 2) - L_r$; **never** confuse fragment length with read length.
- **MACS2** uses a **Poisson** model (with optional Negative Binomial) for peak calling.
- **FRiP ≥ 0.01** is the ENCODE minimum threshold for a high-quality ChIP-Seq dataset.
- **NSC > 1.05** and **RSC > 0.8** are recommended QC metrics from the ENCODE consortium.
- **Sharp peaks (200–400 bp)** → Transcription Factor binding; use **MACS2**.
- **Broad peaks (kilobases)** → Histone modifications; use **SICER** or **MACS2 --broad**.
- **Phred Q30** = 99.9% base accuracy; standard for Illumina ChIP-Seq.
- **HOMER** and **MEME** are the de-facto tools for *de novo* motif discovery downstream of peak calling.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: FASTQ File Format and Base Quality Score
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT526 - COMPUTATIONAL BIOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** FASTQ File Format and Base Quality Score

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of FASTQ Format

**FASTQ** is a text-based, ASCII-encoded bioinformatics file format introduced by Phil Cousin (Cock et al., 2010) for storing both a biological sequence (typically a short DNA/RNA read produced by a Next-Generation Sequencer) and its corresponding per-base **quality scores** in a single record. It is the de facto standard output format of high-throughput sequencing platforms such as Illumina, Ion Torrent, and PacBio (post-basecalling).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** FASTQ is the fundamental raw-data container in any NGS pipeline. Before any alignment, assembly, or variant calling is possible, the data must be validated, quality-checked, and preprocessed in FASTQ format.

## 1.2 Structural Anatomy of a FASTQ Record

Every FASTQ file is composed of repeating blocks of **exactly 4 lines per sequence read**. The convention is strict and parser-sensitive.

| Line # | Symbol | Content | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| Line 1 | `@` | Sequence identifier (must begin with `@`) + optional description | `@SRR001666.1 HWUSI-EAS1673_0017:8:1:1041:1196 length=35` |
| Line 2 | *(none)* | The raw biological sequence (A, T, G, C, N) | `GATTACCAAGTCAATAGATCGTAGCTAGCT` |
| Line 3 | `+` | Separator, optionally repeats the identifier from Line 1 | `+SRR001666.1 HWUSI-EAS1673...` |
| Line 4 | *(none)* | ASCII-encoded per-base quality scores (one character per base) | `hhhhhhhhhhhhhhhhhhfffeeddcccbb` |

> [!NOTE]
> A **single FASTQ file** typically contains **millions of such 4-line records** (one per read). An Illumina NovaSeq run can produce 2–3 billion reads, meaning the FASTQ file may be hundreds of gigabytes in size.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy: The "Confidence Notebook"

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy (Plain English):**
> Imagine you are a court stenographer typing the spoken words of a courtroom trial. You write down what you *think* you heard (`ATCG...`), and next to each letter you secretly rate your own confidence on a scale of 0–40. A confident letter like a clear "A" gets a score of 40, while a mumbled, indistinguishable sound gets a score of 2.
>
> The FASTQ file is exactly this: a **notebook where every DNA letter is paired with the machine's "confidence" in reading it**. The line with the sequence tells you *what* the machine saw, and the quality line tells you *how sure* it was. Later, a quality-control expert (the bioinformatician) can look at the notebook and throw away the unreliable parts.

## 1.4 Standard Metrics & Constants

- **Phred Quality Score Scale:** Integer values typically ranging from **0 to 40** (logarithmic).
- **ASCII Offset — Sanger / Phred+33:** ASCII code = Q + **33** (ASCII range 33–73, characters `!` to `I`).
- **ASCII Offset — Phred+64 (legacy Illumina 1.3–1.7):** ASCII code = Q + **64** (ASCII range 64–104, characters `@` to `h`).
- **Most Common Modern Standard:** **Phred+33 / Sanger** (used by Illumina since 2009 / Pipeline v1.8+).
- **Encoding Character Range:** printable ASCII (decimal 32–126).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Phred Quality Score — Mathematical Foundation

The **Phred quality score (Q)** is a logarithmic measure of the probability (P) that a base call is **incorrect**. It was originally developed by Ewing & Green (1998) for the Phred basecaller, and later adopted as a universal standard.

### Step-by-Step Logic of the Phred Score

1. **Basecaller Output:** A sequencing machine's image-processing software analyzes fluorescent intensities and emits both an estimated base (`A`, `C`, `G`, `T`) and a statistical error probability estimate (P).
2. **Error Probability (P):** This is a real number in the range (0, 1] — for example, 0.001 means a 1-in-1000 chance the base is wrong.
3. **Logarithmic Compression:** Because P can span many orders of magnitude, the score is compressed using a base-10 logarithm multiplied by −10. This produces a compact, human-friendly integer.
4. **Rounding to Nearest Integer:** The Q value is rounded to the nearest integer to match the integer ASCII character grid.
5. **ASCII Encoding:** Q is offset by 33 (Sanger) or 64 (Phred+64) and stored as a single ASCII character on the 4th line of the FASTQ record.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why negative 10 and base 10?** A factor of −10 was chosen so that a 10-fold improvement in accuracy corresponds to exactly 10 additional Q-points. For example, Q=20 (P=0.01) is 10× more accurate than Q=10 (P=0.10).

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Rule | Description | Units / Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q = -10 \cdot \log_{10}(P)$ | Phred score from error probability | Q ∈ [0, 60], P ∈ (0, 1] |
| 2 | $P = 10^{-Q/10}$ | Error probability from Phred score | P ∈ (0, 1] |
| 3 | $A_{Sanger} = Q + 33$ | ASCII code under Phred+33 (Sanger) | A ∈ [33, 73] |
| 4 | $A_{Phred64} = Q + 64$ | ASCII code under Phred+64 (legacy) | A ∈ [64, 104] |
| 5 | $A = \text{chr}(Q + \text{offset})$ | Conversion to printable character | printable ASCII |
| 6 | $\text{Accuracy} = 1 - P = 1 - 10^{-Q/10}$ | Probability the base is *correct* | Accuracy ∈ [0, 1) |
| 7 | $P_{read} = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{L}(1 - P_i)$ | Joint error of an entire read | L = read length |
| 8 | $\bar{Q}_{read} = \frac{1}{L}\sum_{i=1}^{L} Q_i$ | Mean Q-score of a read | Q̄ ∈ [0, 60] |
| 9 | $Q_{min}, Q_{median}, Q_{mean}$ | Per-base position-wise statistics | Aggregated per cycle |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Quality Trimming (Trimmomatic, fastp, Cutadapt):** Bases below a Q threshold (commonly Q=20) are trimmed from the 3′ end of reads to improve downstream alignment accuracy.
- **Read Filtering & Discarding:** Reads whose mean Q-score falls below a threshold (e.g., Q=30) are dropped entirely to avoid contaminating the assembly.
- **Variant Calling Confidence:** Tools like GATK weight the evidence for SNPs using per-base Q-scores, so ambiguous bases contribute less to the final call.
- **Adapter Contamination Detection:** Low-quality stretches at the 3′ end often contain Illumina adapter sequences that must be removed.
- **Storage Optimization:** Tools like **CRAM** and **BAM** with binning compress Q-scores lossily (quantization) because they dominate the file size — 50–70% of a FASTQ file is the quality line.

## 2.4 Quality Score Interpretation Table

| Phred Q-Score | Error Probability (P) | Base Call Accuracy | ASCII (Phred+33) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 1.000 | 0% | `!` (33) |
| 10 | 0.100 | 90% | `7` (43) |
| 20 | 0.010 | 99% | `5` (53) |
| 30 | 0.001 | 99.9% | `?` (63) |
| 40 | 0.0001 | 99.99% | `I` (73) |
| 60 | 0.000001 | 99.9999% | `]` (93) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Standard:** Modern Illumina platforms (NovaSeq, NextSeq, MiSeq) typically produce reads with the majority of bases at Q ≥ 30 (the "Q30 standard" benchmark used by sequencer vendors).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: The Phred Quality Score Equation

The Phred score is derived from first principles using the base-10 logarithm of the error probability.

### 3.1.1 Starting Point — Raw Error Probability

A basecaller computes, for each called base $b_i$, a probability that the call is wrong, denoted $P_i$, where $0 < P_i \leq 1$. A confident call gives a small $P_i$ (e.g., $P = 0.001$); a noisy call gives a large $P_i$ (e.g., $P = 0.5$).

### 3.1.2 Compressing with a Negative Logarithm

To make the score **larger for better calls** (intuitive for humans), we take the **negative** of the logarithm:

$$
S = -\log_{10}(P)
$$

This is positive and grows as $P$ shrinks. For $P = 0.001$, $S = -\log_{10}(0.001) = 3$.

### 3.1.3 Scaling by a Factor of 10

Since a 10× reduction in error should produce a "round" integer jump in the score, the log is multiplied by 10. Substituting $Q = 10 \cdot S$:

$$
Q = -10 \cdot \log_{10}(P)
$$

This is the canonical Phred quality score equation.

### 3.1.4 Inverting the Equation

To recover the error probability from a known Q value, we algebraically invert:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q &= -10 \cdot \log_{10}(P) \\[4pt]
\frac{Q}{-10} &= \log_{10}(P) \\[4pt]
10^{Q/(-10)} &= P \\[4pt]
P &= 10^{-Q/10}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conversion logic:** Each step reverses the previous: division by −10 → exponentiation of base 10 → rewrite with negative exponent for compactness.

### 3.1.5 Example Numerical Evaluation — Q=30

$$
\begin{aligned}
P &= 10^{-30/10} \\[4pt]
P &= 10^{-3} \\[4pt]
P &= 0.001
\end{aligned}
$$

So a base with Q=30 has a 0.1% chance of being wrong (99.9% accurate).

### 3.1.6 Example Numerical Evaluation — Q=20

$$
\begin{aligned}
P &= 10^{-20/10} \\[4pt]
P &= 10^{-2} \\[4pt]
P &= 0.01
\end{aligned}
$$

So Q=20 means 1% error rate (99% accuracy).

## 3.2 Derivation: ASCII Encoding Offset (Phred+33 / Sanger)

### 3.2.1 Purpose

The Q values (0–40) are stored as single ASCII characters to maintain human-readable text files. ASCII characters below decimal 32 are control characters (whitespace, NULL, etc.) and are forbidden in text files. Therefore, an **offset** is added.

### 3.2.2 Choosing the Offset

The smallest printable ASCII character is **space** (decimal 32). The next character is `!` (decimal 33). The Sanger/Illumina 1.8+ convention uses:

$$
A_{char} = Q + 33
$$

where $A_{char}$ is the decimal ASCII code of the stored character.

### 3.2.3 Inverse: Decoding Q from ASCII

To recover Q from a stored character, subtract the offset:

$$
Q = A_{char} - 33
$$

### 3.2.4 Worked Example

Suppose the FASTQ quality line begins with the character `h`:

- ASCII decimal of `h` = **104**
- Using Sanger offset: $Q = 104 - 33 = 71$ (this is unusually high; `h` is at the top of the Sanger range)

A more realistic example: character `?`:

- ASCII decimal of `?` = **63**
- $Q = 63 - 33 = 30$ → 99.9% accuracy

## 3.3 Derivation: Joint Error Probability of a Full Read

A read of length $L$ contains $L$ independent bases. Assuming independence (a common approximation in NGS pipelines), the probability that **all bases** in the read are correct is the product of their individual correct-call probabilities.

### 3.3.1 Per-Base Correct Probability

$$
P_{correct,i} = 1 - P_i = 1 - 10^{-Q_i/10}
$$

### 3.3.2 Joint Read Correctness

$$
P_{read,correct} = \prod_{i=1}^{L}(1 - P_i) = \prod_{i=1}^{L}(1 - 10^{-Q_i/10})
$$

### 3.3.3 Joint Read Error Probability

$$
P_{read,wrong} = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{L}(1 - 10^{-Q_i/10})
$$

### 3.3.4 Worked Example

Consider a 5-base read with Q-scores $[40, 30, 30, 20, 10]$:

- $P_1 = 10^{-4} = 0.0001$ → $1 - P_1 = 0.9999$
- $P_2 = 10^{-3} = 0.001$  → $1 - P_2 = 0.999$
- $P_3 = 10^{-3} = 0.001$  → $1 - P_3 = 0.999$
- $P_4 = 10^{-2} = 0.01$   → $1 - P_4 = 0.99$
- $P_5 = 10^{-1} = 0.1$    → $1 - P_5 = 0.9$

Multiplying:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{read,correct} &= 0.9999 \times 0.999 \times 0.999 \times 0.99 \times 0.9 \\[4pt]
&= 0.9999 \times 0.998001 \times 0.891 \\[4pt]
&\approx 0.8895
\end{aligned}
$$

So the joint error rate is approximately $1 - 0.8895 = 0.1105$, i.e., ~11% — dominated by the single low-quality Q=10 base at the 3′ end. This illustrates the classic Illumina **quality decay** pattern.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Encoding Schemes Comparison

| Property | Sanger (Phred+33) | Phred+64 (Illumina 1.3–1.7) | Solexa (Legacy) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Formula | $A = Q + 33$ | $A = Q + 64$ | $Q_{Solexa} = -10 \log_{10}\frac{P}{1-P}$ |
| Q Range | 0–40+ | 0–40 | −5 to 40 |
| ASCII Range | 33–73 (`!` to `I`) | 64–104 (`@` to `h`) | 59–104 |
| Used By | Illumina ≥ 1.8, Sanger, 454 | Illumina 1.3–1.7 | Illumina 1.0–1.2 |
| Status | **Current standard** | Deprecated | Deprecated |
| Decoding | $Q = A - 33$ | $Q = A - 64$ | Re-conversion required |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — FASTQ Generation Pipeline

The following Mermaid diagram maps the *complete functional flow* of how a sequencing machine produces a FASTQ file, from sample DNA to per-base quality scores.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[DNA Fragment Library] --> B[Cluster Generation on Flowcell]
    B --> C[Sequencing by Synthesis SBS Cycles]
    C --> D[Image Acquisition per Cycle]
    D --> E[Intensity Extraction]
    E --> F[Basecalling Algorithm]
    F --> G1[Per-Base Call A T G C N]
    G1 --> H[Per-Base Error Probability P]
    H --> I[Phred Score Q = -10 log10 P]
    I --> J[ASCII Encoder offset 33]
    J --> K[FASTQ Record: 4 lines]
    K --> L[Compressed gz FASTQ File Output]
    M[Quality Filter Q >= 20] --> N[Trimmed and Cleaned Reads]
    L --> M
    N --> O[Downstream Alignment BWA Bowtie]
    O --> P[Variant Calling GATK]
```

## 4.2 FASTQ Record Structure — Schematic Block View

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Line 1 Identifier @SRR001666.1 length=35] --> B[Line 2 Sequence GATTAC...]
    B --> C[Line 3 Plus Separator +]
    C --> D[Line 4 Quality ASCII hhhheeeddc...]
    D --> E[Next Record]
    E --> F[Line 1 Identifier @SRR001666.2]
    F --> G[Line 2 Sequence ...]
    G --> H[Line 3 Plus +]
    H --> I[Line 4 Quality ...]
```

## 4.3 ASCII-to-Q-Score Decoding Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[FASTQ Quality Line hhhhhheeedddcccbbaa] --> P[Read Character One at a Time]
    P --> Q[Get ASCII Decimal Value from ord]
    Q --> R{Determine Encoding}
    R -->|Sanger Phred 33| T[Q = ascii minus 33]
    R -->|Phred 64| U[Q = ascii minus 64]
    T --> V[Q Vector 40 40 40 40 40 40 40 37 37 37 36 36 36 35 34 34 33 33 33 32 32 30]
    U --> V
    V --> W[Apply Q = -10 log10 P to recover Error Probability]
    W --> X[Per-Base Confidence Map]
```

## 4.4 Quality Decay Pattern Along Read Length (Conceptual)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[5 Prime End Cycle 1] -->|High Q 40| B[Mid Read Cycle 50]
    B -->|Moderate Q 30| C[3 Prime End Cycle 100]
    C -->|Low Q 10 to 20| D[Quality Drop Off]
    D --> E[Trimming Decision Threshold Q = 20]
    E --> F[Trimmed High Quality Region]
```

> [!NOTE]
> This decay pattern is characteristic of Illumina sequencing-by-synthesis platforms. The 3′ end of the read typically contains accumulated phasing/pre-phasing errors and decreasing signal-to-noise ratios.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define the FASTQ file format. What are the four mandatory lines in a FASTQ record, and what does the fourth line represent?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
FASTQ is a text-based, ASCII-encoded file format used to store biological sequences (typically NGS short reads) along with their corresponding per-base quality scores. Each record consists of **four mandatory lines**:

1. **Line 1** — Begins with `@` and contains the sequence identifier (and optional description).
2. **Line 2** — The raw nucleotide sequence using the alphabet `A, T, G, C, N`.
3. **Line 3** — Begins with `+` and is a separator (may optionally repeat the identifier from line 1).
4. **Line 4** — A string of ASCII characters, where each character encodes the **Phred quality score** of the corresponding base in line 2. The ASCII value minus the offset (33 for Sanger) gives the Q value, representing the negative log-10 of the error probability.

> **[Mark Allocation: 1 for format definition + 1 for naming the 4 lines + 1 for explaining line 4.]**

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is the Phred quality score? Derive the relationship between a Phred score of 20 and the corresponding base-call accuracy.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Phred quality score (Q) is a logarithmic, integer-valued measure of the probability of an erroneous base call, defined as:

$$
Q = -10 \cdot \log_{10}(P)
$$

For **Q = 20**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P &= 10^{-Q/10} = 10^{-20/10} = 10^{-2} = 0.01
\end{aligned}
$$

This means there is a **1% probability of error**, and the corresponding **base-call accuracy = 1 − P = 0.99 = 99%**.

> **[Mark Allocation: 1 for definition + 1 for correct formula + 1 for correct numerical substitution and accuracy value.]**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` (Choice Option)

**(a)** Explain in detail the **FASTQ file format** with a clearly labelled example record. Discuss why the fourth line uses ASCII encoding instead of storing the raw probabilities. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A FASTQ quality line contains the characters: `hhhhhhhhheeeeeddddccccbbbb````aaaa```` `````` (assume Sanger/Phred+33 encoding). Compute the Phred Q-scores, error probabilities, and the joint error probability of the entire read. Interpret the trend. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

The FASTQ format stores four lines per read:
- Line 1 (`@`): Read identifier — e.g., `@SRR001666.1 HWUSI-EAS1673_0017:8:1:1041:1196 length=35`
- Line 2: Read sequence — e.g., `GATTACCAGTCAATAGATCGTAGCTAGCTAGCT`
- Line 3 (`+`): Separator (can repeat the identifier)
- Line 4: Quality string — e.g., `hhhhhhhhhhhhhhhhhhhhhhhhhhhhhhhh`

**Why ASCII encoding is used** (3 sub-points, ~3 marks):
1. **Compactness:** Storing raw probabilities (floating-point numbers) would require 4–8 bytes per base, whereas ASCII uses 1 byte per base — reducing file size by 4–8×.
2. **Human-readability:** ASCII characters are text-editable, debuggable, and grep-searchable.
3. **Compression-friendly:** Identical ASCII characters compress extremely well with gzip, often achieving >80% size reduction.
4. **Standardization:** A common, platform-independent encoding simplifies tool interoperability.
5. **Integers suffice:** Phred scores are inherently rounded integers, so floating-point storage is unnecessary.

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

Step 1 — Count characters in the quality line. For the given string of 36 characters, the Sanger ASCII values must be tabulated. The first 9 are `h` (ASCII 104), the next 5 are `e` (ASCII 101), the next 4 are `d` (ASCII 100), the next 4 are `c` (ASCII 99), the next 4 are `b` (ASCII 98), the next 6 are `a` (ASCII 97), and the rest (4 characters) are space/lowest quality.

| Character | ASCII Decimal | $Q = A - 33$ | $P = 10^{-Q/10}$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| `h` | 104 | 71 | $10^{-7.1} \approx 7.94 \times 10^{-8}$ |
| `e` | 101 | 68 | $10^{-6.8} \approx 1.58 \times 10^{-7}$ |
| `d` | 100 | 67 | $10^{-6.7} \approx 2.00 \times 10^{-7}$ |
| `c` | 99 | 66 | $10^{-6.6} \approx 2.51 \times 10^{-7}$ |
| `b` | 98 | 65 | $10^{-6.5} \approx 3.16 \times 10^{-7}$ |
| `a` | 97 | 64 | $10^{-6.4} \approx 3.98 \times 10^{-7}$ |
| (low) | 33 | 0 | $10^{0} = 1$ |

Step 2 — Joint error probability. Multiply the per-base correct probabilities (assuming independence):

$$
P_{read,correct} = \prod_{i=1}^{L}(1 - P_i)
$$

For the first 9 bases: $(1 - 7.94 \times 10^{-8})^{9} \approx 0.9999993$.
The 4 trailing Q=0 bases contribute $0^4 = 0$, so $P_{read,correct} = 0$, and the **joint error probability = 1.0 (100%)**.

Step 3 — **Interpretation:** A read containing any base with $P = 1$ (Q = 0) is unusable, since at least one position is guaranteed to be incorrect. The upstream high-quality region is wasted. This demonstrates why **quality trimming** is essential — the tool would remove the trailing 4 low-quality bases.

> **[Mark Allocation: 2 marks tabulation + 2 marks formula + 1 mark numerical evaluation + 2 marks interpretation.]**

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` (Alternative Choice)

**(a)** Define the **Phred quality score**. Explain the difference between **Phred+33 (Sanger)** and **Phred+64 (Illumina 1.3–1.7)** encoding schemes, with a numerical example. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A sequencing run produces a read of length 100 with the following per-base Q-score distribution: the first 50 bases have Q=40, the next 30 have Q=30, and the last 20 have Q=20. Calculate:
   (i) The probability that the **entire read is correctly called**.
   (ii) The probability that the **entire read contains at least one error**.
   (iii) The **mean Q-score** of the read. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

The Phred quality score $Q$ is defined as $Q = -10 \cdot \log_{10}(P)$ where $P$ is the probability of an incorrect base call. It is an integer scale typically spanning 0–40.

**Phred+33 (Sanger):** ASCII code = $Q + 33$. Character range: 33 (`!`) to 73 (`I`). Used by Sanger capillary sequencing and Illumina platforms from Pipeline v1.8 onwards (2009+). **This is the current standard.**

**Phred+64 (Illumina 1.3–1.7):** ASCII code = $Q + 64$. Character range: 64 (`@`) to 104 (`h`). Used by Illumina Genome Analyzer II from 2008–2010. **Now deprecated.**

**Numerical example:** Consider a base with Q = 20.
- Sanger encoding: ASCII code = $20 + 33 = 53$ → character `5`.
- Phred+64 encoding: ASCII code = $20 + 64 = 84$ → character `T`.

Both represent the same Q=20, but the stored character differs. Confusion between schemes can lead to misinterpreting quality — e.g., `5` under Phred+64 would mean $Q = 84 - 64 = 20$, but if mistakenly decoded as Sanger, it would be $Q = 84 - 33 = 51$, a huge over-estimate.

> **[Mark Allocation: 2 marks definitions + 2 marks scheme comparison + 1 mark offset formulas + 2 marks numerical example.]**

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

Given: L = 100, divided into three blocks of Q-scores 40, 30, 20.

Step 1 — Per-block error probabilities.

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{Q=40} &= 10^{-40/10} = 10^{-4} = 0.0001 \\[4pt]
P_{Q=30} &= 10^{-30/10} = 10^{-3} = 0.001 \\[4pt]
P_{Q=20} &= 10^{-20/10} = 10^{-2} = 0.01
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 2 — Per-base correct probabilities.

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{40} &= 1 - 0.0001 = 0.9999 \\[4pt]
C_{30} &= 1 - 0.001  = 0.999 \\[4pt]
C_{20} &= 1 - 0.01   = 0.99
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 3 — Joint correct probability (product over all 100 bases):

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{read,correct} &= (C_{40})^{50} \times (C_{30})^{30} \times (C_{20})^{20} \\[4pt]
&= (0.9999)^{50} \times (0.999)^{30} \times (0.99)^{20} \\[4pt]
&\approx 0.9950125 \times 0.9703767 \times 0.8179069 \\[4pt]
&\approx 0.7896
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 4 — Joint error probability (at least one wrong base):

$$
P_{read,wrong} = 1 - P_{read,correct} = 1 - 0.7896 = 0.2104
$$

So the probability that the read contains at least one error is approximately **21.04%**.

Step 5 — Mean Q-score:

$$
\bar{Q} = \frac{(40 \times 50) + (30 \times 30) + (20 \times 20)}{100} = \frac{2000 + 900 + 400}{100} = \frac{3300}{100} = 33
$$

> **[Mark Allocation: 2 marks per-block P-values + 1 mark product formula + 1 mark arithmetic + 1 mark error complement + 2 marks mean Q calculation.]**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> 1. **Do not confuse offsets** when given ASCII values — examiners specifically test whether students can distinguish Phred+33 from Phred+64. A common mistake is using offset 64 for a Sanger-encoded file, leading to inflated Q-scores.
> 2. **Independence assumption:** The joint-error formula assumes per-base independence, which is an *approximation* — real sequencers exhibit correlated errors (e.g., at the 3′ end). Mentioning this caveat in a long-answer earns ½–1 extra mark.
> 3. **Units of probability:** Q is dimensionless but P is a probability. Never write "Q = 0.001" — that is a probability, not a quality score.
> 4. **Sign errors:** Forgetting the negative sign in $Q = -10 \log_{10}(P)$ produces negative Q-values, an immediate red flag to a KTU evaluator.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- 📌 **FASTQ = 4 lines per read:** `@id`, sequence, `+`, quality. This rule is non-negotiable.
- 📌 **Phred equation:** $Q = -10 \log_{10}(P)$. Memorize both forward and inverse forms.
- 📌 **Q=20 ⇒ 99% accuracy, Q=30 ⇒ 99.9% accuracy, Q=40 ⇒ 99.99% accuracy.** These three benchmarks are the most common exam numbers.
- 📌 **Current standard is Phred+33 (Sanger)**, offset of 33, used by all modern Illumina platforms.
- 📌 **Legacy Phred+64** used offset 64 and is now obsolete but may appear in old datasets.
- 📌 **ASCII decoding:** $Q = A_{decimal} - \text{offset}$.
- 📌 **Quality decay:** Illumina reads typically degrade from 5′ to 3′, motivating 3′-end trimming.
- 📌 **Joint read error:** $P_{wrong} = 1 - \prod(1 - P_i)$ under the independence assumption.
- 📌 **Q-line dominates file size** — 50–70% of a FASTQ file is the quality line, which is why compressors like `gzip` (and formats like CRAM) target it.
- 📌 **N character** in the sequence line means "ambiguous base"; the corresponding quality is typically set to Q=0 or a low value to prevent its use.
- 📌 **Tools to know:** FastQC (visualization), Trimmomatic/fastp (trimming), Cutadapt (adapter removal), BWA/Bowtie2 (alignment).
- 📌 **Examiner traps:** (1) Wrong ASCII offset, (2) missing the negative sign, (3) confusing accuracy and error probability, (4) treating Q as having units.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: NGS Data Quality Control and Preprocessing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT526 - COMPUTATIONAL BIOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** NGS Data Quality Control and Preprocessing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# NGS Data Quality Control and Preprocessing

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Next-Generation Sequencing (NGS) Data Quality Control (QC) and Preprocessing** refers to the systematic and reproducible set of computational operations performed on raw sequencing reads (typically stored in **FASTQ** format) immediately after they are generated by the sequencing platform, prior to any downstream analytical task such as alignment, assembly, variant calling, or quantification.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus** for Computational Biology, this stage constitutes the foundational layer of the NGS analytical pipeline, ensuring that biological inferences are derived only from high-confidence nucleotide sequences. It encompasses two tightly coupled activities:

- **Quality Control (QC)**: Diagnostic assessment of raw read quality using statistical summarization (e.g., per-base quality scores, GC content, sequence duplication, adapter contamination, overrepresented sequences).
- **Preprocessing (Cleaning)**: Active remediation steps (quality trimming, adapter removal, length filtering, duplicate handling) that produce an analysis-ready dataset.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight**: This topic is mapped to **CO4** of PEADT526. Students are expected to *apply* QC tools and *analyze* their output reports in the laboratory component, in addition to describing the underlying theory in the End Semester Examination (ESE).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a librarian receives **10 million photocopied pages** (raw reads) from a manuscript printed by a malfunctioning machine. Before any scholar can study the manuscript's content, the librarian must:

1. **Inspect** every page for smudges, faded ink, and torn corners (this is **QC** — looking at FastQC reports).
2. **Trim** the smudged edges and **discard** pages that are too damaged to read (this is **quality trimming and filtering**).
3. **Remove** sticky notes (paper clips, adapters) accidentally left in the copier (this is **adapter removal**).
4. Only then can the cleaned manuscript be sent for translation or analysis.

If the librarian skips these steps, the scholar may misinterpret smudges as real words and draw false conclusions — analogous to how downstream bioinformatics tools will report false SNPs, misassembled contigs, or biased expression levels if fed unfiltered NGS data.

### 1.3 Key Metrics at a Glance

The cornerstone metric for base-level confidence is the **Phred Quality Score**, denoted **Q**. The most widely cited relationship in NGS is:

$$Q = -10 \cdot \log_{10}(P)$$

where $P$ is the probability that the called base is **incorrect**. A **Phred score of 20 (Q20)** therefore corresponds to a **1 in 100 (99%)** accuracy, while **Q30** corresponds to **99.9% accuracy**, which is the modern industry-accepted threshold for high-quality base calls.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Threshold**: Most KTU-aligned pipelines adopt **Q30** as the trimming cutoff. Anything below Q20 is generally considered **low quality** and is aggressively trimmed in modern short-read Illumina workflows.

### 1.4 The FASTQ Format — The Native Data Container

Every QC tool operates on the **FASTQ** format, a text-based container that pairs a nucleotide sequence with a per-base quality string of identical length. Each read occupies exactly **four lines**:

| Line | Symbol | Content |
|------|--------|---------|
| 1 | `@` | Read identifier (sequence ID + optional description) |
| 2 | — | Raw nucleotide sequence (A, C, G, T, N) |
| 3 | `+` | Optional repeat of the read ID |
| 4 | — | Quality score string (ASCII-encoded Phred values) |

The ASCII encoding scheme most commonly used today is **Phred+33 (Sanger / Illumina 1.8+)**, where each quality character $c$ maps to:

$$Q = \text{ord}(c) - 33$$

The earlier **Phred+64** scheme (used by Illumina 1.3–1.7) used a different offset, and misidentifying the encoding is one of the most common preprocessing bugs encountered in practice.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The NGS QC Pipeline — Operational Logic

The QC-and-preprocessing workflow follows a deterministic four-stage logic. KTU examiners frequently test whether students understand *why* the order matters and what each stage produces.

### Stage 1 — Quality Profiling (Read-Only Diagnostic)

The raw FASTQ file is statistically summarized **without modification**. The de facto standard tool is **FastQC**, which generates eleven modular reports, the most informative being:

- **Per-base sequence quality** (box plot of Q across cycle positions)
- **Per-base sequence content** (proportion of A/C/G/T across cycles)
- **Per-sequence GC content** (distribution compared to a theoretical model)
- **Sequence duplication levels** (relative frequency of identical reads)
- **Adapter content** (fraction of reads still harboring the 3′ sequencing adapter)
- **Overrepresented sequences** (k-mers appearing far more often than chance)

### Stage 2 — Quality Trimming

Bases whose **Phred score falls below a chosen threshold** are excised from the 3′ end of the read (and sometimes the 5′ end, a process called **headcrop**). The most common tool is **Trimmomatic**, which supports four trimming modes:

- **LEADING**: Trim low-quality bases from the start of the read until a base above threshold is encountered.
- **TRAILING**: Trim low-quality bases from the end of the read.
- **SLIDINGWINDOW**: Scan a window of $W$ bases; trim if the mean quality drops below the threshold.
- **MAXINFO**: A balanced adaptive trim that retains read length while maximizing total information content.

### Stage 3 — Adapter and Contaminant Removal

Even after size-selection during library preparation, a residual fraction of reads still contains a 3′ overhang of the sequencing adapter (e.g., **Illumina TruSeq Adapter: `AGATCGGAAGAGC`**). Tools such as **Cutadapt** and the **ILLUMINACLIP** step of Trimmomatic perform *semi-global alignment* of the adapter sequence against each read and excise the aligned portion.

### Stage 4 — Length Filtering and Read Pair Synchronization

Reads trimmed below a minimum length (typically **36–50 bp**) are discarded because they often lack sufficient biological signal. Crucially, for **paired-end** libraries, the two FASTQ files must remain synchronized: if read 1 is discarded, read 2 must also be removed to preserve the upstream-downstream insert-length relationship. Failure to do so corrupts every downstream alignment and variant call.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Frequently Tested Concept**: The difference between **single-pass trimming** and **iterative trimming**. Iterative trimming (re-running QC after trimming) is the gold-standard practice and is often an exam question worth **7 marks**.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The table below consolidates every formula, threshold, and standard metric the student is expected to memorize for ESE.

| Symbol / Term | Formula or Definition | Value / Unit | Engineering / Biological Utility |
|---------------|----------------------|--------------|----------------------------------|
| Phred Quality Score | $Q = -10 \log_{10}(P)$ | Dimensionless | Maps base-calling error probability to a linear integer scale. |
| Error Probability | $P = 10^{-Q/10}$ | Probability $\in [0, 1]$ | Inverse of Phred — used for downstream probabilistic models. |
| Phred+33 Decoding | $Q = \text{ord}(c) - 33$ | ASCII offset of 33 | Standard for Illumina 1.8+, Sanger, and modern NGS. |
| Phred+64 Decoding | $Q = \text{ord}(c) - 64$ | ASCII offset of 64 | Legacy Illumina 1.3–1.7; obsolete today. |
| Industry Standard Cutoff | Q30 threshold | 99.9% base accuracy | Modern trimming threshold; Q20 is loose, Q30 strict. |
| Mean Sliding-Window Quality | $\bar{Q}_w = \frac{1}{W}\sum_{i=1}^{W} Q_i$ | Dimensionless | Used by Trimmomatic SLIDINGWINDOW mode. |
| GC Content of a Read | $GC\% = \frac{G + C}{A + C + G + T} \times 100$ | Percentage | Species/region-specific; QC detects contamination if skewed. |
| Expected GC Distribution | $B(n, 0.5)$ for unbiased random sequence | Probability mass | Theoretical benchmark for per-sequence GC content plot. |
| Adapter Contamination Rate | $R_{\text{ad}} = \frac{N_{\text{reads with adapter}}}{N_{\text{total reads}}}$ | Fraction $\in [0,1]$ | Higher than **5%** indicates poor library preparation. |
| Effective Read Length | $L_{\text{eff}} = L_{\text{raw}} - L_{\text{trim5′}} - L_{\text{trim3′}}$ | Base pairs | Required to be $\geq 36$ bp for typical aligners. |
| Duplicate Rate | $D = 1 - \frac{N_{\text{unique reads}}}{N_{\text{total reads}}}$ | Fraction | High values indicate PCR over-amplification or low input DNA. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Allotment Tip for KTU ESE**: A direct "Define Phred score and derive $Q = -10 \log_{10}(P)$" question is worth **3 marks** and appears almost every semester. Memorize the derivation, not just the formula.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

In clinical genomics (e.g., **NIPT, liquid-biopsy tumor profiling, rare-variant detection**), a single miscalled base can misdiagnose a patient. Therefore, QC-and-preprocessing is not an academic housekeeping step — it is a **regulated clinical-validation gate** under **CLIA, CAP, and ISO 15189** standards. In agricultural genomics (a KTU-aligned domain via Kerala's spice and rice research institutes), QC ensures that marker-assisted selection for **black pepper, cardamom, and Pokkali rice** is not biased by sequencing artifacts.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Phred Score from First Principles

The Phred quality score was originally developed by **Ewing and Green (1998)** at the Sanger Centre / Washington University to quantify base-caller confidence. The derivation proceeds from a single axiom of probability.

### Step 1 — Axiom of Base-Calling Confidence

Let $P$ denote the probability that the base-calling algorithm reports an **incorrect nucleotide** at a given cycle position. By the complement rule of probability, the probability of a **correct** call is $(1 - P)$.

### Step 2 — Choose a Logarithmic Encoding

Quality scores are conventionally expressed on a logarithmic scale so that:

- A **10-fold improvement** in accuracy corresponds to a **constant additive increase** in the score.
- The score remains compact (single byte) even when accuracy spans many orders of magnitude.

The most natural choice is the **base-10 logarithm** because it produces integer scores for round error probabilities.

### Step 3 — Apply the Negative Sign Convention

Since $P < 1$, the logarithm $\log_{10}(P)$ is negative. To make the quality score increase with accuracy, we multiply by $-10$, where the factor of 10 ensures that a 10× error reduction corresponds to a 10-point gain.

$$\boxed{Q = -10 \cdot \log_{10}(P)}$$

### Step 4 — Verify with Worked Examples

**Example A**: Suppose a base-caller has a 1% error rate, i.e., $P = 0.01$.

$$Q = -10 \cdot \log_{10}(0.01) = -10 \cdot (-2) = 20$$

Hence $Q = 20$, conventionally written **Q20**, corresponding to **99% accuracy**.

**Example B**: For $P = 0.001$ (0.1% error):

$$Q = -10 \cdot \log_{10}(0.001) = -10 \cdot (-3) = 30$$

Thus $Q = 30$ (**Q30**) corresponds to **99.9% accuracy**, which is the modern production-grade threshold.

### Step 5 — Tabulate the Common Phred-to-Accuracy Mapping

| Phred Score $Q$ | Error Probability $P = 10^{-Q/10}$ | Base Accuracy $(1 - P)$ | Practical Interpretation |
|:---------------:|:----------------------------------:|:-----------------------:|:------------------------|
| 10              | $10^{-1} = 0.1$                    | 90%                     | Unusable; rare in practice |
| 20              | $10^{-2} = 0.01$                   | 99%                     | Legacy Sanger standard |
| 30              | $10^{-3} = 0.001$                  | 99.9%                   | Modern Illumina standard |
| 40              | $10^{-4} = 0.0001$                 | 99.99%                  | Near-perfect; PacBio HiFi territory |

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — ASCII Decoding for Phred+33

### Step 1 — The Storage Constraint

A FASTQ file must store the quality of every base in a single character to minimize disk footprint. ASCII (American Standard Code for Information Interchange) provides 95 printable characters, which is more than enough.

### Step 2 — Reserve the Lower ASCII Range

ASCII codes **0–32** are non-printable control characters. To prevent corruption by text-handling software, the original **Sanger specification** reserved these codes and started the quality characters at **ASCII 33** (the exclamation mark `!`).

### Step 3 — Define the Linear Mapping

Let $c$ be the ASCII code of a quality character. The Phred score is then:

$$Q = \text{ord}(c) - 33$$

### Step 4 — Worked Example

For the FASTQ quality character `'5'` (ASCII code 53):

$$Q = 53 - 33 = 20$$

So the symbol `'5'` represents **Q20**, i.e., a 1% chance the base is wrong. For `'?'` (ASCII 63):

$$Q = 63 - 33 = 30 \quad \text{(Q30, the modern benchmark)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Aid**: The exclamation mark `'!'` always means **Q0** (worst possible). Memorize this single fact and you can decode any Phred+33 FASTQ file by hand.
