## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — How Each QC Metric Detects a Specific Failure Mode

| QC Metric (FastQC Module) | Healthy Profile | Pathological Profile | Likely Root Cause | Remediation |
|---------------------------|-----------------|----------------------|-------------------|-------------|
| Per-base Sequence Quality | Q $\geq$ 30 across all cycles; smooth gradient | Q drops sharply after cycle 100 | Reagent degradation, phasing | TRAILING trim, SLIDINGWINDOW |
| Per-base Sequence Content | Flat A/C/G/T curves, equal proportions | First 10–12 cycles biased; curve then flat | **Bias-free random priming**; library prep anomaly | HEADCROP first 10 bp |
| Per-sequence GC Content | Sharp peak matching theoretical | Broad, shifted, or bimodal peak | Contamination (e.g., bacterial, mycoplasma) | Re-screen with Kraken2 / centrifuge |
| Sequence Duplication Levels | Linear trace with low slope | Exponential rise at high counts | PCR over-amplification, low input | Re-prep; deduplicate cautiously |
| Adapter Content | $\leq 0.1\%$ | Spike at 3′ end | Read length exceeds fragment length | ILLUMINACLIP with TruSeq fasta |
| Overrepresented Sequences | Sparse | A single sequence $>1\%$ of total | Adapter dimers, rRNA, phiX spike-in | Trim, then re-evaluate |
| Per-tile Quality | Uniform colour | Dark stripes (one tile fails) | Illumina flowcell defect | Discard the affected tile's reads |
| Sequence Length Distribution | Single sharp peak | Broad multi-modal | Mixed library prep, degraded input | Re-demultiplex / re-prep |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Insight**: A 14-mark question often asks you to *interpret* a FastQC plot. Memorize the shape of the **healthy** profiles vs. the **pathological** ones for each of the eleven modules. The table above is your exam-day reference.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Compilation Safeguards Applied

All node IDs below are purely alphanumeric and prefixed with letters (e.g., `node1`, `stepA`). Labels contain no markdown formatting tags. Reserved keywords (`end`, `subgraph`, `graph`, `style`) are never used as standalone node IDs.

## 4.2 Master NGS QC-and-Preprocessing Pipeline (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

The diagram below renders the canonical end-to-end workflow as a block-level functional architecture, mapping every component's role without requiring a physical illustration.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0["RAW FASTQ FILE FROM SEQUENCER"] --> B1["STAGE 1 INITIAL QC PROFILING WITH FASTQC"]
    B1 --> B2{"DO ALL FASTQC MODULES PASS"}
    B2 -- NO --> B3["IDENTIFY FAILING MODULE AND FAILURE TYPE"]
    B3 --> B4["SELECT REMEDIATION TOOL FROM LIBRARY"]
    B4 --> B5["TRIMMOMATIC OR CUTADAPT OR FASTP"]
    B2 -- YES --> B6["PROCEED DIRECTLY TO TRIMMING AS BEST PRACTICE"]
    B6 --> C1["STAGE 2 QUALITY TRIMMING AND ADAPTER REMOVAL"]
    C1 --> C2["LEADING TRIM DROP Q BELOW THRESHOLD AT 5 PRIME"]
    C2 --> C3["TRAILING TRIM DROP Q BELOW THRESHOLD AT 3 PRIME"]
    C3 --> C4["SLIDING WINDOW SCAN W BASES MEAN Q BELOW THRESHOLD"]
    C4 --> C5["ILLUMINACLIP OR CUTADAPT REMOVE 3 PRIME ADAPTER"]
    C5 --> D1["STAGE 3 LENGTH FILTER AND PAIR SYNC"]
    D1 --> D2["DISCARD READS SHORTER THAN MINLEN"]
    D2 --> D3{"IS LIBRARY PAIRED END"}
    D3 -- YES --> D4["SYNCHRONIZE READ 1 AND READ 2 DISCARD TOGETHER"]
    D3 -- NO --> D5["OUTPUT SINGLE END CLEANED FASTQ"]
    D4 --> E1["STAGE 4 POST PROCESSING QC VALIDATION"]
    D5 --> E1
    E1 --> B1
    E1 --> F1["FINAL CLEANED FASTQ READY FOR ALIGNMENT ASSEMBLY OR VARIANT CALLING"]
    F1 --> G1["DOWNSTREAM APPLICATION BWA MEM HISAT2 SPADES GATK"]
```

**Reading the Diagram**:

- The **fast loop** `E1 --> B1` is the **iterative re-QC** step — after trimming, the user must rerun FastQC to confirm the green-tick status. Skipping this loop is the single most common error in published pipelines.
- The **branch on B2** distinguishes **best-practice precautionary trimming** (always recommended) from **failure-driven remediation** (when something is visibly wrong).
- The **D3 conditional** enforces the **paired-end synchronization rule** that determines whether downstream alignment tools will succeed.

## 4.2 Tool Ecosystem at a Glance

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1["RAW FASTQ"] --> B1["FASTQC READ ONLY DIAGNOSTIC"]
    A1 --> C1["FASTP ALL IN ONE TRIMMER QC VALIDATOR"]
    A1 --> C2["TRIMMOMATIC INDUSTRY STANDARD TRIMMER"]
    A1 --> C3["CUTADAPT SPECIALIZED ADAPTER REMOVAL"]
    A1 --> C4["FASTX TOOLKIT LEGACY SINGLE END UTILITIES"]
    B1 --> D1["MULTIQC AGGREGATE REPORT ACROSS SAMPLES"]
    C1 --> D1
    C2 --> D1
    C3 --> D1
    D1 --> E1["ANALYST DECIDES NEXT STEP"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Modern Trend**: As of 2024–2025, the tool **fastp** has gained substantial adoption because it executes trimming, adapter removal, and post-trim QC validation in a **single pass** — replacing the legacy three-tool combination of `FastQC + Trimmomatic + FastQC`. KTU examiners occasionally ask students to *compare* these two philosophies.

## 4.3 ASCII Schematic — Paired-End Synchronization

For paired-end libraries, every read in File R1 must have a surviving partner in File R2 of identical orientation and matching read-ID prefix. The synchronization invariant is:

```
BEFORE TRIMMING                AFTER TRIMMING (L_min = 36 bp)
================                ==============================
R1:  [====read X====]          R1:  [===read X===]   (passes)
R2:  [====read X====]          R2:  [==read X==]     (passes)
R1:  [==read Y===]             R1:  DISCARDED         (too short)
R2:  [==read Y===]             R2:  DISCARDED         (synchronized)
```

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake**: Discarding reads independently from R1 and R2. This destroys the insert-length information and corrupts every downstream paired-end aligner (BWA-MEM, Bowtie2, HISAT2).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]`

**Define the Phred quality score $Q$ used in NGS data. State the formula relating $Q$ to the base-calling error probability $P$ and calculate the error probability for a base with $Q = 40$.**

**Model Answer (3 marks)**:

- **Definition (1 mark)**: The Phred quality score $Q$ is a logarithmic measure of base-calling confidence introduced by Ewing and Green (1998), defined as the negative base-10 logarithm of the probability $P$ that the called base is incorrect.
- **Formula (1 mark)**:

$$Q = -10 \cdot \log_{10}(P)$$

- **Calculation for Q = 40 (1 mark)**:

$$P = 10^{-Q/10} = 10^{-40/10} = 10^{-4} = 0.0001$$

Therefore, a base with $Q = 40$ has an error probability of $10^{-4}$ (one in ten thousand), i.e., **99.99% accuracy**. Such scores are characteristic of PacBio HiFi and Oxford Nanopore Q20+ chemistry.

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model Paper]`

**Distinguish between the FASTQ and FASTA formats. Why is FASTQ preferred for raw NGS data?**

**Model Answer (3 marks)**:

- **FASTA (1 mark)**: A two-line-per-sequence text format containing a header line beginning with `>` followed by the raw nucleotide (or protein) sequence. It carries **no per-base quality information**.
- **FASTQ (1 mark)**: A four-line-per-sequence text format that augments FASTA with a second paired line of ASCII-encoded Phred quality scores, one character per base, enabling base-level confidence tracking.
- **Why preferred for NGS (1 mark)**: Modern NGS platforms (Illumina, Ion Torrent, etc.) report per-base confidence alongside the call. FASTQ preserves this essential QC information so that downstream trimming algorithms can distinguish high-confidence bases (Q $\geq$ 30) from low-confidence ones (Q $<$ 20) and excise the latter, which is impossible with FASTA.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern**: Two questions of 14 marks each are set from this module; the student answers **one**. Both choices below carry sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Full step-by-step model solutions are provided.

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted]`

**(a)** Explain the architecture of the FASTQ format with reference to the **Phred+33** (Sanger) encoding scheme. Decode the following 5-character quality string `"5'5+I"` and report the per-base Phred scores. (7 marks)

**(b)** Describe the complete NGS data quality control pipeline. Compare and contrast the tools **FastQC**, **Trimmomatic**, and **Cutadapt** with respect to their function, input, and output. (7 marks)

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Adapted]`

**(a)** Derive the Phred score formula $Q = -10 \log_{10}(P)$ from the axioms of probability and the requirement of a logarithmic accuracy scale. Tabulate the values of $P$ for $Q = 10, 20, 30, 40$. (7 marks)

**(b)** A paired-end Illumina library has been sequenced and the raw FASTQ files show a sharp drop in per-base quality after cycle 150, with **18% adapter contamination** in read 2. Design a complete preprocessing workflow, naming every tool, parameter, and the rationale for each step. (7 marks)

---

## 5.3 Detailed Model Solutions

### Solution to Question A

#### (a) FASTQ Architecture and Phred+33 Decoding (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Architecture (2 marks)**: Each read in FASTQ occupies four lines:

1. `@` header containing the unique read identifier.
2. The raw nucleotide sequence using the alphabet $\{A, C, G, T, N\}$ where $N$ denotes an ambiguous base.
3. `+` separator optionally followed by the read ID.
4. The quality string with one ASCII character per base.

**Step 2 — Phred+33 Encoding (2 marks)**: Under Phred+33 (Sanger, Illumina 1.8+), the printable ASCII character set begins at code 33 (the `!` character). The mapping is:

$$Q = \text{ord}(c) - 33$$

**Step 3 — Decoding `"5'5+I"` (3 marks)**: Retrieve the ASCII code of each character and subtract 33.

| Character | ASCII Code | $Q = \text{ord}(c) - 33$ |
|:---------:|:----------:|:------------------------:|
| `5`       | 53         | $53 - 33 = 20$           |
| `'`       | 39         | $39 - 33 = 6$            |
| `5`       | 53         | $53 - 33 = 20$           |
| `+`       | 43         | $43 - 33 = 10$           |
| `I`       | 73         | $73 - 33 = 40$           |

**Final Answer**: The decoded Phred scores are **{20, 6, 20, 10, 40}**. The third base (Q6, 75% error) is essentially unusable and would be trimmed by any reasonable pipeline.

#### (b) NGS QC Pipeline and Tool Comparison (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Pipeline Stages (3 marks)**:

1. **Initial QC profiling** with **FastQC** (read-only diagnostic).
2. **Iterative trimming** with **Trimmomatic** or **Cutadapt**.
3. **Length filtering and paired-end synchronization**.
4. **Post-trimming QC validation** by re-running FastQC to confirm green-tick status.

**Step 2 — Tool Comparison Table (3 marks)**:

| Tool | Function | Input | Output |
|------|----------|-------|--------|
| **FastQC** | Diagnostic QC report (no modification of data) | FASTQ file(s) | HTML report with 11 modules |
| **Trimmomatic** | Quality trimming, adapter removal, length filtering | FASTQ (single or paired) | Cleaned FASTQ, optional log of trimmed bases |
| **Cutadapt** | Specialized adapter and primer removal via semi-global alignment | FASTQ, adapter FASTA | Adapter-trimmed FASTQ |

**Step 3 — Pipeline Integration (1 mark)**: In a modern KTU-aligned workflow, the chain `FastQC → Trimmomatic → FastQC` is the gold standard. If paired-end data is used, both files must enter Trimmomatic together to preserve synchronization.

---

### Solution to Question B

#### (a) Derivation of $Q = -10 \log_{10}(P)$ (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Axiom (1 mark)**: Let $P$ be the probability of a base-calling error. We seek a function $f(P)$ that increases as $P$ decreases.

**Step 2 — Logarithmic Choice (1 mark)**: Choose $f(P) = a \cdot \log_{10}(1/P) = -a \cdot \log_{10}(P)$ because:

- It is **monotonic** in accuracy.
- A **10-fold** reduction in $P$ produces a **constant additive** change in the score.

**Step 3 — Scaling Constant (1 mark)**: Set $a = 10$ so that a 10× accuracy improvement gives a 10-point score increase, yielding:

$$Q = -10 \cdot \log_{10}(P)$$

**Step 4 — Tabulation (4 marks)**:

| $Q$ | $P = 10^{-Q/10}$ | Accuracy $(1-P)$ |
|:---:|:----------------:|:----------------:|
| 10  | $10^{-1} = 0.1$        | 90%     |
| 20  | $10^{-2} = 0.01$       | 99%     |
| 30  | $10^{-3} = 0.001$      | 99.9%   |
| 40  | $10^{-4} = 0.0001$     | 99.99%  |

#### (b) Preprocessing Workflow Design (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Diagnosis (1 mark)**: The reported symptoms (quality drop after cycle 150, 18% adapter contamination in read 2) indicate that read length exceeds fragment length, leaving a 3′ adapter overhang — a common issue with short-insert libraries.

**Step 2 — Workflow Design (5 marks)**:

| Step | Tool | Parameter | Rationale |
|:----:|:----:|:---------:|:----------|
| 1 | **FastQC** | Default | Establish baseline QC profile |
| 2 | **Trimmomatic (ILLUMINACLIP)** | `TruSeq3-PE.fa:2:30:10` | Remove 3′ adapter with seed mismatch $\leq$ 2, palindrome clip threshold 30, simple clip threshold 10 |
| 3 | **Trimmomatic (TRAILING)** | `TRAILING:20` | Drop bases below Q20 at the 3′ end (targets the cycle-150 drop) |
| 4 | **Trimmomatic (SLIDINGWINDOW)** | `SLIDIDINGWINDOW:4:20` | Trim when mean Q over a 4-base window drops below Q20 |
| 5 | **Trimmomatic (MINLEN)** | `MINLEN:50` | Discard reads shorter than 50 bp |
| 6 | **FastQC (re-run)** | Default | Iterative validation; all 11 modules should now be green |

**Step 3 — Rationale Summary (1 mark)**: Each parameter directly addresses a specific symptom: `ILLUMINACLIP` solves the 18% adapter contamination, `TRAILING` and `SLIDINGWINDOW` solve the cycle-150 quality drop, and `MINLEN` removes degraded short reads. Re-running FastQC is the final quality gate.

---

## 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Lose Marks in This Topic**
>
> 1. **Sign Error in Phred Derivation (–2 marks)**: Writing $Q = 10 \log_{10}(P)$ instead of $Q = -10 \log_{10}(P)$. The negative sign is essential; memorize it.
> 2. **Confusing Phred+33 with Phred+64 (–2 marks)**: Using offset 64 for modern Illumina data. Since 2014, Illumina has standardized on Phred+33; using offset 64 will produce negative or wildly inflated scores.
> 3. **Independent Discard of Paired Reads (–2 marks)**: Trimming R1 and R2 independently. Always pass both files to Trimmomatic with the `-phred33` flag and the `PE` mode.
> 4. **Skipping the Re-QC Step (–1 mark)**: Failing to mention that FastQC must be re-run on the cleaned data. Iterative validation is worth a free mark.
> 5. **Calling Q30 "100% accurate" (–1 mark)**: Q30 is 99.9% accurate, not 100%. Precision matters in numerical answers.

---

## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Phred Score Formula**: $Q = -10 \log_{10}(P)$; memorize the derivation and the table mapping $Q \in \{10, 20, 30, 40\}$ to $P$.
- **FASTQ Format**: Four lines per read; quality string is ASCII-encoded; Phred+33 is the modern default.
- **Decoding Rule**: $Q = \text{ord}(c) - 33$ for Phred+33; `'!'` always means Q0.
- **QC Tool**: **FastQC** produces 11 modules; memorize the healthy vs. pathological profiles of the top six.
- **Trimming Tools**: **Trimmomatic** (industry standard), **Cutadapt** (adapter specialist), **fastp** (modern all-in-one).
- **Key Trimming Modes**: LEADING, TRAILING, SLIDINGWINDOW, MAXINFO, ILLUMINACLIP, MINLEN.
- **Pipeline Order**: FastQC (baseline) → Trim → FastQC (validation). Iterative re-QC is mandatory.
- **Paired-End Rule**: Always pass R1 and R2 together; never discard them independently.
- **Standard Cutoffs**: Q30 for trimming, MINLEN 36–50 bp, adapter contamination threshold ~5%.
- **KTU Hot Questions**: Derivation of $Q = -10 \log_{10}(P)$ (3 marks), interpretation of a FastQC plot (7 marks), complete preprocessing workflow design (7 marks).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Reads Mapping
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT526 - COMPUTATIONAL BIOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Reads Mapping

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Reads Mapping** (also known as *Sequence Alignment to a Reference Genome*) is the foundational computational process in bioinformatics wherein short nucleotide sequences (*reads*), generated by high-throughput sequencing platforms (e.g., Illumina, SOLiD, Ion Torrent), are algorithmically aligned against a known reference genome to identify their most likely chromosomal origin, position, and orientation.

Mathematically, given a reference sequence $R$ of length $L$ and a set of reads $S = \{s_1, s_2, s_3, \dots, s_n\}$ of average length $k$ bases, the goal of a read mapper is to compute, for each $s_i$, the position $p \in [1, L-k+1]$ (or the set of positions) on $R$ such that the alignment score is maximized (or mismatch cost minimized).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (2024 Scheme):** Reads Mapping sits at the intersection of **Module 4 (Sequence Alignment & Genome Assembly)** and is a mandatory pre-requisite topic before *De Novo Assembly*, *Variant Calling*, and *RNA-Seq Quantification*. A student is expected to understand both the algorithmic foundation AND the practical tool landscape (Bowtie, BWA, SOAP).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Book Detective"

Imagine you have a **huge 3-billion-letter book** (the human reference genome, written in the alphabet A, T, G, C) and a **detective** is given thousands of **tiny torn-out fragments** of pages (the reads) from a *copy* of that book. The detective's job is to find out **which chapter, which page, and which line** each fragment originally came from.

But there is a twist — the copy may have:
- **Typo errors** (sequencing errors → mismatches)
- **Missing or extra letters** (insertions/deletions → indels)
- **Fragments from repetitive chapters** (genomic repeats → multi-mapping)
- **Fragments of unknown orientation** (forward vs. reverse complement strand)

The **read mapper** is the detective's magnifying glass and indexing system — a highly optimized search engine that rapidly scans the reference and pinpoints the origin of every fragment.

## 1.3 Core Categories of Reads Mapping

Reads Mapping can be broadly classified into three alignment paradigms:

| Mapping Type | Definition | Use Case |
|--------------|------------|----------|
| **Global Alignment** | Read is aligned end-to-end across the entire reference region (Needleman–Wunsch based) | Short reads, conserved regions, reference-based assembly |
| **Local Alignment** | Only the best-matching substring of the read is aligned to the reference (Smith–Waterman based) | Reads with adapters, chimeric reads, spliced reads (RNA-Seq) |
| **Semi-Global (Overlap)** | Read aligned such that one end matches the reference, but gaps at the ends are allowed | Suffix-prefix overlaps in *De Bruijn* style overlap graphs |

> [!NOTE]
> **Quality Metrics (KTU 2024 Expectation):** A good mapper should report **(a) Mapping Quality (MAPQ)**, **(b) Number of Mismatches**, **(c) Edit Distance**, and **(d) Number of Multi-mapping Positions**. MAPQ is encoded in **Phred-scaled** quality: $\text{MAPQ} = -10 \log_{10}(P(\text{wrong position}))$.

## 1.4 The Three Pillars of a Read Mapper

Every modern read mapper rests on three architectural pillars:

1. **Indexing** — Pre-processing the reference (or reads) to enable sub-linear time search. Common indices: **Hash Tables**, **Suffix Trees/Arrays**, **FM-Index** (Burrows-Wheeler Transform-based).
2. **Seeding** — Breaking the read/query into short *k-mers* (seeds) and locating their exact matches in the index.
3. **Extension/Smith-Waterman** — Verifying seeds by extending alignment into full read-level matches with gap and mismatch handling.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Pipeline — Step-by-Step Logic

The Reads Mapping process, regardless of the underlying algorithm, follows this canonical pipeline:

### Step 1: Quality Control & Preprocessing
- Input: Raw FASTQ files containing reads with per-base quality scores.
- Action: Adapter trimming, quality filtering (Phred score threshold $\geq Q20$).
- Output: Cleaned FASTQ files.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Phred Quality Score Formula:**
> $$Q = -10 \log_{10}(P_e)$$
> where $P_e$ is the probability of base-calling error. A **Q20** score implies $P_e = 0.01$ (**99% accuracy**), and **Q30** implies $P_e = 0.001$ (**99.9% accuracy**).

### Step 2: Index Construction (Reference-side)
- The reference genome $R$ is indexed into a data structure $I$ that supports fast substring lookup.
- Complexity differs drastically between indexing strategies.

### Step 3: Seed Generation
- Each read of length $k$ is broken into one or more **seeds** of length $m$ (where $m < k$).
- Two dominant seeding strategies:
  * **Continuous Seeding:** Single contiguous seed of length $m$.
  * **Spaced Seeding (Pattern Matching):** A binary pattern of length $m$ defines *match* and *don't-care* positions. Example: `1110100111001111111` (PatternHunter).
  * **k-mer (Sub-sampling) Seeding:** Extract all $k$-mers of length $m$ from the read (e.g., $(k - m + 1)$ seeds).

### Step 4: Seed Lookup via the Index
- Each seed is queried against the index $I$ to retrieve candidate positions on the reference.
- **Exact matches** are stored; mismatches are accommodated by *extension* in Step 5.

### Step 5: Seed Extension & Verification
- Candidate positions are extended into full alignments using:
  * **Hamming distance extension** (mismatch-tolerant, no indels) — fast.
  * **Smith-Waterman** (gap-tolerant, dynamic programming) — slow but accurate.
  * **Band Smith-Waterman** (DP restricted to a diagonal band) — best trade-off.

### Step 6: Best-Match Selection & Output
- The alignment with the **lowest edit distance** (or highest score) is reported.
- Output is typically in **SAM (Sequence Alignment Map)** format or its binary version **BAM**.

## 2.2 Algorithmic Classes of Read Mappers

### Class A: Hash-Based (Prefix Lookup)
- **Mechanism:** Hash the $k$-mers of the *reference* (or *reads*) into a hash table mapping $k$-mer $\to$ position list.
- **Examples:** **MAQ** (Mapping and Assembly with Quality), **SOAP** (Short Oligonucleotide Analysis Package), **Stampy**, **NovoAlign**, **MOSAIK**.
- **Time Complexity:** $O(N \cdot k)$ for index build (where $N$ = reference length), $O(M)$ per query.
- **Memory Footprint:** High (entire reference $k$-mer table in RAM).

### Class B: Suffix Tree / Suffix Array
- **Mechanism:** Construct a *suffix tree* (or array) of the reference. Each read (or its seeds) is searched as a prefix of some suffix.
- **Examples:** **MUMmer**, **Vmatch**, **SEGEMEHL**, **Eland** (uses hashing, but conceptually related).
- **Time Complexity:** $O(k)$ per query after $O(N)$ index build.
- **Memory Footprint:** $\approx 12N$ bytes for suffix array (for human genome $\approx 36$ GB) — memory bottleneck.

### Class C: FM-Index / Burrows-Wheeler Transform (BWA, Bowtie family)
- **Mechanism:** Use the **Burrows-Wheeler Transform (BWT)** + **Suffix Array (SA) sampling** + **Occurrence array (Occ)** + **Rank array** to enable *exact* and *approximate* matching in $O(k \cdot \log\sigma)$ time per query (where $\sigma = 4$ for DNA).
- **Examples:** **Bowtie**, **Bowtie2**, **BWA**, **BWA-MEM**, **BWA-SW**, **BWA-ALN**.
- **Time Complexity:** $O(k \cdot \log\sigma)$ per query.
- **Memory Footprint:** $\approx 2$–$4$ GB for the human genome (highly efficient).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** The Burrows-Wheeler Transform **BWT(R)** is a reversible permutation of the reference string $R$. The BWT is **NOT** itself a compression algorithm — it is a *rearrangement* that groups identical characters together, making it amenable to run-length encoding and FM-indexing.

### Class D: Hybrid / Multi-Index Mappers
- **Examples:** **STAR** (spliced RNA mapper using uncompressed suffix arrays), **HISAT2** (uses Hierarchical Graph FM-index, **HGFM**), **GMAP**, **BLAT** (BLAST-Like Alignment Tool), **minimap2**.
- **Use Case:** Long reads (PacBio, Oxford Nanopore), spliced alignments, cross-species mapping.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Definition | Variables | Units |
|---------|----------------------|-----------|-------|
| Phred Quality Score | $Q = -10 \log_{10}(P_e)$ | $P_e$ = base-calling error probability | dimensionless |
| Mapping Quality (MAPQ) | $\text{MAPQ} = -10 \log_{10}(P_{\text{wrong}})$ | $P_{\text{wrong}}$ = prob. of incorrect mapping | Phred-scaled integer |
| Hamming Distance | $d_H = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \mathbb{1}[s_i \neq r_i]$ | Mismatches per read | bases |
| Edit Distance (Levenshtein) | $d_E = \min(\text{ins, del, sub})$ | Operations to convert $s$ to $r$ | bases |
| Coverage Depth | $C = \frac{N \cdot L}{G}$ | $N$ = reads, $L$ = read length, $G$ = genome size | × coverage |
| BWT Construction | $B[i] = R[SA[i] - 1]$ | $SA$ = suffix array of $R$ | character |
| FM-Index Backward Search | $\text{sp}(c, i) = C(c) + \text{Occ}(c, i)$ | Range update per character | integer range |
| Smith-Waterman Recursion | $H_{i,j} = \max\begin{cases} 0 \\ H_{i-1,j-1} + s(a_i,b_j) \\ H_{i-1,j} - g \\ H_{i,j-1} - g \end{cases}$ | $s$ = substitution, $g$ = gap penalty | score |
| Needleman-Wunsch Global | $F_{i,j} = \max\begin{cases} F_{i-1,j-1} + s(a_i,b_j) \\ F_{i-1,j} - g \\ F_{i,j-1} - g \end{cases}$ | Same as above | score |
| Suffix Array Build (SA-IS) | $O(N)$ time, $O(N)$ space | $N$ = reference length | characters |
| Seed Length (Sensitivity) | Larger $m \Rightarrow$ specificity $\uparrow$, sensitivity $\downarrow$ | $m$ = seed length | bases |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Reads Mapping is not merely an academic exercise — it underpins:
- **Clinical Genomics:** Identifying disease-causing variants (BRCA1/2 screening).
- **Cancer Genomics:** Detecting somatic mutations in tumor-normal pairs.
- **RNA-Seq:** Quantifying gene expression (FPKM, TPM).
- **ChIP-Seq / ATAC-Seq:** Mapping protein-DNA interactions and chromatin accessibility.
- **Metagenomics:** Taxonomic classification of microbial communities.
- **Pipelines:** GATK, BWA-MEM, samtools, Picard — the standard clinical/research stack.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: The Burrows-Wheeler Transform (BWT)

This is one of the **most frequently asked derivations** in KTU board exams for Computational Biology.

### Setup
Let the reference sequence be $R = \text{``GATTACA''}$ with a sentinel terminator `$` (lexicographically smallest character).

### Step 1: Form All Cyclic Rotations
Generate all cyclic shifts of $R$:

```
Rotation 0:  G A T T A C A $
Rotation 1:  A T T A C A $ G
Rotation 2:  T T A C A $ G A
Rotation 3:  T A C A $ G A T
Rotation 4:  A C A $ G A T T
Rotation 5:  C A $ G A T T A
Rotation 6:  A $ G A T T A C
Rotation 7:  $ G A T T A C A
```

> **Conversion Logic:** We append `$` to the end and cyclically rotate; the `$` ensures every rotation is unique (no ambiguity for repeated characters).

### Step 2: Lexicographic Sorting
Sort the rotations in alphabetical order:

```
Sorted:
$ G A T T A C A
A $ G A T T A C
A C A $ G A T T
A T T A C A $ G
C A $ G A T T A
G A T T A C A $
T A C A $ G A T
T T A C A $ G A
```

### Step 3: Extract Last Column
The **BWT** is the last column of the sorted rotation matrix:

$$
\text{BWT}(R) = \text{``ACA\$GTTA''}
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** Each character in BWT is the character that *precedes* the suffix starting at the corresponding suffix array position. Formally:

$$
\text{BWT}[i] = R[(\text{SA}[i] - 1) \bmod (|R| + 1)]
$$

### Step 4: The LF-Mapping (Last-to-First)
The LF-mapping is the cornerstone of BWT-based search. It states that **the $i$-th occurrence of a character in the Last column corresponds to the $i$-th occurrence of the same character in the First column**, and the suffix array position is preserved.

$$
\text{LF}(i) = C(c) + \text{Occ}(c, i)
$$

where:
- $c = \text{BWT}[i]$,
- $C(c) = $ cumulative count of characters alphabetically less than $c$ in $R$,
- $\text{Occ}(c, i) = $ number of occurrences of $c$ in $\text{BWT}[1..i]$.

> **Conversion Logic:** LF-mapping allows us to "walk" the suffix array by reading the BWT, enabling $O(k)$ exact matching of a $k$-length pattern without storing the full suffix array.

### Step 5: Backward Search Algorithm
To find all occurrences of a pattern $P$ of length $m$ in $R$:

Initialize the suffix array range: $i = 1$, $j = n$ (where $n = |R| + 1$).
For each character $c = P[m], P[m-1], \dots, P[1]$ (right-to-left):
- $i \leftarrow C(c) + \text{Occ}(c, i - 1) + 1$
- $j \leftarrow C(c) + \text{Occ}(c, j)$
- If $i > j$, stop (pattern not found).
- Else continue with the next character.

Final range $[i, j]$ contains all suffix array positions where $P$ occurs.

> **Conversion Logic:** Backward search exploits the *suffix* property: if a string $cP$ occurs in $R$, then $P$ also occurs, and we can update the SA range in $O(1)$ per character using the FM-index.

## 3.2 Worked Example: Smith-Waterman Local Alignment (Simplified)

Align the read $s = \text{``GATC''}$ to reference fragment $r = \text{``GATGC''}$ with match $= +2$, mismatch $= -1$, gap $= -2$.

### Scoring Matrix Computation

We compute $H[i,j]$ (the local alignment DP matrix):

|   |   | G | A | T | G | C |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
|   | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| G | 0 | **2** | 1 | 0 | 2 | 1 |
| A | 0 | 1 | **4** | 3 | 2 | 1 |
| T | 0 | 0 | 3 | **6** | 5 | 4 |
| C | 0 | 0 | 2 | 5 | 5 | **7** |

> **Conversion Logic (per cell):**
> - Top-left diagonal: $H_{i-1,j-1} + s(s_i, r_j)$ — substitution.
> - Top: $H_{i-1,j} - g$ — deletion.
> - Left: $H_{i,j-1} - g$ — insertion.
> - Self: $0$ — local alignment floor (unlike NW which uses no zero).
> - **Max** is taken across all options and zero.

### Traceback (Local — Stops at Zero)
The optimal local alignment ends at $(4,5)$ with score $7$:

- $H[4,5] = 7 \rightarrow H[3,4] = 5$ (diagonal, match C-G = +2) $\rightarrow H[2,3] = 3$ (diagonal, match T-T = +2) $\rightarrow H[1,2] = 1$ (top, gap) — stops.

**Optimal Alignment:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Read:}    & \quad \text{``GATC''} \\
\text{Reference:} & \quad \text{``G\_ATC''}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** The traceback begins at the matrix maximum and walks back until a $0$ is encountered (local stop). The cell containing the local maximum may lie anywhere in the matrix, not necessarily at the bottom-right.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix of Major Mappers

| Mapper | Index Type | Seeding | Read Type | Indels | Speed (Illumina) | Memory (Human) |
|--------|-----------|---------|-----------|--------|------------------|----------------|
| **MAQ** | Hash (reads) | 8 bp exact | Short (≤ 63 bp) | No | Medium | ~1 GB |
| **SOAP** | Hash (reference) | 11–17 bp | Short | No | Fast | ~14 GB |
| **Bowtie1** | FM-Index | Exact $k$-mers, backtracking | Short (≤ 50 bp) | No | Very fast | ~2.3 GB |
| **Bowtie2** | FM-Index | Variable-length, gapped | Short (50–1000 bp) | Yes | Fast | ~3.2 GB |
| **BWA-ALN** | FM-Index | Exact, edit distance | Short | Yes | Fast | ~2.7 GB |
| **BWA-MEM** | FM-Index | Super-maximal exact matches (SMEMs) | Short & long | Yes | Fast | ~3.1 GB |
| **BWA-SW** | FM-Index | Smith-Waterman extension | Long | Yes | Slow | ~4 GB |
| **STAR** | Suffix Array (uncompressed) | Maximal Mappable Prefixes (MMP) | RNA-Seq, spliced | Yes | Very fast | ~30 GB |
| **HISAT2** | Hierarchical GF-Index (HGFM) | $k$-mer + graph FM | RNA-Seq, spliced | Yes | Fast | ~5 GB |
| **minimap2** | FM-Index + minimizers | Minimizer chaining | Long (ONT, PacBio) | Yes | Very fast | ~4 GB |
| **GMAP** | Genomic Suffix Array / BWT | Longest-match first | cDNA, EST, long | Yes | Medium | ~6 GB |
| **BLAT** | Non-overlapping $k$-mer index | 4/11-mer pairs | EST, mRNA, long | Yes | Very fast | <1 GB |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: A Modern Read Mapper

## 4.2 Indexing Strategy Decision Tree

```mermaid

    classDef qNode fill:#FFEBEE,stroke:#C62828,color:#000
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Question 1
**Q: Define the term "Reads Mapping" in computational biology. Differentiate between global and local alignment in the context of read mapping.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**
**Reads Mapping** is the process of aligning short DNA sequences (reads) generated by sequencing platforms to a known reference genome to determine their precise genomic origin, position, and orientation.

| Feature | Global Alignment | Local Alignment |
|---------|------------------|------------------|
| **Algorithm Basis** | Needleman–Wunsch | Smith–Waterman |
| **Scope** | End-to-end of both sequences | Best internal substring |
| **Boundary Handling** | Penalty for end gaps | Zero-floor (no penalty at ends) |
| **Use Case** | Highly similar sequences | Divergent / partial sequences |
| **Computational Cost** | $O(nm)$ | $O(nm)$ (same) |
| **Example in Mapping** | Conservative gene mapping | Spliced / chimeric read mapping |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution:** Definition (1 mark), Global explanation (1 mark), Local explanation (1 mark).

### Question 2
**Q: What is an FM-Index? Explain its role in modern short-read aligners such as Bowtie and BWA.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**
The **FM-Index** (Full-text Minute-space Index) is a compressed, searchable index of a text (reference genome) built using the **Burrows-Wheeler Transform (BWT)**, a **Suffix Array (SA) sample**, and **Occurrence (Occ) and C(c) auxiliary arrays**. It enables exact and approximate pattern matching in **$O(m \cdot \log\sigma)$** time per query of length $m$ (with $\sigma = 4$ for DNA), using only **$O(N)$** bits of memory.

**Role in Bowtie and BWA:**
- **Bowtie1 & BWA-ALN** use FM-Index for **ungapped short-read alignment** with backtracking to permit limited mismatches.
- **BWA-MEM** combines FM-Index with **Super-Maximal Exact Matches (SMEMs)** seeding and seed-extension via banded Smith–Waterman, supporting **gapped and long-read alignment**.
- The memory footprint of FM-indexed mappers is $\approx 2$–$4$ GB for the human genome, **$10\times$ smaller** than hash-based or suffix-array mappers.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution:** FM-Index definition (1 mark), Components (BWT/SA/Occ/C) (1 mark), Role in Bowtie/BWA (1 mark).

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each) — With Internal Choice

> **KTU 2024 Scheme ESE Convention:** Each Part B question has sub-parts **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. Students answer **one full question** out of an internal choice pair.

---

### **Question A: (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: (a)** Explain the Burrows-Wheeler Transform (BWT) with a suitable example. Describe its application in read mapping. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer for (a):**
*(Step 1) Definition of BWT (1 mark):*
The Burrows-Wheeler Transform is a reversible rearrangement of a string that groups identical characters together. It is the foundation of the FM-Index.

*(Step 2) Construction with Example (4 marks):*
Let $R = \text{"GATTACA"}$ with sentinel `$`.

Form cyclic rotations and sort lexicographically:

```
$ G A T T A C A
A $ G A T T A C
A C A $ G A T T
A T T A C A $ G
C A $ G A T T A
G A T T A C A $
T A C A $ G A T
T T A C A $ G A
```

The BWT is the last column: **`ACA$GTTA`**.

*(Step 3) Application in read mapping (2 marks):*
In read mappers (Bowtie, BWA), BWT enables **backward search** to find exact matches of a read in $O(m)$ time. The LF-Mapping property $\text{LF}(i) = C(c) + \text{Occ}(c, i)$ walks the suffix array via BWT without storing it, dramatically reducing memory from $\sim 12N$ bytes (suffix array) to $\sim N$ bits.

**Q: (b)** Compare and contrast hash-based, suffix-array, and FM-Index based read mapping algorithms. Cite one popular tool for each category. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer for (b):**
| Criterion | Hash-Based | Suffix Array | FM-Index |
|-----------|-----------|--------------|----------|
| **Index Construction Time** | $O(N \cdot k)$ | $O(N)$ (SA-IS) | $O(N)$ |
| **Memory (Human Genome)** | ~10–15 GB | ~36 GB | ~2–4 GB |
| **Query Time** | $O(k)$ | $O(k)$ | $O(k \cdot \log\sigma)$ |
| **Indel Support** | Limited (extension) | Yes (full) | Yes (backtracking/SW) |
| **Tool Example** | SOAP, MAQ | MUMmer, STAR | Bowtie2, BWA-MEM |
| **Strength** | Simple, fast exact | Provably optimal, gapped | Compressed, scalable |
| **Weakness** | Memory hungry, indel-blind | Memory prohibitive | Approximate matching complex |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Tip:** Present the table on the answer sheet rather than in paragraph form. Marks are awarded for **clear comparative dimensions** (memory, speed, accuracy) and **correct tool names**.

---

### **Question B: (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` (Internal Choice Alternative)

**Q: (a)** Describe the complete architecture of a modern short-read mapper with reference to BWA-MEM. Explain the role of seeding, indexing, and extension. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer for (a):**
*(1) Preprocessing and Indexing (2 marks):*
BWA-MEM first builds the **FM-Index** of the reference genome using BWT, Suffix Array sampling, and Occ/C arrays. The index occupies ~3.1 GB for the human genome and supports backward search.

*(2) Seeding via Super-Maximal Exact Matches (2 marks):*
For each read, BWA-MEM identifies **SMEMs** — the longest exact matches between a read substring and the reference, which are *not* subsumed by a longer match. SMEMs serve as high-confidence seed anchors. **Chaining** then orders SMEMs by reference position to filter spurious seeds.

*(3) Extension and Banded Smith–Waterman (2 marks):*
Chained SMEMs are extended in both directions using **banded affine-gap Smith–Waterman** to handle indels. The band width is typically $w = 50$ cells.

*(4) MAPQ Estimation and Output (1 mark):*
The mapper computes **MAPQ** from the score of the best vs. second-best alignment, then writes a **SAM record** for the optimal hit.

**Q: (b)** Discuss the challenges of read mapping — repeats, indels, sequencing errors, and multi-mapping. For each, suggest a strategy used in modern mappers to mitigate it. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer for (b):**
| Challenge | Description | Mitigation Strategy |
|-----------|-------------|---------------------|
| **Repetitive Regions** | Identical $k$-mers at multiple loci (transposons, segmental duplications) | Multi-mapping reporting (BWA `XA` tag); use of SMEMs to disambiguate; MAPQ down-weighting |
| **Indels** | Small insertions/deletions from sequencing or real variants | Affine-gap Smith–Waterman; BWA-MEM's banded SW; GATK IndelRealigner post-processing |
| **Sequencing Errors** | Mis-called bases (rate ~0.1–1% for Illumina, ~10–15% for Nanopore) | Quality-aware seed extension (MAQ); minimizer-based noise reduction (minimap2); consensus polishing |
| **Multi-Mapping Reads** | Reads that align equally well to multiple loci | MAPQ = 0 assignment; "best score" reporting with `NH:i:n` tag; EM-based allocation in RNA-Seq quantification (RSEM, Salmon) |
| **Read Truncation / Adapters** | Partial reads with adapter contamination | Adapter trimming (Cutadapt, Trimmomatic); local alignment (Smith–Waterman) |
| **Strand Bias** | Reads from antisense strand | Allow reverse-complement alignment (BWA flags) |
| **Coverage Bias** | GC-rich/poor regions under-sequenced | Downstream normalization; duplicate marking (Picard) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout — KTU Valuation Pattern:**
> 1. **Avoid vague terms:** "Read mapping is aligning reads to a genome" (1 mark only). Always specify *indexing strategy* and *alignment algorithm*.
> 2. **BWT ≠ compression:** Many students wrongly claim BWT is a *lossless compression algorithm*. It is a **rearrangement**; compression is a *secondary effect*.
> 3. **MAPQ vs. Phred base quality:** Do not confuse base-level Phred $Q$ with mapping-level MAPQ. They are distinct Phred-scaled metrics.
> 4. **Hash vs. FM-Index memory:** A common error is stating hash tables are *smaller* than FM-Indexes. The opposite is true: hash tables dominate RAM.
> 5. **Tool-version awareness:** If the question specifies BWA, mention *which sub-command* — BWA-ALN (short, ungapped), BWA-SW (long, gapped), or BWA-MEM (most general, gapped, long).
> 6. **Diagrams earn marks:** A labelled block diagram of the BWT construction or a mapper pipeline typically secures 1–2 extra marks.
> 7. **Numerical coverage:** If asked to compute coverage $C = \frac{NL}{G}$, show the substitution of all three values. Do not write the formula alone.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist**

- [ ] **Reads Mapping** = alignment of sequencing reads to a reference genome; outputs positions, CIGAR, MAPQ, optional tags.
- [ ] **Three algorithmic families:** Hash-based (SOAP, MAQ), Suffix Array (MUMmer, STAR), FM-Index (Bowtie, BWA).
- [ ] **BWT** is a *rearrangement*, not compression. It groups similar characters and is the foundation of FM-Index.
- [ ] **LF-Mapping** $\text{LF}(i) = C(c) + \text{Occ}(c, i)$ allows suffix array traversal in $O(1)$ per character.
- [ ] **Backward search** finds exact matches in $O(m)$ time by iterating pattern right-to-left.
- [ ] **SMEMs** (Super-Maximal Exact Matches) are the seeding primitive of BWA-MEM.
- [ ] **MAPQ** is Phred-scaled: $\text{MAPQ} = -10 \log_{10}(P_{\text{wrong}})$.
- [ ] **Phred Q-score:** $Q = -10 \log_{10}(P_e)$ — Q20 = 99%, Q30 = 99.9% accuracy.
- [ ] **Smith–Waterman** = local alignment with zero-floor; **Needleman–Wunsch** = global, no zero-floor.
- [ ] **Hash-based mappers** are memory-heavy but simple; **FM-Index mappers** are memory-efficient (~2–4 GB) but technically intricate.
- [ ] **STAR** uses *uncompressed suffix array* for ultra-fast RNA-Seq mapping; **HISAT2** uses *Hierarchical Graph FM-Index* for memory savings.
- [ ] **minimap2** uses *minimizer chaining* and is the de-facto standard for long reads (ONT, PacBio, HiFi).
- [ ] **SAM** = human-readable alignment; **BAM** = binary, indexed (BAI/CSI); **CRAM** = reference-based compression.
- [ ] **CIGAR string** codes operations: `M` (match/mismatch), `I` (insertion to ref), `D` (deletion from ref), `S` (soft-clip), `H` (hard-clip), `N` (skipped intron).
- [ ] **Common challenges:** repeats, indels, errors, multi-mapping — each has a dedicated mitigation in modern mappers.
- [ ] **BWA-MEM** is the KTU-recommended default short-read aligner; **minimap2** for long reads; **STAR** for RNA-Seq.
- [ ] **Coverage formula:** $C = \frac{N \cdot L}{G}$ — $N$ = read count, $L$ = read length, $G$ = genome size.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mapping Approaches and Algorithms
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT526 - COMPUTATIONAL BIOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Mapping Approaches and Algorithms

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mapping (Sequence Mapping) Approaches** in computational biology are formal algorithmic procedures used to align, compare, and identify regions of similarity between two or more biological sequences (DNA, RNA, or protein). These approaches produce an optimal mathematical correspondence between residues using **dynamic programming**, **heuristic search**, or **probabilistic models**, evaluated under a defined scoring scheme.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, mapping approaches fall into two broad paradigms:
- **Exact (Optimal) Methods** — Based on dynamic programming (DP) guaranteeing the mathematically optimal alignment (e.g., Needleman–Wunsch, Smith–Waterman).
- **Heuristic (Approximate) Methods** — Sacrifice optimality for speed using word/k-tuple seeding and statistical filtering (e.g., **BLAST**, **FASTA**).

A third category, **Multiple Sequence Alignment (MSA)**, extends pairwise mapping to $N \geq 3$ sequences using progressive, iterative, or consistency-based algorithms.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy — The "Spot the Differences" Puzzle:**
> Imagine you have two long newspapers — one from 2024 and one from 2025. You suspect an article was copy-pasted from the 2024 paper into the 2025 paper, but with a few words changed, a sentence deleted, and a paragraph moved. To find it, you scan both side-by-side. This is *sequence mapping*. The DP-based methods are like a meticulous editor who tries *every* possible way to slide the papers together and picks the best fit. The heuristic methods (BLAST/FASTA) are like a speed-reader who first looks for short, identical phrases ("common words") and only zooms into those regions. Both aim to find similarity, but they trade off accuracy for speed.

## 1.3 Key Metrics & Standard Constants

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Value (BLAST default) |
|---|---|---|
| Word size (DNA) | $W$ | **11** |
| Word size (Protein) | $W$ | **3** |
| Substitution matrix | — | **BLOSUM62** |
| Gap open penalty | $g_{open}$ | $-11$ (protein) / $-5$ (DNA) |
| Gap extend penalty | $g_{extend}$ | $-1$ |
| Expectation value cutoff | $E$ | **10** (default) |
| Match score | $s_{match}$ | $+1$ |
| Mismatch score | $s_{mismatch}$ | $-1$ |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Framework of Mapping

Mapping algorithms operate on the principle of **maximizing a similarity score** (or minimizing a distance). The core components are:

1. **Substitution/Scoring Matrix** $S(a, b)$ — rewards matches, penalizes mismatches.
2. **Gap Penalty Function** $G(k)$ — penalizes insertions/deletions of length $k$.
3. **Search/Recurrence Engine** — explores the alignment space.

### 2.1.1 Affine Gap Penalty Model (Industry Standard)

The most widely used gap model in modern aligners (BLAST, ClustalW, Bowtie) is the **affine gap penalty**:

$$G(k) = g_{open} + (k - 1) \cdot g_{extend}$$

where $g_{open}$ is the cost to *start* a gap, and $g_{extend}$ is the cost to *extend* it by one residue. Affine penalties biologically model the fact that a single mutational event (e.g., a frameshift) is more likely than many independent single-base indels.

### 2.1.2 Why "Affine" and Not "Linear"?

A **linear gap penalty** $G(k) = -k \cdot g$ treats each inserted/deleted residue as independent, encouraging many small gaps. A real sequence alignment benefits from **one long gap** representing a single evolutionary insertion. The affine model achieves this by making the first residue of a gap expensive ($g_{open}$) and subsequent residues cheap ($g_{extend} \ll g_{open}$).

## 2.2 The Three-State Dynamic Programming Recurrence

For affine gaps, the DP matrix is decomposed into three coupled matrices $M, X, Y$:

$$
M_{i,j} = \max \begin{cases} M_{i-1,j-1} + S(a_i, b_j) \\ X_{i-1,j-1} + S(a_i, b_j) \\ Y_{i-1,j-1} + S(a_i, b_j) \end{cases}
$$

$$
X_{i,j} = \max \begin{cases} M_{i-1,j} + g_{open} \\ X_{i-1,j} + g_{extend} \end{cases}
$$

$$
Y_{i,j} = \max \begin{cases} M_{i,j-1} + g_{open} \\ Y_{i,j-1} + g_{extend} \end{cases}
$$

where:
- $M_{i,j}$ = best score ending in a **match/mismatch** at $(i, j)$
- $X_{i,j}$ = best score ending with a **gap in sequence B** (deletion from A)
- $Y_{i,j}$ = best score ending with a **gap in sequence A** (insertion into A)

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet for KTU 2024 ESE**

| # | Concept | Formula | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Simple similarity score | $S = \sum s(a_i, b_i)$ | Foundation of all DP aligners |
| 2 | Linear gap penalty | $G(k) = -k \cdot g$ | Simple, fast; used in toy tools |
| 3 | Affine gap penalty | $G(k) = g_{open} + (k-1) \cdot g_{extend}$ | Standard in BLAST, ClustalW |
| 4 | Identity fraction | $\%ID = \frac{\text{matches}}{\text{alignment length}} \times 100$ | Quality metric for BLAST output |
| 5 | E-value (Karlin-Altschul) | $E = K \cdot m \cdot n \cdot e^{-\lambda S}$ | Statistical significance in DB search |
| 6 | Bit score | $S' = \frac{\lambda S - \ln K}{\ln 2}$ | Length-independent score |
| 7 | Needleman-Wunsch (global) | $F_{i,j} = \max(F_{i-1,j-1} + S, F_{i-1,j} + G, F_{i,j-1} + G)$ | Full-length alignment |
| 8 | Smith-Waterman (local) | $F_{i,j} = \max(0, F_{i-1,j-1} + S, \ldots)$ | Sub-region similarity |
| 9 | Sum-of-Pairs (MSA) | $S_{SP} = \sum_{i < j} s(A_i, A_j)$ | Objective function for MSA |
| 10 | SP-score (normalized) | $S_{norm} = \frac{S_{SP} - S_{min}}{S_{max} - S_{min}}$ | Benchmarking metric |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL Markdown Syntax Rule:** In the table above, absolute values and conditional pieces have been written with spaces or the words "min/max" — **never use the literal `|` character inside any table cell**, as it breaks the KTU-Markdown rendering engine.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Personalized Medicine (NGS pipelines):** Read-mapping algorithms like **BWA-MEM** and **Bowtie2** are direct descendants of Smith-Waterman, optimized for short-read alignment against a reference genome (GRCh38, ~3.2 billion bp).
- **Drug Discovery (Homology Modeling):** BLAST searches identify evolutionarily related protein templates whose known 3D structure is used to model a query protein.
- **Phylogenetics & Epidemiology:** MSA tools (MUSCLE, Clustal Omega) underpin pathogen surveillance (e.g., tracking SARS-CoV-2 spike-protein mutations).
- **Forensics (DNA Fingerprinting):** Mapping short tandem repeats relies on the same alignment primitives.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Needleman-Wunsch Global Alignment — Full Worked Derivation

### 3.1.1 Problem Setup
Align two short sequences:
- Sequence A = `GATTACA` (length $m = 7$)
- Sequence B = `GCATGCU` (length $n = 7$)

Scoring:
- Match: $+1$
- Mismatch: $-1$
- Gap: $-2$ (linear, $g = 2$)

Recurrence:

$$
F_{i,j} = \max \begin{cases} F_{i-1,j-1} + S(A_i, B_j) \\ F_{i-1,j} + G \\ F_{i,j-1} + G \end{cases}
$$

with $F_{0,0} = 0$, $F_{i,0} = i \cdot G$, $F_{0,j} = j \cdot G$.

### 3.1.2 Initialization (Row 0 and Column 0)

$$
F_{i,0} = -2i, \quad F_{0,j} = -2j
$$

Concretely: $F_{0,0}=0$, $F_{1,0}=-2$, $F_{2,0}=-4$, $F_{3,0}=-6$, $F_{4,0}=-8$, $F_{5,0}=-10$, $F_{6,0}=-12$, $F_{7,0}=-14$. Symmetrically for $F_{0,j}$.

### 3.1.3 Filling the DP Matrix

We denote the indices. Let A = G(1) A(2) T(3) T(4) A(5) C(6) A(7) and B = G(1) C(2) A(3) T(4) G(5) C(6) U(7).

**Step (1,1):** A=G, B=G → match. 
Diagonal: $F_{0,0} + 1 = 1$. 
Up: $F_{0,1} + (-2) = -4$. 
Left: $F_{1,0} + (-2) = -4$. 
$\Rightarrow F_{1,1} = 1$.

**Step (1,2):** A=G, B=C → mismatch $-1$.
Diagonal: $F_{0,1} + (-1) = -3$.
Up: $F_{0,2} + (-2) = -4$.
Left: $F_{1,1} + (-2) = -1$.
$\Rightarrow F_{1,2} = \max(-3, -4, -1) = -1$.

**Step (1,3):** A=G, B=A → mismatch $-1$.
Diagonal: $F_{0,2} + (-1) = -5$.
Up: $F_{0,3} + (-2) = -6$.
Left: $F_{1,2} + (-2) = -3$.
$\Rightarrow F_{1,3} = -3$.

**Step (1,4):** A=G, B=T → mismatch $-1$.
Diagonal: $F_{0,3} + (-1) = -7$.
Up: $F_{0,4} + (-2) = -8$.
Left: $F_{1,3} + (-2) = -5$.
$\Rightarrow F_{1,4} = -5$.

Continuing the recurrence row-by-row, the final completed matrix is:

| | - | G | C | A | T | G | C | U |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **-** | 0 | -2 | -4 | -6 | -8 | -10 | -12 | -14 |
| **G** | -2 | **1** | -1 | -3 | -5 | -7 | -9 | -11 |
| **A** | -4 | -1 | **0** | **0** | -2 | -4 | -6 | -8 |
| **T** | -6 | -3 | -2 | -1 | **1** | -1 | -3 | -5 |
| **T** | -8 | -5 | -4 | -3 | 0 | **0** | -2 | -4 |
| **A** | -10 | -7 | -6 | -3 | -2 | -1 | -1 | **-3** |
| **C** | -12 | -9 | -6 | -5 | -4 | -3 | 0 | **-2** |
| **A** | -14 | -11 | -8 | -5 | -6 | -5 | -2 | **-1** |

### 3.1.4 Traceback from $F_{7,7} = -1$

We trace back using the rule: if diagonal was the max, take it; if up, insert a gap in B; if left, insert a gap in A. Ties are broken alphabetically (or any consistent convention; KTU answer scripts should *state* the rule).

Starting at $(7, 7)$, value $-1$:
- Diagonal candidate: $F_{6,6} + S(A,C) = 0 + (-1) = -1$. Up: $F_{6,7} + (-2) = -4$. Left: $F_{7,6} + (-2) = -4$. $\Rightarrow$ Diagonal. Emit `(A, C)`.

At $(6, 6)$, value $0$:
- Diagonal: $F_{5,5} + S(C, C) = -1 + 1 = 0$. Up/Left: $-3$. $\Rightarrow$ Diagonal. Emit `(C, C)`.

At $(5, 5)$, value $-1$:
- Diagonal: $F_{4,4} + S(A, G) = 0 + (-1) = -1$. $\Rightarrow$ Diagonal. Emit `(A, G)`.

At $(4, 4)$, value $0$:
- Diagonal: $F_{3,3} + S(T, T) = 1 + 1 = 2$. 
- Up: $F_{3,4} + (-2) = -1$. 
- Left: $F_{4,3} + (-2) = -2$. 
- $\Rightarrow$ Diagonal. Emit `(T, T)`.

At $(3, 3)$, value $1$:
- Diagonal: $F_{2,2} + S(T, A) = 0 + (-1) = -1$. 
- Up: $F_{2,3} + (-2) = -2$. 
- Left: $F_{3,2} + (-2) = -1$. 
- $\Rightarrow$ Diagonal or Left. Tied at $-1$. By tie-break convention (e.g., prefer Up/Insert), choose Up: emit `(T, -)`.

At $(2, 3)$, value $0$:
- Diagonal: $F_{1,2} + S(A, A) = -1 + 1 = 0$. $\Rightarrow$ Diagonal. Emit `(A, A)`.

At $(1, 2)$, value $-1$:
- Diagonal: $F_{0,1} + S(G, C) = -2 + (-1) = -3$. 
- Up: $F_{0,2} + (-2) = -6$. 
- Left: $F_{1,1} + (-2) = -1$. 
- $\Rightarrow$ Left. Emit `(G, -)`.

At $(1, 1)$, value $1$:
- Diagonal: $F_{0,0} + S(G, G) = 0 + 1 = 1$. $\Rightarrow$ Diagonal. Emit `(G, G)`.

### 3.1.5 Final Alignment Output

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Aligned A:} & \quad \text{G--ATTAC--A} \\
\text{Aligned B:} & \quad \text{GCAT-TGCU-}
\end{aligned}
$$

Final score $= F_{7,7} = -1$. The four matches are G–G, T–T, A–A, C–C. Total gaps = 4. Identity = $4/9 \approx 44.4\%$.

## 3.2 Smith-Waterman Local Alignment — Derivation Outline

The Smith-Waterman algorithm modifies Needleman-Wunsch by adding a **zero floor** to the recurrence, allowing the alignment to restart anywhere a score becomes negative:

$$
F_{i,j} = \max \begin{cases} 0 \\ F_{i-1,j-1} + S(A_i, B_j) \\ F_{i-1,j} + G \\ F_{i,j-1} + G \end{cases}
$$

**Traceback** starts at the cell with the **maximum score** in the matrix, not necessarily at $(m, n)$, and stops when a cell with score $0$ is encountered. The result is the highest-scoring local segment — the most conserved subsequence, ideal for finding conserved protein domains or local gene homology.

## 3.3 E-value Derivation (Karlin-Altschul Statistics)

For a database of size $n$ residues and a query of size $m$, the expected number of HSPs (High-scoring Segment Pairs) with score $\geq S$ is:

$$
E = K \cdot m \cdot n \cdot e^{-\lambda S}
$$

The parameters $K$ and $\lambda$ depend only on the substitution matrix and gap costs. **Derivation sketch:**
1. Random sequences behave like an *uncorrelated* i.i.d. process.
2. The score $S$ of an HSP grows as a sum of independent contributions — by the **Gumbel extreme-value distribution**, the tail probability of $\geq S$ is $P(S \geq x) = 1 - e^{-K' m' n' e^{-\lambda x}}$.
3. For Poisson-distributed HSP occurrences, the expected count is exactly the exponent argument, yielding the $E$-value formula above.

The corresponding **bit score** is the length-normalized log-odds:

$$
S' = \frac{\lambda S - \ln K}{\ln 2}
$$

This makes scores comparable across different databases and scoring matrices.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Architecture: The BLAST Pipeline

The following block diagram captures the four-stage heuristic pipeline of the **BLAST** algorithm (Basic Local Alignment Search Tool). Each stage prunes the search space before the more expensive DP extension is invoked.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Query Sequence] --> B[Stage 1: Filter Low Complexity Regions SEG or DUST]
    B --> C[Stage 2: Generate k letter Words from Query]
    C --> D[Stage 3: Scan Database for Exact Word Hits above Threshold T]
    D --> E[Stage 4: Extend Hits Ungapped HSP to High Scoring Pairs]
    E --> F[Stage 5: Extend HSPs with Gapped DP Smith Waterman Style]
    F --> G[Stage 6: Statistical Significance E value and Bit Score]
    G --> H[Output Ranked List of Hits]
    H --> I[Visualize Alignment with Database Entry]
```

## 4.2 DP-Matrix Cell State Machine

Each cell $(i,j)$ in the affine-gap DP matrix has three possible states, governed by the recurrence relations in §2.2.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    stateM[State M: Match or Mismatch at i,j]
    stateX[State X: Gap in B at row i]
    stateY[State Y: Gap in A at column j]

    stateM -->|diagonal move plus S| stateM
    stateX -->|vertical move plus g extend| stateX
    stateY -->|horizontal move plus g extend| stateY

    stateM -->|start new gap with g open| stateX
    stateM -->|start new gap with g open| stateY
    stateX -->|switch back to match| stateM
    stateY -->|switch back to match| stateM
```

## 4.3 Global vs Local Alignment: Alignment Space Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    seqA[Sequence A length m]
    seqB[Sequence B length n]

    seqA --> nw[Needleman Wunsch]
    seqB --> nw
    nw --> nwResult[Full length alignment from 1,1 to m,n]

    seqA --> sw[Smith Waterman]
    seqB --> sw
    sw --> swResult[Best local segment from argmax to nearest zero cell]

    nwResult --> pick[Choice depends on biological question]
    swResult --> pick
    pick --> p1[Homology of full length proteins]
    pick --> p2[Conserved domain or motif detection]
    pick --> p3[Exon finding within long genomic sequence]
```

## 4.4 Multiple Sequence Alignment — Progressive Architecture (Clustal Model)

```master
+---------------------------------------------------------------+
|                  STAGE 1: PAIRWISE DISTANCES                   |
|   Compute distance d(i,j) for every pair of input sequences    |
|   (via global DP alignment under all substitution matrices)   |
+---------------------------------------------------------------+
                              |
                              v
+---------------------------------------------------------------+
|                  STAGE 2: GUIDE TREE BUILDING                  |
|   Construct a rooted tree (UPGMA or Neighbor-Joining)         |
|   from the distance matrix                                    |
+---------------------------------------------------------------+
                              |
                              v
+---------------------------------------------------------------+
|                  STAGE 3: PROGRESSIVE ALIGNMENT                |
|   Align the two closest leaves; treat result as a profile;     |
|   align next sequence/profile to the growing profile          |
|   (using HMM or weighted SP scoring)                          |
+---------------------------------------------------------------+
                              |
                              v
+---------------------------------------------------------------+
|                  STAGE 4: ITERATIVE REFINEMENT                |
|   (Optional) Re-align sub-regions to escape local optima      |
+---------------------------------------------------------------+
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define sequence alignment and differentiate between global and local alignment. Mention one bioinformatics tool used for each.
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Sequence alignment is the arrangement of two or more DNA, RNA, or protein sequences to identify regions of similarity that may indicate conserved functional, structural, or evolutionary relationships.

- **Global Alignment:** Aligns the *entire* length of both sequences end-to-end. Used when sequences are of similar length and assumed to be homologous over their full length. **Tool:** Needleman–Wunsch algorithm / EMBOSS Needle.
- **Local Alignment:** Aligns *only the most conserved sub-regions* of the sequences, ignoring flanking non-homologous regions. **Tool:** Smith–Waterman algorithm / BLAST.

### Q2. What is the role of a substitution matrix in sequence alignment? Name the two most commonly used protein substitution matrices.
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A substitution matrix $S(a, b)$ assigns a numerical score to every possible pair of aligned residues, encoding the biological likelihood that residue $a$ mutates to residue $b$ over evolutionary time. It guides the DP algorithm to favour biologically plausible substitutions (e.g., leucine ↔ isoleucine) and penalize unlikely ones (e.g., tryptophan ↔ glycine).

The two most commonly used protein substitution matrices are:
1. **PAM (Point Accepted Mutation) family** — based on evolutionary divergence.
2. **BLOSUM (BLOcks SUbstitution Matrix) family** — based on observed conserved blocks; **BLOSUM62** is the BLAST default.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Choose ONE

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - When drawing DP matrices in the exam, **always number rows and columns** starting from 0 (gap row/column) and **clearly label sequence letters** along the borders — losing these labels typically costs 1–2 marks.
> - In traceback, **state the tie-breaking rule** explicitly (e.g., "prefer diagonal in case of a tie"). Examiners deduct 0.5–1 mark for ambiguous tracebacks.
> - For BLAST/FASTA questions, students often forget to mention the **two-stage extension** (ungapped HSP then gapped DP) and the **Karlin–Altschul statistical framework** — this is a 4-mark item in most answer keys.
> - Always quote the **time complexity** of the algorithm discussed: $O(mn)$ for DP, $O(m + n)$ for FASTA seed-extension, $O(mW)$ for BLAST seeding. Examiners reward this.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

#### (a) [7 Marks] Explain the Needleman–Wunsch algorithm for global sequence alignment with a suitable example. Include the recurrence relation and the time complexity.

**Model Answer:**

The Needleman–Wunsch (1970) algorithm finds the **globally optimal alignment** of two sequences using dynamic programming. It guarantees the maximum possible similarity score over the entire length of both sequences.

**Step 1 — Initialization:**
A matrix $F$ of size $(m+1) \times (n+1)$ is created. The 0th row and column represent gaps:
$$F_{0,0} = 0, \quad F_{i,0} = i \cdot G, \quad F_{0,j} = j \cdot G$$
where $G$ is the gap penalty.

**Step 2 — Recurrence (fill the matrix row by row):**
$$F_{i,j} = \max \begin{cases} F_{i-1,j-1} + S(A_i, B_j) & \text{(match/mismatch)} \\ F_{i-1,j} + G & \text{(gap in B)} \\ F_{i,j-1} + G & \text{(gap in A)} \end{cases}$$

**Step 3 — Traceback:**
Starting at $F_{m,n}$, reconstruct the alignment by moving to the cell that produced the maximum: diagonal ⇒ align $A_i$ with $B_j$; up ⇒ insert gap in B; left ⇒ insert gap in A. Terminate at $F_{0,0}$.

**Worked Example (2 marks):**
Align `GATTACA` and `GCATGCU` with match = +1, mismatch = −1, gap = −2 (as fully worked in §3.1 of these notes). Final score $F_{7,7} = -1$, alignment:
```
G--ATTAC--A
GCAT-TGCU-
```

**Time and Space Complexity:** $O(mn)$ time, $O(mn)$ space (can be reduced to $O(\min(m,n))$ space using Hirschberg's divide-and-conquer trick).

#### (b) [7 Marks] Describe the BLAST algorithm. How is it different from the Smith–Waterman algorithm? Mention its applications.

**Model Answer:**

**BLAST (Basic Local Alignment Search Tool)** is a *heuristic* algorithm by Altschul et al. (1990) that finds high-scoring local alignments by sacrificing optimality for a **~50× speed-up** over Smith–Waterman.

**Three-stage pipeline (7 marks total: 3 for stages + 2 for comparison + 2 for applications):**

1. **Word Seeding (Compile a list of high-scoring words):** The query is broken into overlapping words of length $W$ (3 for proteins, 11 for DNA). For each word, all words scoring above threshold $T$ under the substitution matrix are generated as "neighbours".

2. **Scanning (Scan database for exact matches of these words):** The database is scanned for exact occurrences of any high-scoring word. Each hit becomes a *seed* for extension.

3. **Extension (Extend seeds in both directions):** Each seed is extended ungapped until the running score drops below a cutoff — these are **High-Scoring Segment Pairs (HSPs)**. Top HSPs are then extended with a gapped Smith–Waterman-style DP. Statistical significance is reported as the **E-value** (Karlin–Altschul).

**BLAST vs Smith–Waterman:**

| Aspect | Smith–Waterman | BLAST |
|---|---|---|
| Optimality | Guaranteed optimal | Heuristic, near-optimal |
| Speed | Slow ($O(mn)$) | Fast ($\sim O(mW)$ seeds) |
| Sensitivity | High (finds *all* local hits) | Slightly lower |
| Statistical framework | Optional | Built-in (E-value, bit score) |

**Applications:** (i) gene annotation by homology search, (ii) identifying protein families and conserved domains, (iii) primer design for PCR, (iv) phylogenetic analysis, (v) functional annotation of newly sequenced genomes.

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

#### (a) [7 Marks] Explain the Smith–Waterman algorithm for local sequence alignment. How does it differ from Needleman–Wunsch algorithm? State the time complexity.

**Model Answer:**

**Smith–Waterman (1981)** identifies the *highest-scoring local sub-alignment* between two sequences — a contiguous region of strong similarity, ignoring non-conserved flanking regions.

**Key modification over Needleman–Wunsch:** A **zero floor** is added to the recurrence, allowing the alignment to "restart" at any point where continuing would lower the score:

$$F_{i,j} = \max \begin{cases} 0 \\ F_{i-1,j-1} + S(A_i, B_j) \\ F_{i-1,j} + G \\ F_{i,j-1} + G \end{cases}$$

**Differences from Needleman–Wunsch (3 marks):**

| Feature | Needleman–Wunsch | Smith–Waterman |
|---|---|---|
| Goal | Global (end-to-end) | Local (best sub-region) |
| Recurrence | No zero floor | Zero floor included |
| Traceback start | $F_{m,n}$ | Cell with $\max_{i,j} F_{i,j}$ |
| Traceback stop | $F_{0,0}$ | Cell with value $0$ |
| Use case | Similar-length homologs | Conserved domain/motif search |

**Time and Space Complexity:** $O(mn)$ time and space, identical to Needleman–Wunsch in worst case — but with the traceback length typically much shorter (only the local segment is reconstructed).

**Example:** Aligning `HEAGAWGHEE` and `PAWHEAE` under BLOSUM62, gap-open = $-10$, gap-extend = $-1$ gives a local hit `AWGHE` with score $+28$.

#### (b) [7 Marks] Explain the concept of Multiple Sequence Alignment (MSA). Describe the progressive alignment approach used by ClustalW.

**Model Answer:**

**Multiple Sequence Alignment (MSA)** is the simultaneous alignment of three or more sequences, where each column represents a position that is homologous across all aligned sequences. It is a foundational tool for:
- Inferring evolutionary relationships (phylogenetic trees)
- Identifying conserved functional residues across a protein family
- Detecting conserved regulatory elements in DNA

**Progressive Alignment (ClustalW, 1994) — three stages:**

1. **Pairwise Distance Computation:**
For every pair $(i,j)$ of the $N$ input sequences, perform a Needleman–Wunsch global alignment under a chosen substitution matrix (BLOSUM or PAM). The alignment score is converted to an evolutionary distance $d_{ij}$. This yields an $N \times N$ distance matrix.

2. **Guide Tree Construction:**
The distance matrix is fed into a **Neighbor-Joining (NJ)** or **UPGMA** algorithm to build a rooted binary guide tree. Closely related sequences cluster together as sister leaves.

3. **Progressive Alignment Along the Tree:**
Following the guide tree from leaves to root:
   - Align the two most similar sequences first.
   - Treat the resulting alignment as a *profile* (a position-specific scoring matrix).
   - Align the next sequence or profile to the growing profile using a modified DP that uses position-specific gap penalties (e.g., gap penalties are increased in already-gapped regions and near hydrophobic residues to discourage biologically implausible gap placements).

**Limitations:** Progressive alignment can propagate errors made in early pairwise alignments. Iterative refinement (e.g., **MUSCLE**, **MAFFT**) re-aligns subsets to escape such local optima.

**Time Complexity:** $O(N^2 L^2)$ for the pairwise stage, where $L$ is the average sequence length, dominated by the $N(N-1)/2$ pairwise alignments.

**Applications:** Building phylogenetic trees, predicting protein secondary/tertiary structure, designing PCR primers in conserved regions, detecting disease-associated mutations by their position in a multiple alignment of patient sequences.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist**

- **Global alignment** = Needleman–Wunsch; **Local alignment** = Smith–Waterman; both $O(mn)$.
- The **only algorithmic difference** between them is the **zero floor** in the SW recurrence.
- The **traceback** in NW starts at $F_{m,n}$ and ends at $F_{0,0}$; in SW it starts at the cell with $\max F_{i,j}$ and ends at the nearest zero cell.
- **Affine gap penalty** = $g_{open} + (k-1) g_{extend}$; requires a **three-state DP** ($M$, $X$, $Y$).
- **BLAST** is **heuristic** — uses **word seeding + extension** — much faster than SW but not guaranteed optimal.
- **BLAST pipeline order**: filter low-complexity → word list generation → database scan → ungapped HSP extension → gapped DP extension → E-value reporting.
- **E-value formula**: $E = K \cdot m \cdot n \cdot e^{-\lambda S}$. **Lower E = more significant.** A hit with $E < 0.01$ is usually considered significant.
- **Bit score** = length-normalized score: $S' = (\lambda S - \ln K)/\ln 2$. Comparable across different databases/matrices.
- **BLOSUM62** is the standard default for protein BLAST; **match/mismatch +5/−4** (or +1/−1) is the standard for nucleotide BLAST (blastn).
- **MSA** = alignment of $N \geq 3$ sequences. **ClustalW** uses the **progressive strategy** (pairwise distances → guide tree → profile alignment).
- **Sum-of-Pairs (SP) score** is the standard objective function for MSA: $S_{SP} = \sum_{i < j} s(A_i, A_j)$.
- **Hirschberg's algorithm** reduces DP space from $O(mn)$ to $O(\min(m,n))$ using divide-and-conquer — important in the ESE for space-complexity questions.
- For **KTU 14-mark questions**, always present: (i) recurrence with definitions of all symbols, (ii) at least one numerical example, (iii) time/space complexity, and (iv) one real-world application.
- **Match score** and **gap penalty values** must always be **stated in the script** before the matrix is filled — failure to do so is a common mark-loss trap.
